review_text:
Resident Evil 7 is so good it's actually stupid.

The Baker family is easily one of the best parts of the entire series. Every time one of them showed up I knew something bad was about to happen. The house is creepy, the atmosphere is great, and the game somehow keeps making simple things feel stressful.

Also, Ethan deserves way more love than he gets. The amount of stuff this guy goes through in this game is insane. By the end I was more worried about him than I was about the monsters.

The game looks great, the horror is great, the pacing is great. There's really not much else to say.

One of the best Resident Evil games tbh (I love you so much Ethan Winters)

A little late to the party, but Resident Evil 7 was absolutely worth it. I came for the horror, but ended up getting attached to the story and characters more than I expected. The tension never really lets up, and some moments genuinely made me feel sad. Such a memorable experience.

This is one of the best horror games I've ever experienced. RE7 is a perfect Resident Evil game. It has all the RE elements, unlike RE3 which focuses mostly on the action-adventure aspect. RE7 literally revived the franchise and brought back all the things we love about Resident Evil.

The level design is great, probably one of the best in the series, and the puzzles were fantastic. It always keeps you on the edge, constantly making you fear what might be waiting for you up ahead. The inventory management and survival aspects are also excellent, forcing you to carefully manage your resources and think about every item you carry. Every bullet, healing item, and inventory slot matters, which adds even more tension to the experience.

I absolutely loved the story as well because it felt fresh unlike the other Resident Evil games we've played before. The main character is one of the highlights of this game. He's not your typical edgy Resident Evil protagonist, but a normal person who just wants to find his wife, which makes the experience feel even more grounded and immersive.

This game also has one of the best atmospheres in the series. From beginning to end, it never lets go of that feeling of tension and dread. For me, it's absolutely a 10/10.

Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is a strong return to survival horror for the series and one of the most unsettling entries in the franchise.

The gameplay shifts to a first-person perspective, which makes everything feel much more immersive and tense. Exploring tight spaces, managing resources, and trying to survive against unpredictable enemies creates constant pressure.

The Baker family is a major highlight. They are creepy, memorable, and genuinely terrifying, especially early in the game when you don’t fully understand what’s going on.

The atmosphere is easily one of the best parts. The isolated setting, sound design, and overall tone make the game feel uncomfortable in a good way, keeping you on edge throughout most of the experience.

Overall, Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is a masterpiece survival horror game that successfully brings the series back to its scary roots while still feeling fresh.

Most people think horror is about monsters.

They're wrong.

Horror is about realizing nobody is coming to save you.

And Resident Evil 7 understands that better than almost any game I've played.

The moment you step onto that property, something feels off. Not in a dramatic way. In a quiet way. Like the house already knows your name. Like it's been waiting for you.

I loved that feeling.

The Baker estate isn't just a location.

It's a trap pretending to be a home.

Every hallway feels too narrow. Every room feels occupied. Every door feels like it has bad intentions. The whole place radiates the kind of energy that makes normal people turn around immediately.

Ethan doesn't.

Which is hilarious.

Because every second he spends in that house is another opportunity for reality to remind him how terrible his decision-making is.

Then Jack Baker shows up.

Now we're talking.

The man doesn't feel human anymore. He doesn't feel like a villain. He feels like a force of nature that somehow learned how to joke. Every time he appears, the atmosphere changes. The walls feel smaller. The exits feel farther away.

And somehow he's having a wonderful time.

I respect that.

What makes RE7 special is how personal it feels. No armies. No cities collapsing. No world-ending catastrophe.

Just you.

One house.

And a family that absolutely refuses to let you leave.

The game slowly strips away every illusion of control. Bullets run low. Supplies disappear. Safe rooms become sacred ground. You start treating locked doors like miracles.

And the house notices.

The deeper you go, the more it feels alive.

Like it's studying you.

Testing you.

Trying to figure out exactly how much pressure it takes before you break.

The answer, thankfully, is quite a lot.

By the end, I wasn't trying to escape anymore.

I was exploring.

Curious.

Excited to see what fresh insanity was hiding behind the next door.

Rotten. Claustrophobic. Magnificent.

A masterpiece that understands the scariest thing in the world isn't being hunted by a monster.

It's realizing the monster is completely comfortable in its own home... and you're the guest.

This game is so good, the vibes are insanely good, the gameplay is really fun, the story was fantastic. This feels like what Resident Evil 1 is trying to be, but without the jank of a 20+ year old game, it's fantastic.

A strong atmosphere, a solid survival horror experience and the Baker family make this game iconic. Definitely hits different compared to other RE games, but it's a solid entry still.

A couple nitpicks:
- Item collection isn't as satisfying as in the other modern RE games (as in the map doesn't go blue).
- Enemies sometimes pull of an insta-bite, dealing an insane amount of damage that feels unfair.
- The Magnum is not worth the effort of obtaining it (it costs a lot and a shotgun is just as good).
- Almost every cutscene being unskippable doesn't do any favors for the game's replay-ability.

DLC overview:
- "Not a Hero" adds an extra Chris Redfield campaign. You can parry attacks in this one, which is alright. Also comes with a "Professional" difficulty that makes sure you learn to parry. Not part of the Season pass, but free.
- "End of Zoe" adds an extra first-person beat-em-up campaign that's just pure fun.
- "Banned Footage Vol.1" adds a wave-defense mode (meh), an escape room (not much to see) and "Ethan Must Die" (tough).
- "Banned Footage Vol.2" adds black jack (twisted, but alright), more story (pretty good) and an arcade style collectathon (silly, but ok).
- The coin DLCs aren't worth it (and not necessary for achievements).
[hr][/hr]
100% achievement completion boils down finding all collectibles, completing challenge runs and beating all high-difficulty modes the game and its DLCs have to offer. This will take multiple playthroughs. There's plenty of detailed guides and walkthroughs out there, which help with everything. Overall, the difficulty can be frustrating (especially in the Ethan Must Die DLC), but it's manageable.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 10/10 – The Game That Saved Resident Evil

I went into Resident Evil 7 expecting a good horror game. What I got was one of the best survival horror experiences ever made.

From the moment you arrive at the Baker estate, the atmosphere is absolutely terrifying. The game constantly keeps you on edge with its incredible sound design, dark environments, and the feeling that something is always watching you. Unlike many modern horror games, RE7 doesn't rely on cheap jump scares—it builds tension masterfully.

The Baker family are some of the most memorable villains in the entire Resident Evil franchise. Every encounter feels intense and unpredictable, especially during the early hours of the game when resources are scarce and survival feels uncertain.

The switch to first-person was a bold decision, but it paid off perfectly. It makes every hallway, every creaking door, and every enemy encounter feel personal and terrifying. At the same time, it still feels unmistakably like Resident Evil thanks to its exploration, puzzle-solving, inventory management, and resource conservation.

The graphics still look fantastic, the DLC is excellent, and the story successfully revitalized the series while setting up future games.

Pros:
✔ Incredible atmosphere
✔ Genuinely scary from start to finish
✔ Excellent level design
✔ Memorable villains
✔ Great resource management and exploration
✔ One of the best first-person horror experiences ever made

Cons:
✘ Made me check every corner of my house for Jack Baker

Final Verdict:
Resident Evil 7 brought the franchise back to its survival horror roots and proved that Resident Evil could reinvent itself without losing its identity. If you're a horror fan, this is essential gaming.

RE7 takes the franchise into a new level of horror. The previous games that lean into survival horror, such as RE1 and RE2, are definitely great in their own ways - but it's not the type of horror that will actually scare you. RE7 was different, it is MUCH scarier than the other survival horror games in the franchise, even Outlast for me personally. I liked how they didn't overdo the jump scares, and instead focused on the atmosphere that made you nervous when walking down the hallways or solving puzzles. There was even this moment in the game where I was focused on solving the puzzles and when I turned, I just saw this kid's feet and I just screamed asf - no SFX or suspense music, just THAT.

In terms of the story, it also has an interesting one. Basically, you, as Ethan, looks for your missing wife in a residence and encounter a new BOW that can do terrifying things to others (not spoiling anything). I found this new form of BOW to be creative, as it is entirely different from what we are used to in the franchise - zombies. If you take the time to read the notes, you'll discover why the BOW behaves in that certain way and it actually makes sense how can this BOW be useful in warfare. The DLCs, Not A Hero and End of Zoe, also add important stuff to the closure of the story, and are there for some sick action (Joe Baker's cool asf). 

Overall, RE7 is such a great game. It just overtook RE4R as my favorite game in the franchise so far. Also, just wanted to mention, the theme song slaps man.

Resident Evil 7 = 8.5/10

Man, I have a special affection for this game, but in this review I’ll try to stay neutral. I spent basically the entire Xbox 360 and PS3 generation without gaming. I was a teenager, wanted to go out more, and ended up spending years away from video games. But when I saw the trailer for Resident Evil 7, I completely lost my mind. Back then, Mia looked insanely realistic — her hair, facial features — and the similarities to Outlast immediately caught my attention.

I genuinely became obsessed with the game and wanted to play RE7 no matter what, so I ended up buying a PS4. (At the time I already had a PC, but I still preferred playing on consoles.)

The experience was incredible. I absolutely loved this game. I thought the gameplay was amazing, and honestly, I never found it as terrifying as many people claimed. To me, this game feels a lot like those 1980s horror movies filled with cheesy gore and cheap practical effects.

Man, fighting Jack is almost hilarious at times, especially during the garage fight and when he grabs that giant pair of pruning shears.

Negative points:

- This game honestly could have had a different title and worked as a parallel story within the Resident Evil universe. Even though I really love this game, I still don’t truly feel like I’m playing Resident Evil. And honestly, making it a numbered Resident Evil game makes it even weirder hahaha.

- The ship section with Mia is extremely boring and should have been skippable. In RE9 I hate playing as Chloe, and in RE2 Remake I hate playing as Sherry, but at least those sections are short. With Mia on the ship, it feels like it never ends. It’s extremely irritating.

- There’s no option to skip cutscenes, and that makes replaying the game much more annoying.

Best RE game out of all of them. RE3 was my favorite out of all of them but then I finally got the balls to play this one and it was the best choice ever. Fire gameplay, fire DLCs. Its my fav game to replay.

It's a great time if it's your first time. A second playthrough is a bit boring, but stuff like Banned Footage's Nightmare mode and the Ethan Must Die mode are a fun gameplay loop. But Ethan? Hands down not only my favorite horror protagonist, but probably favorite protagonist in GENERAL. I'll get a better computer for Village.

(Just make sure you use the shotgun or grenade launcher if you see the floor crawlers. Or as I like to call them, "Floorf#&%ers".)

Pros:
1. The first game with the RE Engine is a stunner even if it showing its age.
2. For the most part a solid story. In the end I like how the Bakers were more tragic than they seemed.
3. Back to basics after RE6...

Cons:
1. ...but almost too back to basics where even RE1 didn't feel this hampered down.
2. Ethan and Mia are the most boring, nothing people protagonists of this franchise.  
3. You move so incredibly slow. Like it feels like the character is trudging through mud and it doesn't help that this was an entry in the series where enemies spawn behind you.
4. Very dark. I set the game's brightness based on how it instructed me, but I was constantly stumbling around to see anything. So glad they allowed you to toggle your own flashlight in RE: Requiem.

Ultimately this game is just a slog. Is it better than RE5 and RE6? Yes, I think so, but I think this is still a low point in the franchise that had some solid ideas that they built upon.

Resident Evil 7 did one extremely important thing and I find it hard to believe that Capcom is ignoring it - it proved that the original Resident Evil formula is completely timeless and works perfectly even within the modern iteration of the series. 

The first half of the game is great, full of tension, solid puzzles, good level design and freshness for the series.  The second half (starting from the most important decision to the end) is a significant drop in the quality of practically everything. 

Still, I think the game is worth playing and is one of the best modern RE games.

Almost 10 years later still aged incredibly, definitely one of the most scariest games ever made but still somehow soo satisfying to play, even though some parts of the game is frustrating and annoying at times the atmosphere and gameplay really carries the game, all guns have great weight to them and feel super satisyfing to use, the horror is also done in a way that's weirdly addicting to find out, you are constantly wondering how the game will scare you now but not in an anxious way more out of curiosity and high expectation, the bosses are cool too other than the jack chainsaw fight(that was super cluncky) but everything else felt nice and rewarding

A truly excellent game that brings back the fluidity of true horror. This is practically my first first-person shooter, and it's absolutely fantastic, maintaining constant tension and fear. The Baker family mansion's design and atmosphere are top-notch, and the gameplay and puzzles are intensely enjoyable and even better than the second installment. The game is well-rounded, perfectly balanced, and a phenomenal experience worth buying.

The Test of Time: The Rebirth of Survival Horror

I have a tendency to return to older games. Videogames pass through two filters in my mind: that of the initial release with the excitement that accompanies it, and that of the second playthrough, months or years later. In a way, they mature along with me. The case of Resident Evil 7 is highly peculiar. The game arrived when the series was at its absolute lowest point of popularity after the "circus" of the sixth installment, and it was probably met with a degree of suspicion back in its day. Having now played the subsequent Village and Requiem, I can say with confidence that RE7 is the best game in the series and certainly one of the most influential video games of all time, as it signaled the return and rebirth of the survival horror genre that we still enjoy today.

From the very first seconds, hearing that almost hypnotic intro song from a child's voice and reaching the courtyard of the mansion a few minutes later, you feel this deafening silence. Without anything even happening, simply getting out of the vehicle and wandering through nature, you feel a shiver run deep through your skin. Capcom's decision to shift the camera to a first-person perspective proved to be a stroke of genius, bringing you into direct, claustrophobic contact with the terror. The game made me shriek more than once, despite the fact that it was already familiar to me. Τhat is something that rarely happens to me. The sound design contributes decisively to this, acting as a silent narrator of the horror. Every creak of the house, every distant footstep, and the absolute isolation make you constantly look behind your back.

At the same time, the game introduces ingenious mechanisms such as the videotapes, an excellent way of reviving the past that functions flawlessly both narratively, allowing you to see the events that transpired, and on a gameplay level by providing clues that later help you solve puzzles. The best part is that it is entirely optional; you can simply skip it, but you must be ready for the roadblocks that will follow. The dynamic difficulty always keeps the game fair, even on the Madhouse level. You will eat four to five wipes in a row at certain points, but the system adapts so well to each player's skills that eventually, you will manage to make it. The proper save points help with this as well. The balanced checkpoints and manual saves work harmoniously, without forcing you to replay large portions of the game, simply reducing the abuse of constant saving through consumable items for saving progress and increasing the risk of making a mistake.

In the story department, here we have perhaps the most complete and self-contained narrative in the framework of the series. Slightly isolated from the scriptwriting chaos of its predecessors, the story follows its own path with excellent escalation. The final twist back in its day had caught me completely off guard, while the writing is so good that, even knowing the villainous actions, you cannot help but feel a brief moment of sympathy for the antagonists.

I could honestly write for hours about how much I love this game. As the years pass and players keep returning to it, the significance and legacy of Resident Evil 7 will be recognized more and more fully.

RESIDENT EVIL 7: Welcome to the Family.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

🎮 Gameplay: 
I recently got into the Resident Evil series with Resident Evil Requiem being my first game, and after doing some digging around, Resident Evil 7: Biohazard quickly stood out as an entry that seems to hold a special place in the hearts of many longtime fans. After finally playing it myself, I can definitely see why.

The gameplay follows the classic survival horror formula. You'll spend most of your time exploring creepy environments, hiding from threats, sneaking past enemies when possible, and running for your life when things inevitably go wrong. As you progress, you'll gradually build up an arsenal of weapons, while crafting becomes an important part of staying alive. Managing your resources, deciding what to craft, and making every bullet count all add to the tension. Truth be told, nobody really plays games like this because they expect groundbreaking gameplay mechanics. The atmosphere, storytelling, and sense of dread are what carry the experience, but that doesn't mean the gameplay shouldn't be polished. Fortunately, the gunplay feels satisfying throughout, but it also feels a tad bit easy and some of the sections could def have been a bit harder then they were.

If I had to point something out though, it would be the boss fights. They tend to be a little too simplistic and never quite reach the intensity I was hoping for. And the same can be said about the puzzles. They require no thought at all. Neither issue hurts the experience in a major way, but a bit more challenge and refined systems here could have elevated the game even further.

📘 Story: 
Resident Evil 7 doesn't waste any time pulling you in. The story starts with Ethan Winters receiving a mysterious message from his wife Mia, who has been missing for years. With no other leads, he travels to an abandoned plantation in rural Louisiana, only to find himself trapped in a nightmare.

One thing I really liked is how grounded the story feels. Instead of immediately throwing you into some world-ending disaster, it focuses on one location, one family, and the mystery surrounding them. That smaller scale makes everything feel more personal, and it helps move the story forward in a natural and satisfying way. The game feeds you just enough information to keep you curious without revealing too much too soon. I wouldn't say this is the deepest or most emotional story you'll ever experience, but it doesn't need to be. It knows exactly what it's trying to do, and it does it well. After finishing the game, I completely understood why so many Resident Evil fans still hold this one in such high regard.

📺 Visuals: 
By today's standards, the graphics aren't exactly going to blow anyone away, but let's be fair here, Resident Evil 7 is almost nine years old. Keeping that in mind, the game has aged surprisingly well. Capcom's RE Engine does a fantastic job of creating a photorealistic and deeply unsettling atmosphere, with the Baker estate feeling dirty, claustrophobic, and genuinely uncomfortable to explore.

The environments are easily the strongest part of the visuals. The lighting, environmental detail, and overall art direction work together to create a constant sense of tension that rarely lets up. There are definitely a few signs of the game's age. Some textures can look a little blurry, and a handful of character models don't quite hold up under close inspection. Even so, those issues are easy to overlook because the atmosphere is so well crafted. Resident Evil 7 shows that good art direction can keep a game looking great long after the graphics themselves have started to age.

🎧 Sound: 
The audio does a fantastic job of bringing the horror to life. The ambient audio really stood out to me. Hearing floorboards creak, footsteps in the distance, or random noises somewhere in the house constantly made me feel uneasy. There were more than a few times where I stopped for a second just to listen before deciding if it was safe to keep going.

The voice acting is also very good, especially from the Baker family. They do a fantastic job of making encounters feel unsettling or creepy. While the soundtrack isn't something I'll remember years from now, it does exactly what it needs to do and complements the atmosphere perfectly. Overall, the audio is a huge reason why Resident Evil 7 works so well as a horror game. It doesn't only rely on loud music or cheap tricks to scare you. Instead, it builds a constant feeling of unease from the moment you enter the Baker estate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

🏆 Overall: ( 8 / 10 )
Resident Evil 7 is simply a great horror game. Other than the visuals showing their age in a few places, it's surprisingly easy to forget that this game is almost nine years old. It still feels remarkably modern, and that's a testament to just how well the game was designed. For me, the setting is easily the standout. Exploring the Baker estate was an incredible experience, and I honestly enjoyed it even more than the setting in Resident Evil Requiem. It constantly keeps you on edge and does an amazing job of making you feel isolated and uncomfortable. 

The game isn't perfect. I would have liked to see more challenging boss fights and puzzles that required a bit more thought, but those shortcomings are easy to overlook. Resident Evil 7 gets far more right than it does wrong, and after finally playing it myself, I completely understand why so many fans rank it among the best games in the series.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

A true survival horror experience that takes resident evil back to it's roots. This game is mind numbingly terrifying and really tests your patience. You have to be very mindful of your ammunition and be able to backtrack a lot for locked chests or items that you need to leave and come back for later. Not really a surprise since you would do the same in previous entries. The new story with Ethan Winters and his wife Mia Winters is very captivating with how it all ties together as you play the story. And without giving too much away one of the more important characters that you'll learn about later Eveline is a very deep character. You really learn more about the lore and background of Umbrella and how the setting of the story takes place if you keep on playing. The Baker family is all interesting with Jack, Marguerite, and Lucas. You really truly understand the family as you uncover the truth behind the house and the family. Overall I'd give it a 9/10 for a survival horror game. I really loved that they went back to the roots to give back the old school survival horror with the new first person horror pov. It really added to the atmosphere and was the cherry on top to this masterpiece.

The game follows Ethan Winters searching for his missing wife, Mia. He travels to a remote plantation where he discorvers the disturbing Baker family and their decaying mansion. As Ethan explores the estate, he learns that the family possesses unnatural regenerative powers and is connected to a strange bioweapon known as Eveline.

What I liked about Resident Evil 7: An excellent horror atmosphere where the game draws inspiration from a horror film, The Texas Chainsaw Massacre. I appreciate how the story does tie into the existing lore in a way that I found rewarding to uncover as a longtime RE fan. There are plenty of exciting refrences to classic Resident Evil like Crest doors, Weight-based puzzle to obtain the shotgun, themed keys, safe rooms, boxes and more. 

Debatable: Capcom took a bold risk by introducing a first-person perspective in RE7. This may not appeal to fans who preferred the classic third-person gameplay in classic RE. 

What I hated about Resident Evil 7: Aside from the Basement, it is the annoying VHS Tape Segments and Mini-Games. While the main story is enjoyable and carefully plotted, the mini-games are overly difficult especially for achievement completionists. I really liked the story in the End of Zoe content, especially how Zoe gets a happy ending when she is saved by her uncle Joe Baker. It feels like a warm and satisfying conclusion to her character arc after everything she went through. However, while the story is enjoyable, the achievement side of it is not. As a completionist myself, the frustrating mini-games and difficult achievement requirements contributed to my overall 6/10 rating of the game.

This was my very first Resident Evil game ever, and I have to say: the atmosphere of the house is genuinely so creepy and terrifying, it’s actually stupid. The moment you step inside, the game somehow manages to make the simplest tasks feel incredibly stressful.

Once you uncover the story, it gets even crazier. If it wasn’t for that brat Eveline, the Bakers would still be a calm, good family. She literally ruined their lives, infected them, and drove them mad. But that bastard Lucas was on a whole different level—he was working with some secret organization, a total traitor who sold out his own family. You can almost feel bad for the others, but I absolutely hated that little prick Lucas. Getting to personally end him in the "Not a Hero" DLC was incredibly satisfying.

Also, can we talk about Ethan Winters? The amount of absolute madness this guy goes through is insane. By the end, I was honestly more worried about his physical and mental well-being than I was about the actual monsters. Total respect.

I jumped in, blasted through the main story and the DLC, and wrapped the whole thing up in about 11 hours. Great horror, amazing pacing, and a fantastic introduction to the franchise for a newcomer like me. 10/10, highly recommended!

Closing Note: After seeing what Ethan went through, I'm now at a point where if I get a splinter at home, I'll probably call an ambulance. The guy lost his left hand, his right leg, and literally collected his missing limbs like they were side quests. Then he just splashes some green first-aid juice on it like, "Yeah, good as new," and moves on. Honestly, I need a bottle of whatever that green juice is; whenever my motivation in life drops, I'll just spray it on myself to instantly heal. 10/10. (MİA <3)

What I like the most about Resident Evil 7, without a doubt, is the 21 mode, an extra DLC that I found really fun. It is a more niche mode, of course, but for me it was the part of the game that stood out the most.

As someone who's only ever played RE6 one time five years ago, I was blown away by the complete 180 this game took. RE7 is horrific in all the best ways. The music, sound design, characters, overall environment, and first-person perspective make an immersive and terrifying experience that I absolutely adore. Playing as Ethan really ties it all together because he isn't a cop or member of the BSAA. He's just some guy who happened to get caught up in everything. The story he oncovers is tragic and made me feel a strange cocktail of emotions. 

Speaking of story, there is a LOT of diversity in the story arcs and map locations, making the game feel unpredictable and extra anxiety inducing. Each section tends to follow the same gameplay loop, but it rarely ever feels repetitive or tiresome.
The bosses are especially distinct from each other in design, environment, and methods used to defeat them. It made fighting them super fun even though I suck at boss fights.

The videotapes are also really cool. I don't see them mentioned often, but they were honestly one of my favorite parts of the game. They're a creative spin on campaigns and an interesting storytelling device.

My only real complaint is that the puzzles felt a little too easy. The Bedroom mini game from the DLC had good puzzles though. Oh, that and the molded are a pain in the ass. Give them an extra headshot or two when they go down. Otherwise you'll start walking away just to have your ankles obliterated.

But anyway. Resident Evil: Biohazard is a great game, especially if you're looking to get your fix on horror and tragic stories. It's easily one of the best birthday gifts I've ever gotten.

I got for 8 EUR to play on Quest 3 PCVR. First time I've modded a game to run on PCVR. At first I used the DX12 game and mod, but it was very janky and crashed. I switched to the DX11 version of the game & mod. 

First runs were very poor visuals and play. Difficult to even get past the options screens. I also didn't know the controls (for back, for example). I was close to returning the game. I removed the SteamVR link file that came with the mod and played through VDXR only. That seemed to be a crucial move for me. I also dialled down Virtual Desktop, turned on VD reprojection, and did some other graphics tweaks in the game.

Removing upper body and disconnecting head attachment (I can't remember the exact phrase) in reframework were absolutely needed (tips found on Youtube).

Finally I got it running with reasonable graphics and latency. It's not smooth like Alyx, but it is an old game that was not designed natively for PCVR. Being forced to move in an action scene, like when you open a sliding screen or fall off a ladder, is a bit disorientating. But you can get past it if you don't suffer from car sickness. My last playthrough took me past the play hours for return, but I'm good with that. I'm now enjoying the game, I start to get the general feel of it (I've never played a Resident Evil game before) and I will now certainly play it right through.

It's not as scary as I was expecting. Not sure why. Like 2 hours in I've been attacked with a knife, hoe and chainsaw, had my hand and leg cut off, had my eyes poked while tied up, saw a policeman's head chopped in half in front of me, several jump scares. I think I'm just enjoying the gameplay experience and realise it is just a game after all.

Conclusion: you need to be a little bit tech savvy to get it running on PCVR, but if you manage, and have bought on discount, it's definitely a top value AAA full length VR game. There are not that many of those around, so using mods to squeeze out some more seems a decent move.

My specs: i9 10 series, RTX 2080 super, 32 GB RAM, Quest 3, Virtual desktop

At this point most people know that Resident Evil 7 was the game that reignited the series and took it back to its roots. I came into this game knowing that, and some of the story already. Before this I had already played the remake trilogy and loved them. 

RE7 is an interesting take on the RE formula. Its influenced by previous games in the series but also by other games outside the series. While RE was in its action era the horror genre had been taken over by first person horror games like Amnesia, Outlast, and PT (not a full game but its influence on the genre was huge). So RE7 has a lot of that DNA in it; from the first person camera, to the focus on many sections involving you being unarmed and hiding rather than fighting. This is how the game starts, and then it blends into a an experience not far off the original couple of RE games. You're wandering around a large map with lots of puzzles and backtracking. Like those games, the action is dialled up the deeper into the game you get, but not nearly to the degree of RE4-6. Looking at the full game, its interesting that it gives you quite a varied experience and I'd say it works really well. 

As a horror game you expect this to be scary, and it certainly is, but I'd say this diminishes as the game progresses. Its definitely at its scariest in the first couple of hours when you're unarmed and avoiding the threat rather than taking it on. In these sections you are pursued by an unkillable enemy at times, a bit like Mr X in RE2R, but unlike in that game these tend to be just small sequences. The parts of the game where you are exploring the map lack that ever-present stalking threat that makes RE2R so special. All that said, the game is still dripping with spooky atmosphere and you'll always be relatively on edge. 

The story is interesting, and in true RE fashion it gets more elaborate and deep as it goes on. The writing is good but inconsistent when it comes to the characters. Ethan as a protagonist is weak, and often seems to underreact to what should be dramatic moments. Mia is creepy and melodramatic, sometimes in a good way, and sometimes not. However, the Baker family is where this game's character work really shines. They are all expertly designed and acted, and incredibly memorable.

One thing I would definitely highlight with this game is that the bundle including all the DLC is incredible value. There are various expansions ranging from an escape room experience, a couple of horde modes, 2 story expansions, and more. They really experimented with these and there's lots of replay value there. 

Overall, RE7 is definitely a fantastic game. It revitalised the series, and even just looking at it on its own its a great RE experience.

After a few entries that felt more like Hollywood action movies than actual horror, Resident Evil 7 finally brings the franchise back to its roots—pure, unrelenting terror. Switching to a first-person perspective was easily the best executive decision Capcom could have made for this series.

You play as Ethan Winters, a regular guy searching for his missing wife, which leads him to a derelict, isolated plantation in Louisiana. That's when the nightmare with the Baker family begins.

PROS:

Incredible Atmosphere: The Baker house is so detailed, claustrophobic, and grime-ridden that you can almost smell the rot through the screen. The sound design is a masterpiece—every floorboard creak will have you turning around in a panic.

The Baker Family: Jack and Marguerite are phenomenal antagonists. The sheer feeling of helplessness while being actively stalked through narrow hallways is unmatched.

Classic RE Gameplay is Back: Inventory management, scarce ammo, puzzle-solving, and saving your game in safe rooms with that iconic, calming music. It’s exactly what old-school fans wanted.

Great Optimization: The RE Engine runs flawlessly. The game looks absolutely stunning and delivers a rock-solid framerate even on older hardware setups.

CONS:

Lack of Enemy Variety: The "Molded" creatures are creepy at first but get a bit repetitive to fight after a while.

The Second Half: The final few hours lean a bit heavier into standard action and shooting, which slightly dilutes the pure horror vibe established in the beginning.

VERDICT:
This isn't just a great Resident Evil game; it’s one of the best survival horror games of the decade. If you want a game that will genuinely get your heart racing, don't hesitate.

Rating: 9/10 (Highly recommended to play in the dark with headphones on!)

Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is a survival horror about Ethan Winters, who is searching for his wife in a plantation home surrounded by an infected family. Resident Evil 6 completely broke me that I legit thought if I should even continue on the franchise after finishing the game. Then I realized that this exists, and I actually have played this game before and really enjoyed it when it first came out. So I really needed this after the previous game, and this is such a breath of fresh air from not only 6, but also the main series as a whole.

This is actually the first Resident Evil game that I find generally terrifying. Not only does adding the first-person perspective add to the immersive horror experience, but also making it grounded makes it feel like you’re actually there. Especially since Ethan Winters is the most relatable main character in the franchise. Your similar thoughts are close to what Ethan was saying out loud when playing for the first time. Granted, Ethan needs to have a bit more development to him, but with him exploring by himself and barely talking to anyone, I’m not gonna act like that doesn’t make sense. The Baker family is such a brute force of nature that when I was trying to hide from them, I was on edge, trying to get away as far as I could. They’re so scary that I honestly forgot how to defeat them because I was getting too scared sometimes, which is rare that I can say from any of the games in the series. The graphics are outstanding that still look great to this day, and the bosses are a lot of fun to deal with. I can see the final boss being disappointing to people, and I do think it’s the weakest boss battle out of all of them, but I still have fun with the build-up that leads to a satisfying payoff. 

The story in itself is surprisingly straightforward, which, to me, is better than being secondary, and it builds a lot of story elements towards the lore that I was actually interested in where it was going, which I’ve never said that for a Resident Evil game before. This game was trying new methods to really keep you in suspense, especially when bringing tapes into the game that allow you to control the characters in them as well, and it remains effective while keeping it contained in the reality of the situation. The puzzles were fun, the soundtrack is creepy, and the Baker house is so memorable that it is generally my favorite main location in all of the main Resident Evil games. It’s intricate, it has so much detail that brings character to the family that is both disgusting, harrowing, and tragic. I love Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is my favorite game of the franchise that gives me the horror elements and the story elements that I’ve been wanting from a Resident Evil game ever since I started the series, while keeping the gameplay as engaging and entertaining that kept the spirit of the franchise as well.

I've had an absolute blast playing Resident Evil 7 Biohazard. The graphics, optimization, story, and overall atmosphere are all top-notch. The game managed to scare me more times than I'd like to admit, and it constantly kept me on edge from beginning to end.

The Baker family is easily one of the most memorable parts of the game. Every encounter feels tense, and the shift back to a more horror-focused Resident Evil experience was exactly what the series needed. Exploring the house, uncovering the story, and trying to survive with limited resources made every moment feel rewarding.

The soundtrack and sound design are phenomenal. Whether it's the eerie silence before something goes wrong or the music during major moments, everything works together to create an unforgettable horror experience.

I bought the Gold Edition, so I still have the DLCs waiting for me. I've only had a small look at them so far, but they seem just as fun and well-made as the main game. I'll definitely be returning to complete all of them sooner or later.

Overall, Resident Evil 7 is amazing in almost every aspect. Great story, terrifying atmosphere, memorable characters, solid optimization, and plenty of content to enjoy. If you're a fan of horror games, this is absolutely worth your time.

Resident Evil 7 was my first ever RE experience, and to this day, remains my favorite installment in the series.

Gunplay:
The gunplay is good. While not as unique as other games, the character building allows that aspect to thrive. The character speed can be a problem occasionally, because why am i walking at a leisurely pace in front of horrors beyond humanity? But otherwise, the gunplay is good, with the exception of the flamethrower, as to my knowledge, it serves no real purpose after the area it is acquired in.


Graphics:
The graphics are a little dated for a game from 2017, but honestly, the graphics add to the horror, amplifying a disgusting, vile place beyond the screen, emulating a moldy look.

Story:
The story is what really brings this game beyond all the rest, the antagonists are understandable and empathetic, as opposed to evil mastermind because humanity must evolve! The characters are all likeable and provide value beyond the surface lever, the protagonist is extremely likeable, standing in as a sort of self insert.

Bosses:
The boss fights are another huge highlight, each boss is extremely different and unique in their fights, from a chainsaw battle with Jack, to a professional hide and seek battle with Marguerite, each boss provides so much value to the game.

Enemies:
This is the one huge complaint that the game has, enemy variety. You can count the amount of enemies on one hand, with all of them not being too different from each other or requiring a separate strat then "shoot till dead." The enemies remain terrifying through out the game thankfully.

Overall RE7 was an incredible experience which I'm grateful to have experienced.

★★★★★ — “A return to true horror… and a reminder that I am absolutely not built for this level of helplessness.”

Resident Evil 7 hits with the kind of confidence only a franchise reboot can have. From the moment you step into that rotting Louisiana mansion, the game grabs you by the throat and whispers:
“Remember when survival horror actually made you sweat?”

The opening hours are pure atmospheric gold — claustrophobic hallways, creaking floors, the Bakers casually violating every health and safety regulation known to mankind. You feel helpless, genuinely powerless, and it works beautifully. Then, as the game progresses, the power curve shifts. You go from “please don’t let anything move” to “fine, I’ll move it myself,” and that progression feels incredibly satisfying.

The graphics are fantastic, the gore is unapologetically intense, and the whole presentation nails that grimy, grounded horror tone. The story is decent and fits the setting perfectly — a twisted family drama wrapped in mold, misery, and questionable life choices. Sure, there are some plotholes and a few “wait, how does that make sense?” moments, but the atmosphere and gameplay are so strong that I happily looked past them.

By the time the finale ramps into full action‑showdown mode, it feels earned — a natural escalation from the terrified crawl through the mansion to the explosive endgame. It’s a wild ride, and it works.

A fantastic title, a brilliant reinvention of the series, and an easy recommendation.

Clear 5/5 — A horror masterclass that kickstarted my Resident Evil run all over again.

ACTUAL RATING: MIXED
Steam forces a binary choice, so I have to vote 'Recommended'. The first 4 hours are a survival horror masterpiece. But be prepared for the fact that the last 4 hours are a linear, mechanically bankrupt shooter. It was hard to recommend a game that completely abandons its own genre halfway through. If you buy this, get it on a massive sale and ONLY play on Madhouse difficulty.

If you are buying this strictly for a pure Survival Horror experience, you are going to be disappointed by the time the credits roll. Resident Evil 7 is a game with a massive identity crisis: it builds a flawless, terrifying foundation and then completely abandons it because Capcom was too terrified to commit to the tone.

THE GOOD:

    The Prologue: Exploring the Guest House in the daytime with zero context is arguably the best opening to any Resident Evil game ever. It lets the atmosphere and the silence do the talking.

    The Bakers: The first 40% of this game is a masterclass in game design. Jack Baker restricts your movement perfectly, and the psychological horror of the Marguerite section is the absolute peak of the game.

    The VHS Tapes: An incredible way to teach the player mechanics without breaking immersion. The "Happy Birthday" tape is literal genius and one of the best puzzles Capcom has ever designed, actively punishing you for spamming buttons.

THE BAD:

    The Mid-Game Cliff: The exact second you defeat Marguerite, the game stops being survival horror. It completely flatlines and turns into a highly unoriginal, linear corridor shooter. (Lucas Baker is still an amazing antagonist though)

    Unearned Power: The survival economy completely breaks. Your character is magically handed a submachine gun and a mountain of remote bombs that you didn't even have to work for, trivializing the rest of the game.

    The Final Act: The Salt Mines section is a painfully linear, incredibly short hallway sequence where the game morphs into the action-packed stupidness of RE4.

THE UGLY (Mechanics):

    Zero Enemy Variety: You fight exactly three types of Molded (Regular, Bladed, and a four-legged Licker-type). That's it. There are no grunts or unique variants to justify the arsenal you get.

    Brain-Dead AI: The Molded are slow, tethered to doorways (they despawn if you just walk into another room), and can be entirely cheesed just by holding the block button.

    Resource Overload: Even on Normal difficulty, you are handed so much Chem Fluid, enhanced handgun ammo, and shotgun shells that the tension completely evaporates.

THE EXPANSIONS (Not a Hero & End of Zoe):

    The Good: End of Zoe is pure, satisfying catharsis. Having a stalker enemy back is great, and Joe Baker's brawler mechanics—especially once you get the advanced gauntlet—are incredibly fun and exactly the kind of tactical combat Ethan lacked. Not a Hero introduces the "White Molded" (Regenerators) that require specific RAMROD ammo to kill; a brilliant survival-horror restriction that desperately belonged in the main campaign's Baker Estate.

    The Bad: Both DLCs are painfully short (about an hour each). Not a Hero ends with a boring final boss and feels like it either needed more development time or should have been scrapped entirely to flesh out Joe Baker's crafting system and extend End of Zoe.

THE VERDICT:
The environment design of the first half is incredible, but the combat balancing in the second half ruins it. They built a perfect haunted house and then handed you an assault loadout at the front door. The DLCs introduce great mechanical concepts, but suffer from incredibly short runtimes.

PSA: If you actually want to experience the Survival Horror loop Capcom originally intended, DO NOT play on Normal. Play on Madhouse Difficulty. It forces manual saves via Cassette Tapes, remixes item locations, makes Jack more aggressive, and is the only way the game actually justifies its own mechanics.

It's the game that saved the franchise, so I'm inevitably more lenient towards it because of my attachment to RE. That said, the game is really nice. The whole section at Baker's house is really suggestive and overall well though, and the various enemies, weapons and bosses are introduced progressively in a smart way. The latter sections (shipwreck and mine) feels way more like a run and shoot game, which lowers the overall quality of the gameplay (especially the remote bombs feel really stupid and are kinda immersion breaking in a way, given that you're playing a "horror" game).
The horror part is very present, but tends to vanish the more you go on; usually in RE games it happens because the more you go on, the more confident you become, but in RE7 you can feel the game switching from a survival horror to a fps, which is sub-optimal.

If you like RE games, I cannot but suggest it, but if you're new to the franchise, it would be better to start from RE2 or RE4, and then move to RE7.

[b]Resident Evil 7: Biohazard[/b] is a masterclass in survival horror that left a massive impact on the genre. Almost ten years ago, I remember watching people stream this game, and at first, I didn't even realize it was a [b]Resident Evil[/b] title. Since that day, I was completely hyped to experience it myself. While my love for survival horror stretches back as long as I can remember, few games in the genre manage to deliver an impact this profound. 

The industry experienced a major shift when Hideo Kojima released [b]P.T.[/b] (the [b]Silent Hills[/b] playable teaser). Even though it never progressed past a short demo, it permanently raised the bar for cabin-style horror. [b]Resident Evil 7[/b] is the closest any full game has ever come to capturing that exact, terrifying vibe. The atmosphere is thick, and the tension during gameplay is relentless - it constantly feels like something is hunting you. Shifting the focus heavily toward horror and away from action was absolutely the right move for the franchise.

The visual direction is incredible. The found-footage style aesthetics and tight camera movements give the environment a restricted, narrow, and claustrophobic feel. From a narrative perspective, it's a haunting experience because you spend most of your time fighting victims of a tragedy rather than a malicious enemy. Despite all the gore and violence, I felt a deep sense of sorrow for the Baker family. That final, emotional conversation between Jack and Ethan gave the story incredible depth. Lucas, on the other hand, was an absolute psycho whose pre-existing mental illness was simply amplified by the Mold to create pure chaos. 

Meeting the new characters was a joy, and seeing the return of Chris Redfield was a wild surprise—though he is definitely not the same guy we remember punching boulders... Overall, the story is fantastic. I cannot wait to see how Ethan's journey continues in the next chapter!

Resident Evil 7 is one of the scariest games I have ever played.

The atmosphere is incredible. From the moment you step into the Baker estate, everything feels disturbingly believable. The environment, the sound design, the pacing, and the overall horror direction come together to create a constant feeling of dread. I was genuinely terrified for large parts of the game.

What impressed me most is how immersive it felt. Many horror games eventually stop being scary and become action games with horror elements. Resident Evil 7 never fully lost that tension for me. There were many moments where my main feeling wasn't "that was fun" but rather "I actually survived that." I mean that as a compliment. The game does such a good job of making you feel vulnerable that every victory feels earned.

Because of that, I would give one warning: this game can be genuinely stressful. If you're looking for a more relaxed experience, this might not be for you. For me, however, that intensity is exactly what made it so memorable.

The story is engaging, the characters are memorable, and the game does an excellent job balancing exploration, puzzles, resource management, and horror. It feels like a perfect modern interpretation of what Resident Evil can be.

I would honestly call it a masterpiece of horror game design.

One thing that surprised me was how much I enjoyed the DLCs, especially Not a Hero and End of Zoe. After spending so much time feeling stressed and hunted throughout the main game, those DLCs felt incredibly satisfying and almost therapeutic. They let me release all the tension that had built up over the course of the story. That is purely a matter of personal preference though, and I don't think they are better than the main game. The main campaign remains the heart of the experience and is exceptional in its own right.

After experiencing RE4 classic, RE4 remake, and RE9, I've really only experienced the high-action based games so far, and this game has really shown me the true colors of the Resident Evil franchise. Being entirely horror and method based, I was on the edge of my seat throughout the entire game. There was no parry system, no roundhouse kick, and no abundance of ammo to save me. I needed to carefully plan what I needed to do while being chased or jumpscared left and right. I was completely hooked on the story as well, everything flowed perfectly, up to the very end, where I was met with the feeling of emptiness and shock after playing such a flawless game, just like the rest of the Resident Evils I've played so far. Amazing work as always Capcom.

For its time, this game was a breath of fresh air for the Resident Evil franchise. It took the series in a different direction, giving players a break from zombies and instead having them hunted by a deranged family, which Capcom executed exceptionally well.
From a graphical standpoint, the game was stunning for its time and, even today, it can still compete visually with some modern titles.
As for the story, it is relatively engaging, although certain elements could have been handled differently to better match the game's epic opening. As you progress through the game and get closer to the end, it becomes clear that the story grows a bit too fantastical, even within an already imaginative setting. The creatures that replace the zombies eventually become repetitive and tiresome, while the mutated main antagonists are so overpowered that even Superman would struggle to defeat them.
The game would have been a 10 out of 10 if it had not gone to such extremes and if the villains had remained in their human forms, which were already creepy and terrifying enough.
Replacing the T-Virus with a sticky black mold capable of regenerating tissue felt unnecessary. It would have been better if the story had been connected more directly to the events of Raccoon City and the T-Virus, rather than introducing what essentially amounts to an alien-like organism that falls from the sky.
For that reason, I give the game a score of 7 out of 10. Despite its flaws, I still highly recommend it.

For my first ever Resident Evil game (and frankly my first real horror game,) I thought that Biohazard was a masterpiece of a game, especially for it being made in 2017. The overall atmosphere of the game from the very start to around the final act of the game is extremely well, and you can really tell the amount of love and detail there was that was put into the game. However, I have 1 minor and 1 major qualm with the game. The first is the boss fights. There is a nice handful of boss fights within the game, however all of them are for 4 specific characters, that being Jack and Marguerite Baker, your wife Mia, and Eveline, the main villain of the game. While the fights for Jack do feel well done, I can not say the same for the rest. All of Marguerites fights are just dragged on and boring, Mia's fights (I didn't do the last one) are just heavily scripted, and Eveline's are just extremely tedious. But my major con for this game is basically just the final act. You would expect the game to end after giving either Zoe Baker or Mia the serum, but no, your gonna play for maybe another hour in a boring cargo ship and a mineshaft, before fighting a boring scripted boss fight. I sincerely don't know how I made it through the ship, but I'm glad I did. Is this better than 8? No. But does it execute horror better? Yes, 8.5/10

The Masterful Rebirth of Survival Horror
*Resident Evil 7: Biohazard* is the game that brilliantly saved the franchise by shifting to a terrifying first-person perspective and returning to the series' true survival horror roots. Dropping you into a decaying, isolated plantation in Louisiana, you play as Ethan Winters searching for his missing wife. It replaces the over-the-top action of previous entries with pure, claustrophobic dread, intense resource management, and one of the most terrifying antagonist families in gaming history.
🟢 Pros
 * **Terrifying Atmosphere:** The Baker family plantation is a masterpiece of horror level design—filthy, dark, deeply unsettling, and dripping with tension.
 * **Return to Classic Survival Horror:** Features fantastic old-school mechanics, including limited ammo, inventory management, safe rooms, and clever puzzles.
 * **The Baker Family:** Jack, Marguerite, and Lucas are unforgettable villains. The dynamic stalker mechanics, especially with Jack Baker chasing you, keep your heart racing.
 * **Immersive First-Person View:** The shift to first-person makes the horror feel incredibly personal, visceral, and intensely claustrophobic.
🔴 Cons
 * **Weak Enemy Variety:** Outside of the main Baker family bosses, the standard "Molded" monsters are repetitive and lack visual or tactical variety.
 * **Linear Final Act:** The final third of the game (especially the ship and mine sections) shifts away from psychological horror and focuses more on linear action, dropping the pacing slightly.
 * **Unskippable Cutscenes:** If you are replayng the game for speedruns or higher difficulties, being unable to skip the heavily scripted story cutscenes can feel tedious.
### Final Verdict: 9/10
> **Highly Recommended.** A horror masterpiece that completely revitalized the franchise. If you want a genuinely scary, atmospheric, and high-tension survival experience, this is an essential addition to your Steam library.
>

[h1] 💎 A Masterpiece I Managed to Severely Underestimate [/h1]
[hr][/hr]
For a long time, I bypassed RE7, mistakenly grouping it with RE6 and thinking Capcom had lost their way. The first few hours in the Baker house felt like just another gritty indie horror in the vein of [i]Outlast[/i] or [i]The Texas Chain Saw Massacre[/i]. But I decided to come back to the game, finish it—and man, what an [b]unpolished diamond[/b] it turned out to be!

[h2] What you need to know before playing: [/h2]

[b]Return to the roots:[/b] The game brilliantly misleads you. Yes, it starts as a claustrophobic slasher, but starting from the wrecked ship and the salt mines, it shifts hard into classic Resident Evil tracks. Laboratories, secret documents, corporate conspiracies, the mold virus, and that signature series spirit unfold at full throttle.

[b]The story puzzle:[/b] The narrative here is monumental. When you reach the finale and all the scattered pieces of the puzzle (who Eveline really is, what Mia's actual job was, and why Blue Umbrella is flying the choppers) click into place—it's pure bliss.

[b]The bridge to RE8:[/b] If you've already played [i]Village[/i], completing RE7 will blow your mind with how deeply connected they are. Eveline's backstory is a tragic drama, and Ethan Winters has officially entered my top tier of the most stubborn and badass protagonists in gaming history. The guy literally refuses to die as long as his family is in danger (and his iconic trick of splashing first-aid fluid to reattach severed limbs [spoiler]makes perfect biological sense now[/spoiler]).

[hr][/hr]
[b]The Verdict:[/b] If you, like me, once dropped RE7 halfway through, thinking it wasn't a "true" Resident Evil game—go back and give it a second chance. It’s a fantastic, atmospheric, and very smart horror game that restored the franchise to its former greatness.

[i]P.S. Make sure to get it with the "Not a Hero" DLC—it lets you play as Chris Redfield and finally punch Lucas in his smug face after all the nerve-wracking mind games he puts you through in the main game.[/i]

[b]10 severed chainsaw hands out of 10.[/b]

I have not felt both so loved and hated before playing this game. If you asked little silly me a few weeks ago how I felt about this game I would have told you it is probably one of the best horror experiences ever crafted by man. No matter how many times I play this game I will always be scared, either from the stupid bugs in the old house or Jack bolting it at Mach 10 towards me on Madhouse, I never feel safe in this game and I have never been more scared by a game or anything in my life.

Oh how stupid I was, the Bakers were never the true threat, the real horror in this game comes from the 100%. I have discovered a new rage inside me, the self-loathing I put myself through to continue getting all the achievements has not been matched by a single other person in human history. "Oh, I can get all the DLC cheap! Let me get it!" If only he saw the pain and agony I went through after getting one-shotted about 3609 times in Ethan Must Die, if only he saw the pain and agony I went through after getting one-shotted by Jack at the end of Nightmare. If only he knew, he could have prevented the torture I had to go through, but no, I sit here writing this review with a 100% game, was it worth it?

End of Zoe actually brought me to my knees, normal mode and Joe Must Die were both fine, challenging, but fine. No amount of CIA torture could have prepared me for getting The Only Guns You Need. Whoever created that achievement clearly must have a huge disregard for the human race and their sanities, I have died more to those crocodiles after beating up The Swamp Man for the first time than I have died in the entirety of the base game and its other DLC's. After a point, I wasn't even mad at dying, I just looked in the mirror and asked myself why I chose to do this with my free time, I felt nothing. After getting the Dual AMG's it was a bit more bearable, but I have not felt that much of indescribable anger and self-hatred in my whole 18 years of living. And you remember when I said Joe Must Die was fine? I kind of lied, the section after the floating platforms with the crocodiles might actually have been worse. Call me bad, call me trash, call me any names you like, I died on that section so many times I questioned myself if this 100% was still worth getting, after getting jumped by sodding crocodiles I think quite literally over 20 times I wanted to curl up in a ball and cry like a baby. This DLC's 100% is not for the weak, if you have even a slight doubt you can sucker punch crocodiles before they jump at you with their 300 I-frames I would highly advise against doing this 100%.

I won't even talk about 21 that much, whoever pitched that idea for a DLC clearly hates everyone around them, and whoever approved it might be just as bad as them. And no, it is not pure skill, you follow a guide to a T and suddenly the last opponent in Survival+ pulls out a trump card where he steals your wife and kids and then proceeds to Chinese waterboard you.

Those two DLC's actually brought me to the brink of insanity, if not for MrReign, dormouse03 and Mint Muffled, I think I would have transformed into a Molded. On the topic of Molded I hope whoever created the Four Legged Molded knows that they caused me intolerable anguish. After getting one-shotted by those in End Of Zoe and Ethan Must Die I have hated those crawly knobheads to no end.

Every other DLC in this game I guess you could call fun. Not A Hero professional mode is amazing, Daughters is amazing, Bedroom is amazing, Jack's 55th Birthday was strange.

So after my constant moaning and malding on this game, why do I recommend it? 
The base game is the pinnacle of horror even after 9 years, and I would like everyone to experience it.

But to you dear viewer, if you ever decide to 100% this game, just know you will end up absolutely despising this game, you will unlock new levels of hatred and rage after getting one-shotted by everything and everyone that breathes.

Go tell Aunt Rhody that everybody's dead? How about go tell Aunt Rhody that I have no sympathy for the human race after experiencing this game's DLC. Tell her I have lost sympathy for everything.

The remake of [i]Resident Evil 2[/i] was a pleasant and welcome change to the tedium and now obsolete design that is RE0 and RE1, and from the very first ten minutes of gameplay, I was hooked and actually looked forward to my journey to the game's conclusion even though it did have some frustrations that are just innately due to how [i]Resident Evil[/i] is designed.

Just like seemingly every other [i]Resident Evil[/i] title, there is a pattern where you play as both a male and female character. Each character in REmake 2, Claire Redfield and Leon Kennedy, have their own separate campaigns. Given that there are achievements for beating the game in less than five hours, the campaign can be relatively short but if you aren't rushing it, it's probably about seven to ten or so hours per character. Of course, REmake 2 resorts to that gimmick that developers love to implement where there is a "true ending" which requires you to beat the game four times to achieve it, two times on each character, and it mostly just comes across a cheap method to padding total game time. Your second playthrough on each character, if you opt for that, feels more like a sort of "what if" timeline parallel with a few changes like in the cutscenes and the final boss being more intense. It's a harder campaign in that item locations and puzzle solutions have been altered and there's a stricter ammunition economy. I didn't necessarily find it worth the free time to play through the game four total times but the first playthrough on each character was fun even if it doesn't differ too greatly between Leon and Claire's journey to make a difference. 

Overall, their respective campaigns are similar with only a few slight deviations in the areas they can unlock and explore and each protagonist will meet different unique NPCs but like I mentioned, it's still very similar that I don't think players are missing anything if they just beat it the one time and move on. But Claire is pretty hot in her redesign and so she deserved the playthrough after Leon, anyway. The themes differ where Leon's humble beginnings as a rookie police officer has that cop versus corruption and criminal investigation angle as he helps expose a conspiracy through an emphasis on action and duty, and Claire's story focuses more on family and protection as she is searching for her brother where it becomes more emotional and personal with themes of motherhood/sisterhood depending on how you interpret her relationship with the NPC that is unique to her storyline. But aside from these minor plot points, it's very identical in the bosses you fight with a slight variation where one person is pestered more often than the other by the impervious persistent pursuer that Capcom loves to rely on in this franchise in its delivery of horror, creating more of a prolonged chase sequence. 

This time around Mr. X appears and you can shoot his dumb hat off of his bald head but that's about it, as he is as durable as a tank when all you have is a pistol and shotgun. I felt that his appearance, especially so soon, was meant to usher you along into the next act of the game as he'll stomp around like a petulant child told to go to their bedroom and force you along. It is tense at first but the horror element is short-lived and his constant appearance does get sort of repetitive as he overstays his welcome. He's not particularly dangerous otherwise, doing very low damage if he does somehow sneak up on you, and so the threat level doesn't contribute to anything but just being another resource dump or character you need to try and sidestep. 

Fortunately, while you do begin with very minimum inventory slots, there's much less focus on backtracking akin to RE0 and RE1 because the game contains a storage box where you can stash your surplus of resources, including key items, and an important quality of life change where the game will depict a checkmark on a key item to alert you when it is now useless. You can also upgrade your inventory size as you play through the game, which is great, because one of the many compounding issues of the originals was a severe lack of inventory space for doing anything.

The first act of the game, exploring the museum turned police station, was where the game shone the most and where I had the most joy. It seemed to have the most detail and time spent in perfecting its environment with lots of terrifying compact, dark hallways and rooms to unlock where you are intrigued about what you'll find behind it, but the map quality drops with the next areas. I'd say the final area was probably the worst, and that's saying something as you also have to explore the overused sewer that exists in every zombie game, where some of the zombies (those that look infected by Poison Ivy from DC or something) in the final area just do not die at all.

As is part of its core design philosophy, zombies still take like eight bullets to permanently kill with exception to the special type of zombie I just mentioned in the previous paragraph. You'll shoot them in the head four times and there's a good chance that zombie will rise back to its feet seconds later. You'll perhaps learn that it's strategic to just shoot the zombies in the leg where you can cripple them and run around them or stab them with a knife to conserve ammo, but this is perhaps one of the most tedious aspects of RE as a franchise that honestly needs to be reconsidered. I don't really care if Capcom explained the durability of the zombified humans with logic and the virus making them stronger or whatever, it's just too radically different to how zombies have always been treated in entertainment mediums and especially in another Capcom franchise, [i]Dead Rising.[/i] Apparently, [i]Resident Evil[/i] has something known as "adaptive difficulty" where the game will adjust itself in real-time depending on your performance, but it seemed like it was always intent on trying to punish me regardless.

The puzzling, since that is seemingly a key component to the franchise, exists in REmake 2 but aside from two puzzles, nothing was overtly difficult and took any considerable time to understand or decipher. I did dislike the chess plug logic puzzle as it was badly worded and the block puzzle that is unique to Claire's story, but it wasn't too bad overall.

As the RE purists like to complain about with the remakes retconning the original lore, I'm not too sure as I'm not going to play the original RE2 and RE3 like I had to do with RE0 and RE1 since those are not yet remade like this one, but it wouldn't surprise me if Capcom censored anything too "objectifying" or toned down any sort of romantic tension and involvement between Leon and Ada, as we all know by now that heterosexuality is not something that can be demonstrated too proudly over the alternatives. 

But despite some of its flaws, I did have fun playing through Claire and Leon's story even if it could have used some mixing up and I can see myself eventually replaying REmake 2 in the future, which I cannot say the same for original RE0, RE1, or even the remake of RE3 despite how I did also enjoy REmake 3 while it lasted.

In my playing of each RE game, this is the objective ranking thus far: REmake 2 > REmake 3 > RE0 and RE1

Resident Evil 2 Remake is the most cohesive game I've ever played, and honestly one of the best designed games I've played in general.

The map and mission design are on another level. I don't just mean "good for a horror game" either. I truly mean the kind of level design that developers across the industry should be studying. Every room, shortcut, key item, and puzzle feels carefully placed, and the game constantly has you going back through familiar areas without it ever feeling repetitive. The R.P.D. is easily one of the best maps ever made in gaming. What impressed me even more was how immersive the world felt. Having most of the game take place on one connected map with seamless transitions between areas made everything feel real. Instead of feeling like a bunch of separate levels, it actually feels like one place you’re slowly learning and navigating over time. Very few single player games pull that off as well as this.

I also liked that the zombies were a bit tanky. In a lot of games that would be annoying, but here it makes sense. Leon is a rookie cop walking into a nightmare, not some overpowered action hero. Every encounter feels dangerous, and deciding whether to spend ammo or try to slip past enemies actually matters. Mr. X also adds a nice bit of spice to the game. Whenever you hear those footsteps, you immediately start thinking about how you're going to get around him since he can cut off a big chunk of the map. He forces you to adapt on the fly and keeps the tension high without ever feeling annoying.

The story was another surprise. Zombie stories are usually pretty predictable and all follow the same kind of formula, but Resident Evil 2 Remake actually feels grounded while still being creative. The characters feel like real people stuck in a disaster, and the Umbrella conspiracy, the G-virus, and everything going on under Raccoon City gives it its own identity compared to most zombie media.

Also HUNK is an absolute GIGACHAD. The man wakes up in a city-sized nightmare, loses his squad, and still treats escaping Raccoon City like it’s just another day at work. I’ve actually replayed the game more just to play his mode because it’s that fun. His theme song is also peak. He just reminds me of those strong silent type protagonists from early 2000s games that you don’t really see much anymore.

Resident Evil 2 Remake is one of those rare games where everything just clicks. The atmosphere, level design, survival mechanics, story, and replayability all work together perfectly. If someone asked me what the greatest survival horror is, I would point to this remake.
