[h2]RE2 Remake: A masterclass in survival horror[/h2]


After playing Resident Evil 1 a few months ago, I was honestly a little concerned about how much I would enjoy Resident Evil 2. While I could appreciate what RE1 was trying to do, a lot of its older design choices made it difficult for me to fully enjoy in 2026. Thankfully, RE2 Remake takes everything that made the classic Resident Evil games great and modernises it without losing what made them special in the first place.

After completing both Leon and Claire's campaigns, I can safely say I understand why this game is held in such high regard. It delivers fantastic survival horror gameplay, an incredible atmosphere, memorable characters, and some genuinely terrifying moments that had me constantly on edge.


[h3]The Good[/h3]


The first thing that stood out to me was the atmosphere. From the moment you step into the Raccoon City Police Department, the game does an incredible job of making you feel uncomfortable. Every dark corridor feels dangerous, every door could hide something waiting to jump out at you, and the constant lack of resources means you're always thinking carefully about every encounter. The Police Station itself is brilliantly designed and was easily my favourite area in the game. Slowly unlocking new sections, finding shortcuts, solving puzzles and learning the layout was incredibly satisfying.

The gameplay is where RE2 Remake really shines. It manages to keep the survival horror identity of the older games while making everything feel modern and smooth to play. Resource management remains important, enemies stay threatening for most of the game, and exploration is constantly rewarded. In fact, I somehow managed to miss the shotgun and several other weapons for almost my entire first Leon playthrough, meaning I got to the end of the sewers with little more than my starting pistol. While that was entirely my own fault, it showed just how much the game rewards players who take the time to properly explore.

One of the biggest highlights of the game is The Tyrant, known unofficially as Mr. X. I genuinely think he is one of the best horror enemies I've encountered in gaming. Hearing his footsteps getting closer while you're trying to solve a puzzle or search a room creates an incredible amount of tension. The first time he smashed through a wall in front of me nearly made me jump out of my chair. Even after completing both campaigns, he never really stopped being intimidating. The fact that he remains a constant threat without becoming overly frustrating is a huge achievement.

The presentation is also fantastic. The graphics still look incredible, with highly detailed character models, excellent lighting, and some genuinely impressive environmental detail. Combined with the excellent sound design, the game does a brilliant job of keeping the tension high. The music that plays when Mr. X spots you immediately puts you into panic mode, while the audio during the plant zombie sections was some of the most unsettling sound design I've heard in a horror game.

I also really enjoyed both campaigns. Leon and Claire are both likeable protagonists and I never found myself wishing I was playing as one over the other. Leon's campaign was probably my favourite from a gameplay perspective because I preferred his weapons, while Claire's campaign had some stronger story moments, particularly her relationship with Sherry. The differences between the A and B scenarios were also larger than I expected, giving the game a lot of replay value and making a second playthrough feel worthwhile rather than repetitive.


[h3]The Bad[/h3]


The biggest criticism I have is that while the story is good, it never reaches the level of greatness that some of gaming's best narratives achieve. There are some strong character moments and a few memorable twists, but this is very much a game carried by its gameplay, atmosphere and level design rather than its story.

I also think the A and B scenarios were a slight missed opportunity. I enjoyed both campaigns and appreciated the differences between them, but I would have loved to see them more directly interconnected. Instead, they often feel like alternate versions of events rather than two stories happening alongside one another. It isn't a major issue, but it feels like there was potential for something even better.

Finally, while the Sewers and Laboratory are both good areas, neither of them quite reaches the same level of quality as the Police Station. The opening section of the game is so strong that the later environments struggle to match it.


[h3]Final Thoughts[/h3]


Resident Evil 2 Remake is exactly what a remake should be. It successfully modernises a classic game while preserving everything that made the original special. The gameplay is excellent, the atmosphere is fantastic, the presentation still holds up incredibly well, and Mr. X is one of the most memorable horror antagonists I've encountered in a game.

More importantly, this is the Resident Evil game I would recommend to newcomers. While I can appreciate the importance of Resident Evil 1, Resident Evil 2 Remake feels like the perfect entry point for modern players. It delivers the survival horror experience the series is known for without many of the frustrations that come with older game design.

It may not have a masterpiece-level story, but almost everything else it does is outstanding.

Is it a perfect remake? No.
Is it a good one? Yes.

[b] PROS [/b]:
One of the remake's greatest strengths is its atmosphere. The game is dark, unsettling, and constantly keeps players on edge. Every hallway, room, and corner feels threatening, creating a sense of tension that rarely lets up. The sound design and lighting work together exceptionally well to make exploration both exciting and nerve-racking.

The graphics are another major highlight. Capcom did an outstanding job with the visuals, delivering detailed environments and impressive character models. The Raccoon City Police Department, sewers, and laboratory all look fantastic.

The gameplay is also excellent. The over-the-shoulder camera perspective is a perfect fit for a modern Resident Evil title, striking a strong balance between action and survival horror. Combat feels satisfying, resource management remains important, and the controls are responsive and intuitive. The remake successfully modernizes the classic gameplay without losing the tension and challenge that made the original game memorable.

[b] CONS [/b]:
While the story is enjoyable, it feels like a missed opportunity in some areas. The game could have benefited from additional cutscenes, particularly regarding William Birkin's transformation. In the original game, we were given a much longer (and frankly badass) cutscene showing Birkin's decision to inject himself with the G-Virus and then the massacre of the Umbrella soldiers. In the remake, much of this is reduced to a brief video recording found during the campaign, making his transformation feel less impactful and less emotionally engaging.

Another disappointment is the limited interaction between Claire and Leon. I almost forgot the other character even existed halfway through my first playthrough back in 2019. Since both characters are experiencing the same outbreak from different perspectives, it would have been nice to see more scenes that develop their relationship and show them working together throughout the story. In the original game, they interacted in the S.T.A.R.S. Office and regularly communicated via radio during certain sections, making it feel as though the other half of the story was still unfolding even when we weren't with them. Their interactions are memorable, but there simply are not enough of them, [spoiler] despite the two notes left by the other character in the Second Run and the brief scene before the final boss fight. [/spoiler]

The biggest issue is the implementation of the Second Run. Aside from a few alterations and an [spoiler] additional final boss battle [/spoiler], it feels largely unnecessary. Many of the same puzzles, boss fights, and locations are reused with only minor changes. Furthermore, there are several inconsistencies between Scenario A and Scenario B, creating confusion regarding the timeline and events. Rather than feeling like two complementary campaigns that tell different sides of the same story, the Second Run often comes across as a simplified copy-and-paste version of the first scenario. A more distinct and divergent approach would have made replaying the game far more rewarding.

[b] OVERALL [/b]
RE2R is an outstanding survival horror game that successfully modernizes a beloved classic. Its atmospheric world, impressive graphics, and excellent gameplay make it one of the strongest entries in the series. However, the story could have explored certain characters and events in greater depth, and the Second Run system falls short of the dual-scenario concept established by the original game. Despite these shortcomings, it remains a fantastic remake and a must-play for both longtime fans and newcomers to the franchise.

I played the original Resident Evil 2 back in 1999, and this remake feels very different, but in a good way. It is much more fluid and action-oriented, with modern gameplay that makes everything feel smoother and more responsive. The graphics are excellent and really bring Raccoon City to life.

One of the best additions is Mr. X. When he appears, the gameplay changes completely. What was once a methodical exploration game suddenly becomes much more tense, forcing you to constantly adapt and rethink your routes through the police station.

Strangely enough, though, it also feels a bit less scary than the original, and the game feels shorter than I remember.

Overall, it is a great game and a very successful remake. While it changes the tone quite a bit, I think those changes ultimately work in its favor.

The terrifyingly claustrophobic atmosphere of the R.P.D. station completely consumes you. Walking through pitch-black corridors with nothing but a flashlight, hearing the wet footsteps of a Licker on the ceiling, and realizing you only have 4 bullets left in your clip is pure, unadulterated dread.

Every single bullet is worth its weight in gold; you don't just shoot, you gamble with your survival. Missing a headshot means you're running for your life. And then there's Mr. X—the heavy, rhythmic thud of his boots echoing through the walls, knowing he is actively hunting you while you're trapped in a corner with no ammo, elevates the psychological horror to an unmatched level. Navigating Leon and Claire through this absolute nightmare to unlock every single achievement was an unforgettable thrill. Capcom set a golden standard here.

"Hold your fire! I'm a human!" — 9.5/10  [/i][/b]

After replaying the original Resident Evil 2 for the first time in ~15 years, I decided to revisit my review.

The easiest way to describe RE2 Remake is that it's not a replacement for the original. It's a different interpretation of the same story.

The gameplay is where the remake shines. Combat feels great, movement is responsive, survival horror is much stronger, and every encounter feels more dangerous. Mr. X is one of the biggest improvements. In the original he's mostly a B scenario scripted threat. In the remake he becomes a constant source of pressure and one of the most memorable parts of the game.

The puzzles are also much more interesting, the boss fights are significantly better, and every major area gets enough time to breathe. Unlike the original, where the game starts rushing toward the finish after the police station, the remake gives each location room to develop.

Marvin is another huge improvement. His introduction is one of the best character moments in the game, and after seeing the RE4 Remake knife easter egg it became even better.

What I missed from the original is the story structure. Ada feels more mysterious here, but she also gets much less development and backstory. Several side characters have less screen time, and some interactions that helped connect Leon, Claire, Sherry and Birkins together are either shortened or removed. The original also does a better job showing why characters end up where they do later in the story.

The atmosphere is different too. The remake is scarier and more oppressive, but the original captures the feeling of a dying city better. The opening streets and sound design still hit differently.

In the end, I don't think one version is better than the other. The remake delivers better gameplay, survival horror, puzzles and bosses. The original delivers a stronger overall story and better character interactions.

Both are different games and both are great.

[h1] This Game Sucks Ass! [/h1]

Resident Evil 2 was my first experience with the RE series, and it was anything but pleasant.

First of all, the game constantly forces you to backtrack to solve all kinds of puzzles, which quickly becomes annoying. The story is predictable and boring, and progressing through it feels like an actual chore. There are only two major boss enemies throughout most of the game: William and Mr. X. That's it.

The worst part, however, is the amount of ammo it takes to kill a regular zombie, even with perfect headshots. Only a few zombies will go down after two shots to the head. Most of them require four, five, or even SIX bullets before they finally die.

And you know what's even worse? Sometimes you'll clear out an area, leave, and then come back later only to find that some of the zombies have gotten back up and you have to deal with them all over again.

Keep in mind that this is a game where ammo is extremely limited and every bullet is supposed to matter. Yet because of this awful design, you're constantly running out of ammunition. I actually had to download a f**king trainer because I got completely stuck. I had no ammo left and literally couldn't progress without cheating myself some more.

That is absolutely unforgivable game design.

This game is everything a remake should be. Having played the OG on the PSOne years ago, revisiting Raccoon City in this modern version felt both nostalgic and fresh. It perfectly respects what made the original special while completely rebuilding the experience with stunning visuals, tighter gameplay, and a darker, more immersive atmosphere.

The game captures survival horror at its best, limited resources, constant tension, smart puzzle design, and unforgettable moments of fear, especially whenever Mr. X is hunting you down, Damn hate that guy🤣  Leon and Claire’s campaigns are both engaging, and the way the remake modernizes the classic formula without losing its identity is impressive. I played it twice, and it still delivered the same excitement. A masterpiece remake and easily one of the best Resident Evil games ever made.

[b]Resident Evil 2 Remake[/b] is just a really great survival horror shooter. It forces you to be careful at first, but after a couple of runs, you just memorize enemy spawns, behavior, and key item locations. By that point, you can run through the entire game in 1.5 or 2 hours, even with all the backtracking and full map-vacuuming for loot.

The game keeps the player in an artificial tension using music, loud sounds, ambient soundtrack, and their own reluctance to leave their comfort zone. The atmosphere is created so well that even on my sixth playthrough, hearing Tyrant’s footsteps nearby still made me nervous.

But in reality, the player is given more than enough opportunities even on the highest [u]Hardcore[/u] difficulty. With a basic ability to not catch every single punch from Mr. X or a zombie with your face, and not wasting trillions of rounds on every single enemy, you will have plenty of ammo and healing items.
By the end of the game, there were consistently 6 or 7 full heals in the storage box and a massive amount of ammo for the whole arsenal that I couldn't even use because there was no space for it.

All it takes is skipping a few super-tanky segments, not dumping the entire military budget worth of ammunition into those damn sewer turtles, and realizing that you can literally melt bosses down in 20 seconds using only a combat knife with a single flashbang and be totally fine.

Its replayability and immersive gameplay is what made me go for the platinum.

After completing Resident Evil 2 Remake 100%, getting every trophy, every record, and every S+ rank, I can honestly say this is one of the best gaming experiences I've ever had.

What impressed me the most was how replayable the game is. Whether you're trying to improve your rank, learn better routes, unlock rewards, or experience the story again, the game always gives you a reason to come back. Every run feels more rewarding than the last, and it's one of those rare games that never really gets old while you're working toward 100%.

The gameplay is fantastic, the atmosphere is tense, the level design is excellent, and the balance between action and survival horror feels just right. Very few games stay this fun after multiple playthroughs, but Resident Evil 2 Remake manages to do exactly that.

Honestly, my biggest criticism is that you eventually run out of Resident Evil 2 Remake content. Once you've unlocked everything, completed every challenge, and seen all the game has to offer, there's not much left to do except wish there was more. To me, that's a sign of a great game.

The only thing I would've liked is for the Cat Ears accessory to be usable in the main story mode instead of being locked to The Ghost Survivors. After all the work required to unlock them, it would've been nice to use them in a regular playthrough.

Overall, Resident Evil 2 Remake is a masterpiece and one of the most replayable games I've ever played. Even after 100% completion, I still wish I had more of it left to experience.

This game is the origin story of two iconic RE characters: Leon Kennedy and Claire Redfield. RE2 has two separate story arcs to play through with each character. If you finish one character, you can progress to another character. The game sets it up as Leon A and Claire B or Claire A and Leon B. Amazing survival gameplay where you have to constantly pay attention and think carefully, or you’ll get gobbled up by zombies or miss clues that will help you solve crucial puzzles for progression. The ost for this game is great, especially when it comes to a certain enemy, and the side games are pretty fun as well. Would suggest waiting until you beat both Leon and Claire before doing the side games because they do have minor spoilers for the main story. With how amazing the game is, it's no wonder Capcom made multiple other games featuring Leon (like in RE4 and Requiem) and Claire (Code Veronica), and added those two in collabs with several other franchises. Would highly recommend.

This game was certainly a doozy! It's a game I've played across multiple consoles, but have never 100%. Well, until now. There's something so charming about this game, the environment, and the story that always keeps me coming back for more. 

I truly have no notes for the environment Capcom has created. However, the 4th survivor and Ghost survivors extra content SUCKS. I felt they were hard for the sake of being hard. Another issue I did have was I did feel like the story was a little half baked. The A and B runs have massive continuity issues and it felt rushed. Almost like the game wasn't supposed to have the A and B runs and it was an added change mid development. 

I feel like the A and B runs had a lot of potential and could've had massive changes to the story and the path the characters take. Despite all that, I did enjoy the story overall. Claire I felt had the most comprehensive story. Her run specific files give a lot more to the world building and gives you more fun and interactivity with the story over Leon's run. He is essentially told the plot from his partner, Ada. So it made his run feel a little more disconnected.

Overall, I really enjoyed this game. I will be playing it again, just probably not the extra modes. I really liked the content that the story has to offer and I love watching the unique challenge runs people do. It is a great start to the Resident Evil franchise if you've never played it, while still being a great edition for Veterans of the world.

Bought this game as a part of the Resident Evil Remake trilogy while it was on sale (80% discount I think). I've never actually played any of the RE games before, but i knew what it was. I've watched walkthroughts of RE 7 Biohazard, RE Village and RE Requiem. Anyways, now about the game. So i'm not usually the guy that likes horror genre because I get easily scared and piss my pants, but this game was definitely worth it. I will try to explain why without spoiling anything. 
First of all, the story. The story is absolutely satisfying and it offers dozens of thrill, intrigue and scary moments. The thrill is found in parts where you're either being chased by the boss or fighting him. Intrigue and dose of mystery when you're investigating new part of the map and scary part is obviously in those jumpscares and mini jumpscares that follow you through the whole story. I was playing on moderate and I have to say that it was just hard enough for you to actually try and also just easy enough defeating bosses to still give you that satisfaction. One minor (and the only one) gripe I have about the story is that it all can feel a bit rushed, but that's just my critique anyway. Another excellent thing is that the game offers you 2nd run after you finish your first one, offering you to be faster and compare your overally progress. The story is generally well thought out. 
Secondly, the game mechanics. I must mention the ammo and weapons. So unlike in other games that give you weapons, here, they are not as OP, at least not most of them. Ammo is something you don't want to waste if not necessary. The game offers you just enough ammo to survive throughout the game. The fact that the limit of ammo is so clear made me try harder and actually think about where and how I will use every single ammo. Okay, now on to the puzzles and item based progression. So the puzzles in this game are not too hard, but they definitely make you think for a moment or two. I do wish that they were a bit harder, but once again, that is just my taste. Item based progression is I'd say the core of not only this specific game, but the whole RE franchise. Come to one point, you need this, you find that, you continue. The game is also based on these moments when you either find that crucial item for your story to continue or you put it on its place. When that happens, the game does that mini autosaves and you don't know if you're cooked or if you're good. Also, the crafting in the game. Whilst the game doesn't give you as much ammo or heals, it gives you resources that can be combined with other of the same kind to craft more ammo or heals. I really like that mechanic. Next we have multiple safes and lock vials that can be unlocked with discovering what their codes are. They are not crucial for the story, but it can definitely help you and make you stronger. One thing that you have to watch out aswell is the free space in your inventory. Just how many times did my inventory get full when i needed him to be emptier, making me discard something to make additional space. 
Thirdly, the graphics. The graphics is just amazing. Amazing! Main character(s) look stunning. They are gorgeous. Very deatiled, the facial expressions, the clothes, the hair etc. 
Lastly, mobs. There are multiple types of mobs that you have to kill based on how hard is it to kill them or how do they act, how fast they are etc. Bosses...they are interesting. Only thing I'll tell you that you won't be left disappointed.
To conclude, this game is a banger and I think that, for that time when it was made, which i think it's like 2019 or so, that is absolutely crazily good. For anyone that still hasn't played this and isn't sure if he7she shall buy it or not, I recommend you to buy. For anyone that likes horror genre, I recommend you to buy. For anyone that wants their brain to work while playing this game, I recommend you to buy. I don't know when was the last time I felt like the game I was playing was not just the waste of time before playing this game. Lastly, i recommend everyone to buy this cuz oh man it is one hell of a classic.

After completing all 44 achievements, multiple S+ runs, challenge runs, and every extra mode, I can confidently say that Resident Evil 2 Remake is an outstanding modernization of the classic formula.

What impressed me the most is how the game creates tension without relying on outdated mechanics. The difficulty comes from decision-making, route optimization, inventory management, and resource conservation—not from fighting against the controls or awkward camera angles.

Mr. X deserves special praise. He wasn't scary to me as much as he was stressful in the best possible way. His presence constantly forces you to adapt your plans, rethink your routes, and manage your resources more carefully. Even after several playthroughs, hearing his footsteps nearby never stops creating pressure.

The map design is excellent. Once you learn the RPD and start optimizing your routes, each run becomes faster and more satisfying. Going from feeling lost to completing S+ runs in under two hours is incredibly rewarding.

The extra modes are also fantastic. HUNK and Tofu add a fun twist to the gameplay, while "The Ghost Survivors" offers unique challenges that test your knowledge of the mechanics in different ways. "No Time to Mourn" was definitely the toughest challenge for me, but overcoming it felt great.

If I had one minor criticism, it's that completing S+ on the B scenarios doesn't unlock any additional rewards or achievements. Considering the effort required, some extra rewards would have been nice.

Overall, Resident Evil 2 Remake perfectly balances survival horror, action, and strategic gameplay. It respects the original while modernizing everything that needed to evolve.

Whether you're a longtime fan or completely new to the series, this is an easy recommendation 9/10

Resident Evil 2 Remake is the game that made me fall in love with the Resident Evil franchise as a whole. It's captivating and does an AMAZING service to the original, which I also played (and honestly enjoyed much more than the remake, but that's a review for another day). The puzzles, the absolute terror I felt whenever I saw Mr. X, the environment! There are a lot of aspects to this game that I could rant about forever and ever, but what really gets me is the simplicity of it. You've got zombies and two campaigns where you're playing as relatively normal people who just want to help and escape with their lives. Leon and Claire are some of the best RE protagonists we have. I think that the intertwining of Leon and Claire's stories could have been done a bit more intricately, but that's really a minor complaint when it comes to RE2R. Both Leon A and Claire B are incredibly done, and it's so cool to see the level of detail the devs put into making the runs unique yet similar to one another. Seeing the same location from a different perspective really changes your experience.

TLDR; If you're new to Resident Evil, start with this game. If you're anything like me, you'll end up seventeen games later writing a nine paragraph steam review.

I'll start off with this - I haven't played the original RE2 and I don't intend to. The first RE game I ever played was RE5.

With Resident Evil I've always felt like there's really not that much to the story. We all know it has something to do with viruses and bioweapons and the misuse of both. The question has always been, what creative way have they thought up to cover this fact into. I won't lie, for me RE has never been about some deep lore, but it has been about the characters, which brings me to say -what a nice cast we got in this game.

More in depth - Leon and Claire, each having separate goals arrive at a gas station and meet in most disturbing circumstances when they are both being attacked by zombies. They high-tail it from there and arrive in Raccoon city to find it infested with more of the same horrors. It is when a truck crashes into their car that their path diverges, but somehow they both end up in the same mess regardless. Leon being bound by duty as a police officer, he intends to uncover what has happened in the city. Claire, a younger sister of a different RE protagonist, looking for breadcrumbs to find a trail that would lead her to her brother.

The game is separated into two campaigns - the player can choose between Leons campaign and Claires, and each of their campaigns are supposed to compliment the other. My first thought is that each of their campaigns would somehow intertwine in some interesting and logical way, but to my misfortune that's just not the case. I'd say about half of each characters campaigns is truly unique, but the rest of it are generally the same events and same boss battles which did pull me out of the immersion quite a bit, especially since both are supposed to happen at about the same time. To get the true ending I had to play through Leons campaign and and then Claire's B campaign.
I do wonder what happens the other way around, if I played as Claire first and then Leon's B campaign, but I'll play through it that way some other time.

The game confused me at the start, having played RE5, RE7 and RE8 before, I figured it should be similar to those - shoot first and think later, but boy, was I mistaken. RE2 is more of a survival horror with 3rd person shooter elements. Having come across my first zombie I figured I had shoot it.. dead. Ended up unloading my only magazine on just the one zombie leaving me defenseless against the horde that was out there. In fact, there's not enough bullets to beat every single enemy in the game. Instead one has to be sparing with ammo and focus on inventory management and save most of the bullets for really difficult encounters or boss battles. Luckily, there are quite a few different weapons in the game and they each have upgrades that keep making them feel fresh. Plus, there's ammo crafting. There are also alternative weapons like grenades and knives that can serve as either a get out of jail-free card when the character gets grabbed or can be used to stun or blow up a bunch of enemies. When it comes to the knife - can be used to cut down bosses with slashing efficiency. Crafting also applies to healing items as is customary in RE games.

In order to progress through the game, one has to solve a lot of well made and some funky puzzles. One of the first ones the player will stumble upon - have to find 3 emblems scattered through the first location - the police station, in order to open a secret passage. Getting each piece requires the player to find the right combination of glyphs to unlock each emblem from their pedestals and, as for the combinations, they are written on some notes the player can pick up from someone. I don't mind these puzzles' they're not bad, but they do require quite a bit of backtracking every so often, which does get tiresome going through the same corridors all the time to pick up a thing that was locked behind a door where a different McGuffin was needed to open it. Through each campaign, each character will have their own unique sets of puzzle combinations to keep it fresh.

Let's talk about the enemies. I do love the monster designs in RE. We have the classic zombie - cannon fodder that cane take quite the beating and still continue to stumble towards the player character. Visually, not interesting, but effective. Zombie dogs - they are fast, but take very few shots to take down. Lickers - these things are creepy and annoying, they are fast and have a very long range, and the fact they crawl around and are quite uncomfortable to kill because of that. G Adults were crazy, I think they're not supposed to be killed at all, but dodged instead, they're large, bulky and have a lot of health. Lastly we have the plant infested zombie that resurrects after a while. All of them have the same weakness though - fire and knives. Then there's Mr. Tyrant who shows up at 1/5th of the game and chases the player giving the game an even more ominous atmosphere. I won't spoil the bosses though, but I did like the G's designs from his 2nd version.

Speaking of atmosphere and visuals, the game looks very good, though it does suffer from too many shiny/wet surfaces. Character designs and monster designs are spot on and the world design is ominous and dark in many places but beautiful in it's own way. Generally the lighting is very good, especially with RT enabled, but looks good without it also. My only gripe is with the facial expressions, they're not very expressive but it is what it is.

Audio was good and I don't have any complaints. Leon did have some cheesy one-liners at times, but it's not an issue. Sound design was really good and prepares the player for the occasional jump-scare noise.

All in all, it was a good experience and highly recommended. The game is quite difficult though with some crazy difficulty spikes at times, but very enjoyable none the less. I give it a 7/10. The way the campaigns were structured left me wanting a bit more as I had to fight the same bosses with both Claire and Leon in the same arenas in A and B campaigns, even though they're meant to tell the tale from two sides.

I don't know if it's just nostalgia, or because i haven't played good games for a while what makes feel like this is the best game in the world, but i am sure that this is one of the games perfectly made and tuned to give player the best survival horror experience ever

Before I played this game, I had only tried Biohazard when it released years ago. I really liked Biohazard but never went further into the other games. I bought most of the RE's on Sale, and I started with RE2R.

The opening gives a good feeling about what you can expect from the rest of the game, or so I thought.
You have scarce ammo, need to keep a good eye on healing items or gunpowder to craft with. And there is a lot of backtracking through the different levels (if you really know what you are doing, you can avoid this somewhat). The Zombies are not the biggest danger at first but will get progressively stronger throughout the 8ish hours you wander Raccoon City.

But there is more to it than that. after some time you realize there are things out there that are more dangerous than any zombie could ever be. The Lickers, G-Adults, Zombie Dogs. And lets not forget about Mister X (Tyrant). The feeling that this huge walking bioweapon is always on the search for you can be terrifying and gives me Goosebumps every time I hear his footsteps echoing through the halls.

Also the fact that there are 4 story's to play + a bunch of extra modes really sells this game and adds a lot to the replayability. i only have 19 hours while writing, but I know there will be a lot more.

Overall I give this exciting and thrilling game a 8.5/10

I never thought I'd attempt to play a RE game after years of avoiding it. Honestly, I'm way too easily scared to actually enjoy a horror game, and plus zombies single  player aren't really my thing. Never quite understood how they became such a popular theme in the first place. But hey, I had a blast with RE:2. Playing it is stressful and nerve-wracking, and I still don't understand why I put myself through it, but I had to say this is overall a very refined and polished survival horror experience even with  the  anxiety heartrate when mr x walks pasts.

So, how scary is it? Well, for the record, I chickened out and chose the easier difficulty (shame on me -.-), so for me it was actually not as scary as I had prepared for, but still it got me good quite a few times.. probably also just because I'm so sh!te and that just balanced out the easy difficulty level. Anyway, it feels more survival and shooting than horror for me.

Then we have the puzzles. They are (thankfully) quite easy, and most of them just involves finding and collecting objects and installing them (or using them in some way and most of  the  time panicking  where to go when i hear  the stomping) in the correct place. But of course, you'll have to deal with zombies and whatnots in the proccess of solving these puzzles, and that is the name of the game essentially. 

My favourite aspect is the scarcity of the resource and your extremely limited inventory space, and that's obviously the core of the survival elements in this, as in previous titles. You really have to think what to take and what to throw away and make most use of what you have at hand, which is a lot of fun. Again, I'm so sh!te at scavenging stuff that by the time I beat the game I still only had a handgun and a granade launcher even so, i honestly had a great time with this game eventhough being scared almost the whole time and  will be  looking forward for the  other resident evil games  like resident evil 4 and resident evil requiem.

One of the best games I've ever played and a great entry point into the Resident Evil franchise.

The gold standard for remakes.

Classic RE2 is a classic for a reason but the tank controls, fixed camera, cheesy voice acting, and PS1 graphics make it a hard sell these days especially for a horror game (I personally never found classic RE2 scary). RE2 remake on the other hand with the still jaw dropping visuals (literally jaw dropping with some zombies), fantastic sound design, and perfectly fine tuned balance of survival and horror makes for one of the best survival horror experiences out there. It successfully updates the old school RE formula. It's one of the rare games that make a regular zombie terrifying by making them these slow inevitable threats that take over 6 headshots sometimes to take down. Getting chased by Mr. X is such a dynamic intense experience every time. The campaign is very replayable and there's bonus content that'll test your skills. An easy recommend. There's a reason so many franchises (especially horror) are now making big remakes of older games. It's because this one was that good.

Im playing on DX12 version, but ive heard DX11 runs better, so if you have any performance issues with the game - try running DX11 beta version instead. You will have to download +20GB of data again tho. If you prefer to stick with the DX12 version, but while the game likes to crash often (even on startup), try using -dx11 launch option command in the properties menu on steam, this might actually help. 

UPDATE: in my case, i actually had to restart my PC for the game not to crash anymore, because i didnt shut it off nor restart it for a long time and this game actually didnt like that somehow - game was crashing like crazy, during start up, while alt-tabbing every time etc. While other games like Metro franchise were all good in that regard, no matter for how long i didnt restart my computer, and i consider Metro games being worse optimized than these Resident Evil ones - so this difference is really weird, when i think about it.

Finally, my old save files from 2024 doesnt work in neither DX11 or DX12 versions, which sucks. For the 100 percent steam achievement grind, ill have to play the full run from complete 0 once again. Well, its a good game, so i guess this is not that big of a problem.

★★★★★ — “What a remake… 25+ years later. Damn, I feel old.”

Booting up the Resident Evil 2 Remake in 2026 feels like someone handed me a time machine and a slap in the face at the same time.
Twenty‑five years after the original PS1 panic‑fest, I’m back in the RPD — except now everything looks so good it’s borderline disrespectful to my childhood memories. The atmosphere is thick, moody, and so perfectly crafted that even walking down a hallway feels like a life decision.

The remake doesn’t just modernize RE2 — it obliterates the old jank and replaces it with silky‑smooth movement, tight gunplay, and lighting that deserves its own award. Leon and Claire glide through the station like Capcom finally decided to give them knees that bend. Zombies? Horrifying. Lickers? Still therapy‑worthy. Mr. X? Now legally classified as cardio training.

And the best part: this remake didn’t just impress me — it triggered a full Resident Evil run. One minute I’m enjoying the nostalgia hit, the next I’m knee‑deep in the entire series again, wondering how many hours of my life I’m about to sacrifice to Umbrella‑related chaos.

What a remake. What an atmosphere. What a reminder that I’m getting older while Leon somehow keeps getting prettier.

Clear 5/5 — A masterpiece that reignited my whole Resident Evil marathon.

So, like, I own more than 6 RE games and I have to say that, for me, they all pale in comparison to this one. Like, the story is simple, but it doesn't have to be anything special; the main point of it is how these two inexperienced survivors traverse the hellish Raccoon City. I always gravitate more to Claire's story than Leon's, so now that we know that the Code: Veronica remake is real, it's even better. But, like, the horror in this game is on another level, and it doesn't have to have those magical-like things that are in Village; it's horror at its simplest.

And this game has my favorite stalking antagonist, Mr. X. He is so menacing because you know you can't do anything but slow him down for a few seconds, and every noise you make, he reacts and comes to you. Like, I had nightmares about those footsteps when you are low on ammo and there is no safe room close. The sewers are worse than the police station but still quite good, though those sewer monsters are really unfun to fight against.

Then, as you come down to the laboratory as Claire, I feel like the quality of the design suffers a little bit, like with all RE games. What I mean by this: at the police station, there were things you found earlier that you didn't need, and because of the limited inventory, you couldn't take them. You later remembered that it was there and you went to pick it up. In the laboratory, there is nothing like that there; it's very linear, but it's still a lot of fun. So, I have to say that especially Claire's story was one of those campaigns that I never forgot, and every year I replay this amazing game, so I couldn't recommend it more.

(6 months old review, woops!!)
When Mr. X first showed up I totally froze, trapped myself in a room aiming at the door, and pondered for minutes whether or not I was gonna put up with this. Turns out, once you understand his behaviour and the places he can't go, it's not that bad. But the point still stands, that this fairly simple idea of an unbeatable tank freaked me out in ways other resident evil games couldn't manage. You could say that it's only because it's unkillable, but damn, the look of the creature plus the introduction of its heavy footsteps really sells the nightmare of it all. It's a terrifying order, but not bypassable, you just have to play its little game and learn how to do it. And I think that is at the core of what makes re2 such a delightful entry in the series.

I haven't played the original game, simply because I totally suck at fixed-perspective games. It's probably good, I don't know. That is to say, I'm treating this remake as a standalone thing, and as such, I totally get the hype for "mansion-based" resident evil games. I have a blast with the more rollercoastery type games that were re4 and 5, but going back and forth in a single labyrinthian location, slowly untangling your way throughout, is a terrific and rewarding feeling. It tests navigation, resource management and puzzle solving all at the same time. Mr. X wouldn't have worked in re4, but here, it makes sense, since you're supposed to get progressively familiar with the layout of this place. All this leads to a killer first half of re2 that I couldn't get enough of.

Sadly, that is quite literally just the first half. The second half goes back to the more hallway-y re design of re4 and 5. There's nothing inherently wrong with that, but the mansion segment really built re2 up to be a different, way more methodical sort of game, and it doesn't quite lend itself to this change of pace. Resident evil 4 is one of the most bombastic and exhilerating games I've ever played, so putting re2's design in this type of scenario leads to a lot more frustration and annoyance than I would've liked. There are some great moments here, like the two final bosses which I really enjoyed, but the peak of the game is long behind.

Now, I did play the second scenario with Claire to see what changes, and it was certainly interesting to say the least. There are some good changes here and there, like Mr. X showing up way earlier, getting access to some previously locked areas, and the whole orphanage section, but by and large, it repeats a lot of the same beats as Leon's story, making the supposed overlapping storyline shtick feel very silly and wasted. But on a gameplay standpoint, I didn't entirely mind this, since it gave me a reason to essentially replay the game with the benefit of hindsight, and as a result, I had a much less difficult and frictious time. Parts like the lab which made me tear my hair out at first, went by very easily in the second go, and I didn't even die in the final boss.

This is to say, there's a level of learning curve to this game that transcends the duration if its campaign, and that's okay. I still would prefer the game to have sticked to the mansion from beginning to end, it really is far and away the best thing about this game, but the rest of the game does benefit from repeat playthroughs, and is very good in its own right. RE2 is, at the end of the day, a very lopsided but engaging experience that reminds me why I dig this series so much, and I really hope its type of design gets revisited some day. I played this at the recommendation of my girlfriend and I had a blast! Previously I thought that horror games weren't my thing, but in the case of Resident Evil at least, I was proven wrong. 

First of all I have to say that the game looks great, both in the graphical and artistic sense, and the gameplay feels really tight. The story and dialogue are on the goofy side but as far as I know that is an expected and beloved part of the series by now. 

I used to look at gameplay from RE games and think that it wouldn't be my thing, because it looked really unsatisfying to me. That the player has to use so many bullets to put even one ordinary enemy down, multiple headshots even, and on top of that the aiming looked really clunky, ammunition hard to come by AND enemies kept coming back for some reason even though it looked very much like they were killed. That just didn't entice me. 

But after playing this one I get it. Everything is precisely tuned to make the game challenging and stress the player out. For example your character sprints relatively slowly, which will add to the stress of a lot of situations, but the sprint is still just fast enough to avoid certain attacks by a hairs width, if timed correctly. 

The weird aiming and ammo scarcity follows the same principle. In order to succeed you have to stay calm and collected, while the game is actively trying to stress you out. If you don't take your time to aim and make sure your bullets actually count, you'll be ♥♥♥♥♥♥. 

Then of course there's the exploration and puzzle solving part of the game, probably the core of the experience. This is also super satisfying. It almost makes the game feel like its not linear, even though it is mostly, because you keep exploring and finding new stuff within the same level, unlocking new rooms and finding new clues.

The modern remake of Resident Evil 2 stands as one of the most tightly designed games in the survival-horror genre, redefining the power of a game remade in full HD terror. After playing Resident Evil 0, I was excited to jump into the second chronological game as it was famous for being some people's favorite game spanning the entire series through the 2019 remake and I can safely say that this game exceeded any and all expectations I had prior in the most atmospheric and horror filled game I have ever played. 

Firstly, the game's visuals are absolutely stunning with the remake fully utilizing the RE engine to its fullest capacity to maximize each section of the game through its design, the characters and enemy design alike are top of the line as everything comes to life around the corner whether you like it or not. The sound design is also impeccable as every single sound emanates through in helping to craft the perfect horror experience from zombie moans or atmospheric effects such as the weather and flickering lights throughout the whole game. The gunplay has seen a complete evolution when compared to previous titles switching from fixed camera angles to over the shoulder combat which has drastically elevated the gameplay to be more action-packed while not tuning down the horror aspect with it only growing intensely through every encounter as you have a frontal view of everything approaching. The headshot mechanic is also well utilized as every single headshot has a chance to blow up a zombie's head leading to better ammo management if you're lucky but the RNG of it always makes encounters thrilling from the start. Mr. X injects an entire layer of tension of horror into the game when he is introduced as simple traversal is flipped on its head when he is chasing you with the sound design being equal parts terrifying and foreboding as you need to plan your next move fast lest you get decked quickly. Leon & Claire are well introduced as the game's main characters and I can see how they've become series favorites with how charming they are and I'm curious how future games will handle them as protagonists or simply moving figures within the series overall. I felt more emotionally connected playing as Claire with her taking care of Sherry, but Leon's campaign was still a joy to play through in seeing how his and Ada's relationship developed in the face of all the horror of Raccoon City. Each area of the game is pretty fun to traverse with my favorite being the RPD as everything is well connected and it's just fun to navigate with more being unlocked, the sewers as a whole were fine with navigation pretty quick if you know what you're doing and the lab was pretty expansive which I liked compared to how cramped the first game's was. 

It's hard to find anything to complain about with how much I loved the game with my only true complaint being how spongy enemies were as a whole as they did take a proper amount of ammunition to deal with as you also can cripple most without needing to kill, but when basic zombies can take almost ten bullets to fully kill it can be tiresome at times especially with how many are thrown at once. 

For achievements, the game had a number of various requirements similar to past titles such as beating it without healing and collecting everything with some new ones thrown in such as not opening the item box or taking a minimum amount of steps while playing. As a whole, it's just as comprehensive as the first game with needing to complete it multiple times as both Leon & Claire with ranks being awarded based on time and making sure to complete each campaign on Hardcore difficulty. The highest rank in the game was S+ which wasn't required for completion, but the reward granted was enticing enough to want to try for it and with enough practice I didn't find it too challenging barring some annoying sections in the sewers. The hardest achievement for me was completing the No Way Out scenario in the DLC.

Overall, I had a phenomenal time playing through Resident Evil 2. I wasn't sure how the first game could be eclipsed by another title, but this game had everything I could ever want out of a sequel with the perfection of the survival-horror genre as a whole. Everything about the remake was crafted masterfully to provide a horror filled experience with more emotional beats and non-stop thrills to carry all the way through the game's story. The characters all help the world come to life as you're thrusted into the world in the shoes of Leon as they try to navigate the city's horrors with the tone persisting the whole way through. The true ending of the game also helps to instill a drive to see through to stopping Umbrella and to peel back the layers of mystery leading to the events of the game and what may occur in the future. This game is a must play for anyone curious about the series with it being an incredible starting point or simply to play one of the best survival-horror games ever made.

This was one of my favorite gaming experiences ever. I’m not really a horror fan, but Resident Evil 2 has always been a rare exception.

Back in the PlayStation 1 days, it gave me one of my first truly immersive gaming memories. I used to play with headphones on, completely absorbed by the atmosphere, the sounds, the tension, and the jump scares.

So I was nervous about the remake. I didn’t know if it could capture that feeling again. But honestly, it did more than that. It improved it.

Since I hadn’t played the original since the late 90s, many details felt fresh again. The remake changes enough to feel new while keeping the soul of the original. The over-the-shoulder camera makes it more immersive, the visuals are excellent, the voice acting is strong, and the story moves better.

This is exactly what a remake should be: faithful, modern, and improved where it matters. Capcom nailed it.

I had a blast replaying it, and even my 12-year-old son got hooked. It also completely revived my interest in the franchise. Now I want to keep playing the rest of the series.

This is why remakes should exist.

Resident Evil 2 Remake is a gem among remakes. I will stand by that. The quality in gameplay, graphics, and character design is just absolutely phenomenal. This is what the golden standard should be for RE remakes. It set a great standard and guideline for future projects, and if only Capcom held onto that...

Though the story and the characters slightly - really only much slightly - differ from the original, which I wouldn't even claim is *that* dated for a 1998 game, it still manages to bring out the main aspect and core of horror and puzzles that most people would expect from a Resident Evil game. You're running around the R.P.D., not sure if Mr. X is on your trail just yet, and once you hear those heavy steps rushing towards you, all while you're middle in unlocking a little box, you start panicking. What do I do? How do I run? Can I make it into temporary safety before he catches up to me again? It's unsettling to be pursued relentlessly by this massive bioweapon...and once you do get to a safe place and unlock that little annoying box, you figure it was pointless. Thanks for the missing key cap, I'm sure it will help me destroy my stalker.

All jokes aside, Resident Evil 2 is fun, scary for the first few runs, but it's filled with story and action, and the fact that you can witness the beginning of the downfall of Raccoon City in so many different perspectives makes this game so replay worthy. I had a good laugh here and there when it came to the second runs for both Claire and Leon. I really truly enjoy the diversity in which Capcom is offering us with this. I think the only segment I will always ♥♥♥♥ my pants in is Sherry's segment...then again, she must be scared out of her mind as well. Poor girl :(

Even the little bonus scenarios, The Ghost Survivors chapters, were very fun to play! They again just gave us even more perspectives and I truly appreciate that as a RE lore enthusiast. It's refreshing to be able to witness the core of Resident Evil's beginnings in Raccoon City from a mere civilian perspective. It reminds me strongly of the Outbreak games. I love that little touch. 

Except the No Way Out scenario. I'd rather drink dish soap than play that ever again.

Resident Evil 2 is one of the best survival horror games ever created. Developed by Capcom, the remake perfectly modernizes the 1998 classic while keeping the terrifying atmosphere that made the original so memorable.

The game follows rookie police officer Leon S. Kennedy and college student Claire Redfield as they try to survive a zombie outbreak in Raccoon City. The story is simple but engaging, filled with tension, mystery, and emotional moments that keep players invested from beginning to end.

One of the game’s biggest strengths is its atmosphere. The dark hallways of the Raccoon City Police Department create a constant feeling of fear and suspense. Every corner feels dangerous, especially because resources like ammunition and healing items are limited. This makes every decision important and increases the survival horror experience.

The gameplay is excellent, combining exploration, puzzle-solving, and intense combat. Zombies are slow but extremely durable, making encounters stressful and realistic. Mr. X, the unstoppable tyrant that hunts the player throughout the game, adds another layer of tension and unpredictability.

Visually, the game is stunning. The graphics are incredibly detailed, with realistic lighting, gore effects, and character models that make the horror feel even more immersive. The sound design is also exceptional, using silence, footsteps, and sudden noises to keep players constantly on edge.

Although some side characters could have been developed more and the campaign is relatively short, the game remains highly replayable thanks to multiple scenarios and unlockable content.

Overall, Resident Evil 2 is a masterpiece of modern horror gaming. It successfully balances action, fear, and storytelling, making it a must-play experience for both longtime fans and newcomers to the series.

Yeah, no. This is pretty much the most overrated game in the RE saga.

Positive things:

- Character models and VA are great.
- Excellent sound effects.
- Strong character personalities and development.
- The story is decent, even though not much actually happens (and this honestly goes for all the first games).

Negative things:

- Horrible gameplay. There's no dodge or parry mechanic. It doesn't make the game harder, it just makes it more frustrating.
- I know many people are fans of the unkillable foe, I'm not. It completely breaks the game's pacing, and if you haven't completed all the puzzles before he appears, good luck. There's no way you're getting everything done once that unkillable ♥♥♥♥♥ starts chasing you.
- The crosshair mechanic is awful. The chance of landing critical hits depends on how small the crosshair is, which I think is a terrible design choice.
- Not all zombies actually die. Many people already know this and may find it interesting, but I don't. You can apparently kill zombies in a hallway, come back hours later, and find them still alive. I know there are ways to tell whether a zombie will stay dead when it drops, but that doesn't make it a good mechanic, especially when you have to use double the amount of ammos to kill 1 single zombie.

Overall, the game constantly makes you feel like ♥♥♥♥. The fact that you're often forced to leave enemies alive because of the mechanics I mentioned is just plain stupid.

The puzzles are generally good, but they could have benefited from being a bit more linear. It's easy to end up carrying too many key items for completely different puzzles at the same time, which clutters your inventory and isn't particularly good game design. Yes, you can drop them in the chest but having to do that all the time is a pain in the ass, and again it's bad game design. Don't forget about the unkillable guy, good luck putting the items there, doing the puzzles, retreaving the other items from the chest while he's following you.

I nearly finished RE4 Remake, and they got almost everything right there. It's just a shame that you have to play this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ of a game before getting to the much better one.

I'm not a RE fan, so my opinion isn't biased like that of 99% of the community. I won't call this a good game just because everyone else says it is. It's simply not, you may like it for whatever reason, I'm just glad I finished it so that I won't touch it ever again.

P.S. One more thing to add, the mods for Claire's outfits are crazy good, ngl. Absolutely try them.

Forgive me if this review is kind of messy, first serious review.
This game is absolutely a 10/10.
As my first-ever Resident Evil game I played*, I love this game. First of all, the storytelling and setting.
The setting is amazing;
Even though I read the history of Resident Evil in 5, and it got me to know what's going on, I was completely blown away. Seriously. I almost fell off my chair from the first little jumpscare.
The virus spreading around like wildfire seemed basic to me at first, but as it progressed, I started to learn more; it isn't just a zombie apocalypse, it's the end of the world threatening to happen. People are dying and turning everywhere, things are mutating... It had me squealing and whimpering in fear in the first 30 seconds of gameplay.
I've always wanted to play a survival horror game. I have zero survival horror games besides Resident Evil, and before I got the few games I did at a sale, I had never played survival horror before, and I can safely say this completely threw me off.
And speaking of gameplay;
I got the hang of things quick, which is amazing. The tutorials are clear and they pop up only when you need them to! Like for example, key items. When you try a door, you go into your inventory and search for a key item to use on the door. You simply get told key items are items that need to be used on something for your benefit. It's amazing that it doesn't just pop up right as I'm in the middle of something like surviving, and only when I don't know what to do. The guns are lovely, they're also well-balanced. (Shotgun shells are scarce but one or two shots do the job, handgun ammo is somewhat common but drains way faster, grenades aren't just one-hit kills, it's just perfectly balanced.) Healing items are scarce too, which makes you want to either use knives to counter or spend a bit too many bullets...

*(I guess I played a bit of 5, but I paid no attention to anything so 2 is my first real one.)

I really like RE4 Remake and RE: Requiem, they were both great experiences for me. But RE2 Remake is seriously overrated, and here's why:

1. Way too much backtracking. You're constantly running through the same rooms, and on a first playthrough you often have no idea where to go next. I spent half an hour wandering the police station trying to figure out how to get the jack handle part

2. The combat is really weak. Regular enemies rarely die from pistol shots — they just go down temporarily and get back up. This makes the game feel less like a shooter and more like constantly running away from zombies. It gets better toward the end once you have the shotgun and more ammo, but that's still a huge chunk of the game

3. The sewers are absolutely miserable. After the atmospheric police station, the sewer level is a massive letdown — dull environment, heavy backtracking, and a confusing maze of corridors

I get that the game wasn't designed to be an action shooter, which is exactly why it feels so much less engaging than RE4 Remake. I have zero desire to go back for achievements or a second playthrough

For $60, the amount of insane quality and polish you get here is staggering. If you like games that actually make you uncomfortable instead of just holding your hand, this is the absolute peak of the genre. Capcom took notes on everything that worked in RE7 (crafting, the weapon wheel, diegetic cassette tapes) and fixed everything that didn't.

THE CAMPAIGN STRUCTURE:
Play Leon A, and then play Claire B if you want a true challenge. It is a perfect pairing, and for the first time, the A and B scenarios feel like narrative equals. Leon is no longer a passive spectator, and Claire’s fierce, protective characterization is easily the best version of her in the franchise.

THE CLAIRE B ARSENAL:
While Leon gets standard heavy-duty firearms, Claire gets a completely unique, highly tactical loadout:

Handgun: Utilizes enhanced ammo making a brilliant return from RE7.

Grenade Launcher: Forces tactical choices between Flame and Acid rounds depending on the enemy.

Spark Shot: A unique slot-waster that deals massive damage, provided you are patient and good with your spatial positioning.

Submachine Gun: Brilliantly rebalanced from 1998—buffed because you can craft the ammo, but nerfed with a 24-round magazine and lower damage. Incredibly useful for the final G5 fight.

Minigun: The ultimate, earned payoff for the G4 fight.

THE SURVIVAL ECONOMY CLIMAX:
That sounds like a lot of firepower, but ammo is brutally scarce—you either get ammo you don't needor you craft the ammo you want yourself. I found myself completely out of ammo and heals against the G4 and G5 Birkin fights on the B Scenario. That is exactly how Survival Horror is meant to be played: you should be down on one knee, completely desperate, shooting a boss to pieces with whatever bullets you have left. This game delivers that exact tension perfectly.

THE MECHANICS & AI:

No Free Defense: Unlike RE7, there is no zero-cost block button to cheese encounters. You actually have to respect the physical space of the hallways. There are defensive items which make a return from RE1 Remake. 

The Apex Predator: Mr. X is a calculated, terrifying force of nature who chases everybody regardless of your scenario. In Leon's story he is an active antagonist. His dynamic stalking forces you to make reckless errors and ruins your perfectly planned save-room trips.

The Minor Characters: Kendo, Marvin, and Annette Birkin are completely fleshed out with devastating emotional weight. Even Ben Bertolucci is vastly improved; his pure, expressive terror perfectly establishes the stakes.

THE CUT CONTENT:
Yes, the 1998 "Zapping System" is gone, but honestly, it makes total sense. Trying to code that level of persistent state-tracking into the massive, dynamic physics ecosystem of the RE Engine would be a nightmare. The sheer quality of the standalone campaigns more than makes up for it.

Finished Leon in 5:39:00 with a B rank and a minor heart attack. Started Claire's 2nd run expecting a nightmare, but her grenade launcher turned it into an absolute breeze. 10/10, would get stalked by a giant trench-coat man again.

Resident Evil is one of my favorite franchises so far. I admire the time the horror and the action that Capcom put into it.

Resident Evil 2 Remake was a very good remake The atmosphere was claustrophobic you didn't know what was behind the corner and you were scared most of the time. They made the design of the zombies, Lickers, and all the enemies really well.

Mr. X is no different. When you hear his footsteps especially on your first run you are scared and ♥♥♥♥ your pants. I like how they made him stalk you in the RPD not like Nemesis in the Resident Evil 3 Remake.

This game overall is amazing. The DLCs are not bad but I can't say that they are some of my favorites from this franchise but I enjoyed them.

BUT this game has some flaws: the movement system. Claire and Leon move like they have cramps they move with a little jog. If they were a little faster or maybe had a dodge button or a parry this game would be better.

The bosses: I liked Mr. X's final form when he is without the trench coat and when his arm is like a weapon and William Birkin's. The evolutions were well made I liked the design of G-3.

The characters: Leon was made perfectly—a rookie that missed his first day and is new to this mess. Claire wants to find her brother because she doesn't have anyone else.

Ada a spy that tries to sell the G-Virus to the highest bidder and Sherry is a little girl whose parents are the "parents of the year" the mother is crazy and puts work before her daughter and her father evolves like a Pokémon almost every time that you kill him.

Overall, this game is amazing: 8/10.

I'm doing a 30th anniversary marathon after playing Resident Evil: Requiem which made me fall in love with the Resident Evil universe.
This was one of the titles I was most exited for! And man, was it fun! So many good scares, the atmosphere and sound design. I loved every second. Both playthroughs with Leon and Claire were a lot of fun, where Leon dominated in the first half during the RPD, and Claire once we got into the sewers and NEST. They're both amazing characters (Leon will probably forever be my fav, but Claire, holy damn, the woman she is!).

What hooked me and infected my brain positively:

-The setting of the story
-Sound design and atmosphere
-Replayability as playing with Leon or Claire
-The puzzles were engaging (still haven't solved all yet!)
-Many good scares and tension
-Different story elements depending on which character you chose
-Different weapons depending on character
-Plot twists and background information
Bonus: Ada's wet mouth (that one is for all you barking at her)


The character movement felt smooth which made the game really enjoyable to play. However, a small little thing I wish that could have been done, is that Claire and Leon interact more, and/or that their stories don't overlap where they do, like they're both fighting William. They could have given Leon a different thing to fight, like they did at the end I guess, with our beloved Mr Eggs. It worked very well in the beginning of the B scenario, which I really liked, but to the end of it, it was quite the same. Think they could or should have kept it like that. Safe to say, RE2Remake is my fav so far after RE9, but close followed by RE1R!

[h1] A Misrepresentation of Jill Valentine, and How Is This Jill Different from the One Capcom Has Kept Hidden in the Basement Since 2012? [/h1]

RE3R is a game with plenty of pros and cons, but I don’t want to go over all of that right now. First of all, I don’t want to come across as just another armchair critic who dislikes the new version simply because of a dusty layer of nostalgia—no, I want to make it clear right away that I’m a new gen fan for whom RE3R is only the second game in the entire franchise.
After getting to know the franchise better and replaying RE3R, I felt quite disappointed by what one of my favorite characters had ultimately become. Of course, both characters—Jill from the original RE3 and Jill from RE3R—are highly skilled S.T.A.R.S. operatives dealing with a viral outbreak, but these two games have completely different narrative tones: they take quite distinct approaches to her personality, emotional state, and interactions.
[h2] ★ Tone and demeanor ★ [/h2]
✦ Original RE3
The original Jill has a grounded, quietly resilient, and classic action-hero stoicism. Not surprisingly, this game has significantly more action than the previous two games combined. Given the character trends of the time, it’s no surprise that Jill is portrayed this way. Her trauma following the events in the Arkley Mountains is reflected in her transformation into a more stoic heroine who no longer needs Barry Burton by her side. This is no longer “Jill Sandwich”; we’re presented with a more seasoned operative who knows how to handle enemies like these. Clearly, the game was designed for fans who had already played the previous titles and could therefore see how Jill had grown since the events at Spencer’s mansion. Her strength is quiet, professional, and methodical. She isn't prone to outbursts; she focuses entirely on the mission of surviving and escaping. However, despite this more significant change in her personality, we can still recognize the Jill that fans have loved since RE1. She’s kind, caring, compassionate, and willing to do anything to help others who’ve gotten into trouble.
• [i] “I'm sorry about your daughter, but there isn't going to be any rescue. We have to get out of here!” [/i]
• [i] “Hey, is someone wounded back there?...Calm down. You're safe now. Everything is going to be okay.” [/i]
Despite everything, this Jill is resilient and has a strong core—no matter what happened, no matter all the obstacles and setbacks, she still continued on her path, never complaining, never losing heart. She knows she has no other choice. [i] “This is my last chance for survival... This is my last escape...” [/i]
✦ RE3R
Just like the original Jill, the Jill from the remake is a product of her time. Remake Jill is significantly more aggressive, dynamic, and outwardly defiant. She follows trends in modern female protagonists from other games, embodying what’s currently quite popular—namely, the archetype of the “strong” female heroine, the so-called “badass” with a layer of sarcasm and defiance (Leon S. Kennedy just rolled over in his grave). This Jill is not characterized by stoicism or groundedness; her trauma has taken over her personality. Jill’s response to this trauma isn’t quiet melancholy—it is fierce, raw anger. She uses sharp sarcasm and profanity as armor to mask her vulnerability, which is a fairly common trope for portraying seasoned operatives who’ve been through a lot. To many, this approach may seem more realistic, although in my opinion, it’s simply more popular, since there are many characters in the RE franchise who behave the same way.
In my point of view, this Jill lacks many of the traits that were shown in the original game. Compare her reaction to the wounded Mikhail in the original and the remake:
• [i] “Mikhail: ...My team was sent here to rescue civilians. [coughs, the camera pans down, showing that he is injured.] Jill [sarcastically]: Right. How's that going for you?” [/i]
Despite everything, Jill still agrees to work with people connected to Umbrella in order to save as many civilians as possible. Although she supposedly possesses the same kindness and desire to help everyone else, all of this is laced with a thick layer of sarcasm that stands in stark contrast to the original Jill. I want to emphasize that this is just my take on it, but I believe that sarcasm is a personality trait that doesn’t suit Jill in such large doses. We can see similar aspects of her personality even in RE1R, but it was toned down and not used in nearly every scene to show just how “badass” this character is. Even in the original, there weren’t that many cheesy lines, which... Well, says a lot.
[h2] ★ Interaction with the Nemesis ★ [/h2]
✦ Original RE3
Nemesis is an overwhelming, terrifying obstacle. Jill reacts to it with genuine horror and professional focus. Her priority is to find a way past or through it to keep moving. You can tell just how exhausted she was at the end of the game by what she says to Nemesis:
• [i] “You want S.T.A.R.S.? I'll give you S.T.A.R.S.!” [/i]
The only cheesy line we get, as if it were a reward, when we defeat Nemesis in the final stage. Nemesis truly feels like an unstoppable threat—one that has already killed one of your teammates and is now closing in on you unhindered. There’s no time to waste on idle chatter; you need to focus solely on your own survival.
✦ RE3R
Nemesis is a personal enemy she actively mocks. Jill screams insults at it, turning her fear into pure, defiant spite.
Nemesis in RE3R is a missed opportunity. It’s psychologically important how characters view their enemies, how they address them, and exactly how they react. Mr. X (RE2/RE2R) is incredibly intimidating to many players, and none of the main characters would ever dare to say things like “a b/tch who can’t even swim” or “hey, f/ckface!” on the Tyrant because it’s out of place and ruins the whole unnerving atmosphere (just to clarify: I'm not talking about the final lines; I'm only referring to how the characters treat their enemies throughout the game). If the main character isn't afraid, why should I be?
This Jill isn't afraid of Nemesis; she's annoyed by his presence and is ready to fight him no matter what.
[h2] ★ Visual Design ★ [/h2]
✦ Original RE3
A blue tube top, black miniskirt, and a white sweater wrapped around her waist. What a product of late-90s game design.
✦ RE3R
Jill's look has been completely redesigned to prioritize realism and practicality: a layered tactical tank top, utilitarian cargo pants, combat boots, and a shoulder holster.

This take might be quite provocative, but I believe that games don’t necessarily have to lean toward realism when choosing outfits for their characters. It’s just a game, and as far as I’m concerned, it doesn’t really matter what a character wears, as long as their personality and character development throughout the story are completely captivating. I have to admit that I prefer the original outfit simply because of how iconic and memorable it is, as opposed to the tank top and jeans that have become the go-to look for female characters in modern games—as if no other clothing in the world existed. However, I can understand why Jill’s look was changed—let’s be honest, female characters wouldn’t be dressed like that today to avoid causing any controversy or conflict.

[h2] ★ Conclusion ★ [/h2]
It’s important to remember that this is a remake, and a remake is definitely not a remaster, which has to stay true to everything in the original game. It was obvious that many things would be changed in the name of realism or modern trends, just as they would be in any future Capcom remake. However, for me, this will remain a disappointment, given how much my favorite heroine has been altered just to conform to modern standards. Still, all that’s left is to accept it and welcome the new Jill—the gritty, unyielding action-thriller protagonist whose survival is driven by fierce defiance and explosive grit.

Although the new dodging mechanic is buggy as ♥♥♥♥, the game is still INCREDIBLY GOOD. There’s significantly less content compared to RE2 (at least that’s how it feels after finishing it), but it’s more dynamic and interesting. In my opinion, it’s not better than RE2, but it’s not much worse either (the plot in RE3 is sometimes a bit cringeworthy; Jill would have died 6–7 times (from injuries, rocket shrapnel, etc.) if the game’s events had happened in real life). 8+/10
