Where [i]Resident Evil 2's[/i] remake became more frustrating/annoying later on into the game, [i]Resident Evil 3's[/i] remake begins rather intensively during what is basically the game's prologue/tutorial phase and this decision remains a very persistent nuisance as you play through the game, only being given short reprieve in between numerous chase sequences, although not entirely free of danger from zombies or other mutated creatures such as what lurks beneath the surface inside of the sewers. Perhaps similarly to Leon's adventure in REmake 2, most of REmake 3 is just one prolonged chase scene against a character that is equivalent to that of an actual raid boss, with all of the other beloved features that have become iconic to RE's identity such the small, oppressive inventory size that they want to stick to. Remaking these games with a proper third-person view helps immensely with the quality and fun of the game, but it wasn't hard to come to the decision that REmake 2 is better despite how both games have their flaws, some of it just innately due to Capcom's philosophy with RE as a whole. But still, I can't even fathom how Capcom could deliver on something this intense with how the video games used to be designed originally. The attention to detail is noticeable even from the first five minutes: there's a desk fan blowing in the apartment of the main protagonist and papers pinned on the board react to the wind generated by the spinning of the blades, although you'll notice a lack of bloody footprints from the characters as they walk through puddles of blood, and so it's somewhat inconsistent.

There are a few changes to REmake 3 versus REmake 2 that we can notice immediately: there is no longer three different coloration of herbs, only now green and red, which is a decent streamline as it's now one less item clogging up your inventory space, and there's also a much needed 'sidestep' ability that lets you forward/backwards/sidewards dodge zombies that lunge at you from ten feet away and still somehow grapple you, but perhaps to try and balance the game with this new mechanic, they've removed the previous ability to escape grapples before sustaining damage if you had a "sub-weapon" like a knife or grenade. If you are grappled, you are now guaranteed injured. You can also do a sort of "perfect dodge" if you time it right, which has your character rolling a good distance while the game enters into slow motion for what is like a millisecond. This dodge is important to master because, as per RE logic, zombies still take eight bullets to kill and so at least it's a bit easier to try and run by them if you are low on supplies, and to make it worse, they respawn as well where it'll even evolve at some point to be harder to kill. I also like that I don't have to press three buttons simultaneously to throw a grenade or slash with the knife, it's now part of the main weapon wheel that you can customize, and the knife no longer has that annoying durability meter that makes it completely useless (since you can't even repair it in previous titles).

Mastery of the perfect dodging mechanic also helps against one of the major antagonists to this particular title who will constantly Hulk jump from behind you and land in front of you and who can charge you like a bull from behind. Where Mr.X (Tyrant) of REmake 2 was far less of a threat, only ever stomping behind you like an angry, petulant child told to go their room and only really serving to be a device that rushes you along into the next act of the game, this new unkillable stalker in REmake 3 is far more annoying in design. I don't know if it is bugged or what, but he'll stagger you if you do miss your dodge and before your character has the time to stand back up, he'll just spam stagger you to death. It's very cheap and something that surely isn't intended or shouldn't be intended if so, as all of your agency is instantly removed and you're just helpless to continue your escape attempt. I'd surmise that this newly added dodge ability is solely because of how they designed Nemesis, as he'd be impossible to escape otherwise if you could only jog at a leisurely pace like in previous titles.

Puzzles in REmake 3 are seemingly nonexistent or just so easily solved that it seems barely a puzzle. Mostly, you're exploring red sections of the world map as this illuminates to you that you've missed items in this area that can be collected. You'll come across the usual 'key items' that are needed for backtracking such as for lockpicking containers or doors. I discovered an interesting secret where lobbing a grenade at the unkillable stalker's feet will cause him to drop boxes that can be used to upgrade Jill's starting pistol, which I thought was a cool hidden mechanic that rewards the player for using their items rather than hoarding it, and is no doubt something a lot of players will have overlooked.

REmake 3 does something cool where you get to explore the RPD station just prior to Leon or Claire's arrival in REmake 2, where you get to see how the Marvin character got bit as well as some other details like how the other police officers were killed inside the station, where they are just corpses when you see them in REmake 2. The station is still in disrepair and needs a cleaning crew, but Carlos's interactions in the station inadvertently served to inconvenience Leon and Claire once it was their turn, and I enjoyed that tie-in. It helps to answer some questions the player may have had about the situation at RPD before they arrived. It's an interesting way to handle these two video games, as Jill and Luis's journey is happening exactly the same time as that of Leon and Claire's journey but the two groups just barely miss each other despite both trying to survive the apocalypse around them. It's not exactly a prequel but more so a parallel or overlapping story where Jill and Carlos's story begins on September 28th and then you have Leon and Claire arriving a day later, making it feel concurrent and having you wonder where exactly Claire and Leon are after they blew everything up.

But beyond just reusing the same villain through numerous phases like in previous titles, the biggest disappointment of REmake 3 is in its length. The campaign here is considerably shorter than REmake 2 and that is perhaps what sets the two apart in a tier list. REmake 2 can take five to seven hours just for one character, and probably around the same time for the second character, maybe a bit quicker since you'll have become more experienced and more familiar with the map design and puzzles. And then double that if you then want to replay it again on each characters to see how the endings change. REmake 3 features the standard pattern that I'm noticing with the franchise where you have two playable characters, one male and one female, but when you begin to play as the dude, Luis, the game is basically halfway over at that point. In fact, the achievement for just sprinting through the game to beat it in less than two hours is not actually something that Capcom should consider a good thing. If you're a video game developer and a player can do something like that, you have a video game that needs a few more hours worth of content. I surmise that their attempts to add more substance to an otherwise short (and therefore overpriced) video game is to add more post-game content in the unlocking of an in-game shop where your feats awarded you with coin that can be used to buy a new outfit for Jill or more inventory space, etc., but there's really no point unless you just want to replay the game again.

It was still enjoyable, but I'd say it isn't worth full price and is better purchased on a sale.

In my playing through the franchise, this is the objective ranking of each game that I've played thus far as of this review: REmake 2 > REmake 3 > RE:1 & RE:0.

Resident Evil 3 Remake is simply a lot of fun to play. Switching between Jill and Carlos keeps things fresh, and Nemesis remains a relentless threat throughout. Revisiting the Raccoon City Police Department before Leon’s arrival is a great touch, and Raccoon City itself feels alive with chaos and destruction at every turn.

I know some people criticize the game for being too short, but that was not my experience. My first playthrough lasted around 9 hours, and the pacing never dragged. It is a focused, cinematic experience with no unnecessary filler.

Compared to Resident Evil 2 Remake, it is more linear, with less exploration and backtracking, which may disappoint some players. For me, though, that tighter structure helps maintain constant intensity. If RE2 is a marathon, RE3 is a sprint because it is fast, direct, and adrenaline-driven.

Overall, Resident Evil 3 Remake is a highly enjoyable mix of horror and action. It may not be as expansive as RE2, but it delivers a thrilling, tightly paced ride from start to finish.

Rather than replacing the slower, exploration-heavy experience of RE2, it adds a sharper, more action-driven layer to the overall Resident Evil experience.

Highly recommended.

Resident Evil 3 Remake – How to Turn a Masterpiece into a DLC

As a long-time Resident Evil fan, and someone who considers Capcom one of the best developers in the industry, this game still manages to make me irrationally angry years after release.
After the incredible Resident Evil 2 Remake, I genuinely believed Capcom couldn't possibly mess up Resident Evil 3.

They proved me wrong.

============================
Missing Content: The Real Nemesis
============================

Around half of the original game is gone.

The Clock Tower? Gone.
The Park? Gone.
The Cemetery? Gone.
The Gravedigger? Gone.
The Dead Factory? Gone.
The branching choices? Gone.
Mercenaries mode? Also gone.

I played the demo before release and absolutely loved it. I couldn't wait to explore a modern version of Raccoon City built on RE Engine.

Then the full game launched.

Turns out the city from the demo WAS the city.

Capcom finally had the opportunity to show Raccoon City descending into complete chaos on modern hardware and somehow managed to flush that opportunity straight down the toilet.

===============================
Gameplay: Somehow Worse Than RE2R
===============================

One thing I never expected was that RE3R would actually feel worse to play than RE2R.

The quick knife button from RE2R? Gone.
R1 is now mostly dedicated to switching grenade launcher ammo, which takes forever.

Speaking of grenade launchers...

In the original game, my favorite weapon was the grenade launcher with freeze rounds. Watching zombies shatter into icy chunks was incredibly satisfying.

Naturally, RE3R removed them.

Instead, we get mine rounds that are useful for approximately two boss fights.

The original ammunition crafting system, with enhanced handgun rounds, shotgun shells, and multiple ammo combinations?

Completely removed.

==================================
Nemesis: From Nightmare to Punching Bag
==================================

After seeing what Capcom did with Mr. X in RE2R, expectations for Nemesis were sky-high.

In the original game he was relentless.

In RE3R?
One grenade.
That's all it takes.

Most encounters are heavily scripted and the number of times Nemesis actually stalks you can be counted on one hand.
The flamethrower boss fight is good.
Unfortunately, it's basically the last time you'll see Nemesis in his classic form.

After that he spends most of the game cosplaying as a giant mutant dog or Birkin from OG RE2

Whose idea was that?

===================
Carlos and the Hospital
===================

Carlos was never my favorite character.
RE3R somehow made sure he stayed that way.
The hospital section feels rushed, the gunplay feels awkward, and the final defense sequence where zombies endlessly climb through windows feels like it wandered in from a completely different game.
Considering how weak the combat mechanics already are, this section becomes an endurance test rather than entertainment.

Seriously, just give the man a shotgun.

========================
Zombies Somehow Got Worse
========================

Remember the incredible dismemberment system from RE2R?
The one where zombies could lose limbs, crawl around, and react realistically to damage?

Yeah, that one stayed in RE2R.

By the time Carlos reaches the final section, zombies practically explode and despawn the moment they die.

Progress.

======
Puzzles
======

The original game didn't have many puzzles.

The remake solved this issue by removing them entirely.

==========
Brad Vickers
==========

In the original game, Brad's death was one of the most memorable moments.
Nemesis brutally executes him, instantly establishing himself as a terrifying threat.

In RE3R?

Brad gets bitten by a regular zombie near the start of the game.

That's it.

===================================
Inferno Final Boss: A Human Rights Violation
===================================

This deserves its own section.
Whoever designed the final Nemesis fight on Inferno difficulty either never tested it or is a certified masochist.

I've beaten countless games on the highest difficulty.
Souls games.
Action games.
Challenge and speed runs.

Nothing prepared me for this nonsense.
One mistake equals death.
Every attack must be perfectly dodged.
You spend more time running from the typewriter, skipping cutscenes, and loading saves than actually fighting the boss.

It's less of a boss battle and more of an anger management program.

===============
Mercenaries Mode
===============

After finishing the game, I spent about ten minutes searching through the menus looking for Mercenaries Mode.
One of the best bonus modes in classic Resident Evil series.

I never found it.

Rumor has it I'm still looking.

========
Costumes
========

The original game rewarded repeated playthroughs with multiple unlockable costumes.

RE3R gives us one.

ONE.

You can pay extra money for Jill's original outfit, which honestly should have been included from the start.

Carlos also gets a hairstyle that transforms him into a man experiencing the absolute peak of a midlife crisis.

============
The Good Stuff
============

>>Music

The soundtrack is good.
Especially the final Nemesis battle theme.

Although after dying hundreds of times on Inferno, Stockholm Syndrome may be influencing my judgment.

I’d like to have an option to play the game with sound effects and music from OG RE3 though


>>Characters

The characters are significantly more developed than in the original game. Probably thanks to cutscenes, that take 25% of your playthrough.

Jill is great, although hearing her drop an F-bomb every few minutes still feels weird.
Personally, I would have preferred Julia Voth's face model over Sasha Zotova's.

Sasha sometimes looks so much like a young Milla Jovovich that I start having flashbacks to the Paul W. S. Anderson movies.

I've never asked for this.

======
Verdict
======

Resident Evil 3 Remake isn't a terrible game.
That's actually what makes it so frustrating.
It's a decent action game.
It's a bad remake.
And it's a terrible follow-up to Resident Evil 2 Remake.

The fact that Separate Ways in Resident Evil 4 Remake feels longer than this game says everything.

If Capcom had sold this as a large RE2R expansion instead of a full-priced standalone title, the backlash would've been much smaller.
Maybe we'd even have kept the zombie dismemberment system.

I still dream about a Resident Evil 3 Definitive Edition that restores everything that was cut.
But with every passing year, that dream feels more unrealistic.

At this point, the modders are our last hope.

=============
Recommended?
=============

Only on a deep sale.
And only after you've played the original.

The red headed step child of the modern Resident Evil games. While not bad, RE3 struggles from poor enemy design, a very on rails action movie presentation, and a poor hybrid of action/survival horror. It feels like the team from the beginning set out with an amateur plan, cutting out portions of the original, dumbing down and stream lining the challenging but satisfying gameplay of RE2, and hoping action set pieces make up for the lack of depth. Overall, I would say RE3 has some great features, up to the standard set by the franchise, but also misses the mark on so many other aspects. It will always sit as a "what could have been" entry for the series.

All that being said, this is a fun and polished game. Its more than worthy of its current price tag. It just doesn't hold up when compared to the other remakes.

Despite it's short length(which stings even more due to sections being cut from the OG), this is top entertainment from start to finish. 

In terms of spectacle, Capcom captures Racoon City falling apart without leaving much to desire. In terms of atmosphere alone, this is game gets full marks. The vibrant streets of Racoon City are a stark contrast to the darker tone of RE2RE or RE4RE. The background noise, ranging from sirens, to shots being fired, screaming, etc. fully immerses you in Racoon City's final days. 

The pacing is a lot faster than the OG RE3 too. Which started after the fact, and Jill being one of the remaining people in Racoon City, after it fell. You also explored the city a bit before Nemesis showed up. The Remake however puts you right into the very moment Racoon City falls apart, and Nemesis being there right from the start. I do like this change actually, because OG RE3's start is a bit of a drag before it actually gets going. 

The fact that it is so immersive, and so impressive visually, makes it's short length even more of a let down. Same goes for the characters. The actress playing Jill, Nicole Tompkins, is absolutely nailing it, with Sasha Zotova providing the face. Again, it feels like the time is cut short for such a good performance. 

Gameplay wise, RE3 takes the franchise into a more action oriented direction. And that went for the OG RE3 as well. Something some ppl already lamented back in the day. Personally I did enjoy this direction. The gameplay is a lot faster than 2 or even 4, but it can feel kinda "arcade" to some people. Even more because RE3's dodge button is back, which allows you to avoid being grabbed when triggered at the right moment. Ammo is everywhere, enemies don't take much to kill or cripple, and you're running through the streets like Sonic through Green Hill Zone. Simply not everyone's idea of Resident Evil. 

Story wise it's all about escaping Racoon City. Nemesis shows up. You run. Nemesis shows up again. You run. Explosions happen. You run. Rinse and repeat. But its not like this game is the Mr.X section of RE2 stretched out to its own game. Nemesis shows up in specific chase sequences for the most part. 

And that's basically the game and the 5hrs you're gonna spend with it. 

I don't blame anyone giving this a bad review, if only just to send a message to Capcom regarding cut content. But what can I say, I enjoyed it from start to finish. I only wish there was more.

Resident Evil 3 is a solid survival horror game that delivers a tense and action-focused experience in the Resident Evil universe. It keeps the core elements of the series while leaning more into faster-paced gameplay compared to earlier entries.

The gameplay is smooth and responsive, with satisfying combat and a dodge mechanic that adds some depth to encounters. Managing resources still matters, which helps keep some tension even during more action-heavy moments.

Nemesis is one of the standout parts of the game. Being chased by him creates pressure and unpredictability, especially when he appears unexpectedly during exploration.

The atmosphere and setting do a good job of showing the chaos of Raccoon City during the outbreak. It feels intense and fast-moving, though it doesn’t always reach the same level of horror as other entries in the series.

Overall, Resident Evil 3 is an enjoyable but fairly short entry in the series. It has strong moments and fun gameplay, but its shorter length and lighter horror focus hold it back a bit. 

Gameplay: 7/10 Good | Overall: 7/10 Good

Having played the OG Resident Evil 3 on the PSOne, I went into the remake with high expectations, and while it does change a lot from the classic version, I still really enjoyed it. The game looks fantastic, plays smoothly, and does a great job bringing Jill Valentine’s escape from Raccoon City into a modern cinematic experience.
I played it twice, The first play through I didn't know when you take down Nemesis it drops an item🤣🤣🤣

I do wish it had included more content from the original and been a bit longer, but what’s here is polished and fun from start to finish. It may not fully capture everything that made the original special, but it’s still a very enjoyable Resident Evil game and a solid remake in its own way.

I just wrapped up 100% on Resident Evil 3 Remake, and while I definitely had a good time with it, I don't think it was quite as good as RE2.

The biggest difference is that RE3 feels a lot more like an action horror game than a survival horror game. Resources are still limited at times, but Jill being an absolute badass shows it. Instead of slowly creeping through dark hallways wondering if it's worth spending ammo on a zombie, you're usually mowing through enemies and pulling off dodges. It's still really fun, but it's a pretty different experience from RE2 despite both games taking place during the same outbreak.

One thing I wish there was more of was Raccoon City itself. The opening section is awesome and really sells the chaos of the city collapsing, but I was hoping there'd be more time spent exploring it. Compared to RE2's interconnected map, RE3 feels a lot more linear. From what I've heard, quite a bit of content from the original game was cut or changed for the remake, and honestly, it does feel like there should've been a little more here.

Then there's Nemesis...as a stalker, he's right up there with Mr. X when it comes to making me panic whenever he shows up. The dude just does not leave you alone. The difference is that Nemesis is a much bigger part of the actual gameplay, with multiple boss fights throughout the story. Thankfully, most of them are pretty fun and varied, so fighting him never really got old.

The characters were another highlight. Jill is great, Carlos ended up being way more likable than I expected, and the two of them have a really fun dynamic throughout the game. I also liked how we got to see a different side of Umbrella through some of the U.B.C.S. members. Mikhail went out like bro. RIP Tyrell too. You were the man.

Overall, I think RE3 is a good game, just not the game I was expecting after RE2. It trades a lot of the survival horror and puzzle-solving for faster-paced action, and while I personally prefer RE2's style, I still had a blast with this one.

Overall: 7.5/10 – Would ignore a perfectly good evacuation plan and get chased across the city by Nemesis again.

A lot of people complain about the run time of this game -- and to be frank, if you're looking for a longer game, then you will be disappointed. 

I'll just be to the point; this game has/had potential to be a great game, not just a good/fun experience. You can see the ideas/concepts in mind, but they don't come together like they do in Resident Evil 2 remake. Raccoon City feels vibrant but empty, the story feels choppy in spots and almost bordering the edge of too campy. It's not as scary because it feels like you'll have plenty of ammo to protect yourself. Also, Nemesis just feels more like a hindrance and less menacing.

That being said, the music is unsettling and jarring. The levels are (mostly) designed well to create that sense of claustrophobia and like you never have enough space to feel safe, and there are some certainly tense moments that can make you want to occasionally 'nope' out of this game. 

It's a good game, it just needed a little more TLC on the way before getting released.

10/10.

As someone who has never played the original Resident Evil 3: Nemesis, I had a good time with Resident Evil 3 Remake.

Writing this review after completing the game on ALL difficulties as well as all the achievements.. 

I really liked the mix of horror, action and story. 
Something I love in this one is that It is not the type of horror game where the only "horror" is some stuff popping out at your face, although it there are some monsters that sometimes just pops but the they manged to do this very well when it comes to placement and amount of them. It also depends on the difficulties. 

The game play is also great. Nice weapons. Nice movement. Nice cutscenes. 
A small detail developers: I would love to have some more shortcuts for the weapons.

I also liked the characters and how their personalities fits each of them depending on their roll in the game.  

Very good story. Nothing that touched my soul but still, realistic and nice to fallow, especially if you are a Resident Evil fan who wants to understand the story of the upcoming RE games.

It is not however a good remake. Buy it when discounted. Don't buy at full price.

As someone who has played most RE games, when people hate on this game i don't understand it. its a solid RE game that gets unnecessary hate because a lot of content from the OG was left out, but that doesn't take away from the fun and replay value this game offers. i have many hours playing this game on console and i recently bought it for my PC because i genuinely enjoyed this game. If you haven't played the OG RE3 seriously consider playing this one.

This game's more of an action movie chase akin to Terminator 2 than any real zombie survival feel. If that's what you're lookin for, congrats, this game is for you. If not, RE1, 2 and 4 are better.

This is a really short game that creates replayability by increased difficulty instead of extra routes and challenges like RE2.

I suggest only picking this up on heavy discount. If you only want to go through the story once, you'll only get maybe 6 hours of game play. I picked this up on sale for $5, and I feel that's about right for how short the game is.

As for gameplay, running from the Tyrant and having to fight it multiple times got old fast. At higher difficulties you sometimes get stun locked and killed as the Tyrant gets faster in attacks as the difficulty ramps up. It's totally cheese and lazy programing.

All this said, I do love playing this game, truth me told I play it in a different language to learn that language as I have played this so much, this is because I like jill and the characters in this game, and the dodge mechanic is really fun and I do think its slightly more polished that re2 remake despite that being the better game. Plays like the Terminator 2 turned into a videogame. A constant rush of being hunted down by the oppressive Nemesis. It was the S.T.A.R.S. of the PSX game and it's no different here.

Carlos was a wimpy idiot in the PSX game. This retcon'd version is superior in every way. Similary the weaker elements of the PSX story were cut out or cleaned up and made a lot more sense.

Only 2 complaints:

1) It's a bit short and I can see how it upset people who paid the RRP at launch.
2) It stretches the definition of an RE game a little bit.

If there was another couple of hours worth in it that were equivalent to the first section then I think it'd rate a lot higher. But as it stand's it's still a fun game.

The enemies weren't that bad either as far as being bullet sponges. My only complaint is one small boss fight later in the game where the enemies use instakill attacks randomly which feels out of place with the rest of the game and put a bad taste in my mouth. Overall a good experience though. Now, on to RE4 Remake.

I know why it is hated but still it is playable and engaging even though it’s sort of short game to play (it’s not if you play for the first time). A very short game. The dodging mechanic is neat and all but where's the cut content?

Also the inferno mode is actually bonkers.

This is a tough one to recommend, there's some things they don't tell you that make the game almost unplayable, like how to dodge. The maps in the city don't layer right making them almost useless. Ammo is terribly spars. Nemesis is unrelentless, and zombies, aside from being total bullet sponges, are very slow until you try to run by them and suddenly they're Deion Sanders. I wasn't aware of the dodge till after I was frustrated enough to switch the game to Assist mode (which you can't change back afterwards). After that it was smooth sailing and the game was actually enjoyable.

Compared to the RE2 remake this one seems to put more an emphasis on the music/atmosphere which is great. And you're outside more in the city. If you're a 40yo RE OG like me it's pretty awesome to be out in Racoon City in 3D though the game is pretty much completely reimagined. Still, it does sort of manage to maintain that classic depressive RE atmosphere and overall I ended up really liking it.

So yeah get it and play it. If you're not out to impress anyone, a non-gamer or very casual, just play on Assist mode. There's still plenty of challenge.
This game is good. It's not as spooky or tense as Resident Evil 2, it is very action packed though. Good game.

If I could vote both yes and no I would. Honest review of this after completing it once. I feel overall the story is lacking. Like you're working up to getting to the bottom of Umbrella and the outbreak in Raccoon City playing as both Jill and Carlos. But Nemesis just feels more scripted and honestly he is more of an annoyance than an actual threat. As long as you're good with resources hunting and saving along with dodging, he's not that difficult. It's not a terrible game overall. Graphically it's a beautiful, story wise it could be better and it lacks replay value like the older PS2/PS3 era games had with the Mercenaries mode in Resident Evil 4 and 5. The Shop mode when unlocked doesn't offer much, just Jill's costume from RE1, a bunch of coins that assist in health and other attributes when equipped. You can unlock Jill's Samurai Edge which is a thousand times better than the standard pistol you start with. There is also a unlimited weapons but you need a bunch of points to unlock them by doing challenges in game. Overall, if you can get this game on sale like I did, it's worth a play.

I really enjoyed this one from beginning to end.
Jill was a great protagonist, the story moved at a perfect fast pace, and the action made every moment feel exciting. The graphics and overall presentation were excellent, and seeing this classic game remade with modern visuals made the whole experience even better.

Shorter than I expected, but still a very fun and memorable experience.

[h1] I LOVE THE GAME BUT I FELT SHORT AND ROBBED [/h1]

I love the overall gameplay considering the dodging mechanics, gameplay was true to the original and the boss fights are new and exciting.

However, I was a little bit disappointed because lot of scenes are being taken away from the game, I was expecting to be somehow long like RE2R. And lot of unfamiliar routes (Though I understand capcom trying to make a new map, enemies and atmosphere but I'm sure fans would have more appreciated if they get what they already know from the original), the worm boss is also gone which is one of the iconic boss in RE3 game. 

But I can't deny that it is still a very good game, it has flaws but the gameplay definitely didn't disappoint.

Capcom, if you can read this lot of fans including me are still hoping you can still make a new proper RE3 Remake. Stay true to the original, if story has to be revised to make the story more cohesive and connected to other timeline that's fine but make the experience still similar to what we had from the Original RE3. It's not too late. 

OVERALL, I ENJOYED IT BUT I WANT MORE.
very fun to play although the story isn't much, good replayability value, get it with discount.

I enjoyed RE3 remake more than the RE2 remake. Lots of shooter action, almost no boring content(except small sewers location). The game gets criticized for a short campaign, but it seemed fine to me. In 7 hours I got my fill of the game. 7/10

a decent re game

I heard many things about the RE 3 remake and was always curious to see what I think for it myself.  I think that even though this game is good, it feels like a step back from RE 2 remake.  It is more action packed and a lot shorter than the previous game. This feels like a story DLC for 2 remake and it even reuses models and environments from it too.  Nemesis could have been done a lot better too, after what they did with Mr. X in the previous game I was excited to see what they could do with him here.  Nemesis is mostly scripted and when he does free roam to get the player, it is frustrating and the amount of trial and error is astonishing.  I recommend you get this game on sale, it feels rushed and hollow at the same time but if you are RE fan, get it.

I'm newer to RE. This remake wasn't nearly as interesting as the original, it was too easy, and the bossfights were about as lame as you could get. The very first bossfight in RE4 is better than the final boss of this one. Stick to the RE4 remake, that one is great.

The combat feels great just like the previous title, and the resource economy felt like there's enough to give you options, while still asking a bit of the player. Short, heavily cut, but decent for a single playthrough 
Pros:

The gameplay feels solid and polished for a one-time playthrough.

It's not a completely terrible experience if you catch it on a good sale.

Cons:

Short AF: The game is incredibly brief; you can finish it in just a few hours.

Scripted Nemesis: Unlike the terrifying stalker mechanics of Mr. X in RE2 Remake, the Nemesis chases here are heavily scripted set-pieces unlike og nemesis .

Cut Content: Almost 60% of the original PlayStation game's content is missing (like the Clock Tower area and choices), making the pacing feel highly inconsistent.

Final Verdict: 6/10. It is a decent one-time play, but it feels more like a rushed DLC than a full-price standalone remake. Buy it only on a deep discount.

Do not let the title fool you: Resident Evil 3 (2020) pivots hard away from the Metroidvania puzzle-box design of the RE2 Remake. It trades branching paths and exploration for a highly linear, corridor-heavy experience that feels much closer to the action-packed pacing of Resident Evil 4. It is one of the harder games in the modern engine, but it comes with a lot of structural compromises.

THE GOOD (Characters & Combat Tension):

The Protagonists: The characterization here is fantastic. Jill and Carlos are incredibly well-written. The expanded roles for minor characters like Tyrell and Mikhail are a massive highlight—Mikhail in particular is handled brilliantly, portrayed as a man who respects Jill and genuinely cares about the civilian collateral damage.

Nemesis: The boss fights against Nemesis are consistently intense. Whether it is a scripted set-piece or a dynamic encounter, he demands absolute respect and keeps the adrenaline high.

Zombie Sponginess: Surprisingly, standard zombies have significantly more HP this time around. This actually enhances the survival horror aspect slightly compared to RE2R, forcing you to really commit to taking a target down or burning resources.

THE MIXED (Mechanics & Combat Loop):

The Knife Economy: The knife was tweaked from RE2R. It is now infinite (a massive buff), but it takes up a permanent inventory slot and does less damage. I found myself using it to down enemies constantly, which ironically made me ignore the new dodge mechanic.

The Dodge: The dodge mechanic is back and incredibly responsive. It works exactly as intended, even if you can largely get by just using knife-downs.

Lazy Defense: The brilliant sub-weapon defensive items (shoving grenades/knives into grab attacks) from RE2R are gone. They were replaced by button-mashing QTEs, which feels like a massive, lazy step backward for the combat mechanics.

THE UGLY (Cut Content & Puzzles):

The Chopping Block: The biggest sin of this remake is the cut content. The iconic Clock Tower is reduced to a background skybox, the Gravedigger boss is completely missing, and the game ends in another NEST facility, which feels highly repetitive.

Nikolai's Rewrite: The voice performance for Nikolai is great, but his narrative rewrite is weak. Changing his motivation from pure corporate greed to being a vague "grunt for a rival company" that is never fully explained robs him of his agency as a villain.

Zero Brain Power: The original RE3 had some genuinely challenging puzzles. This remake has exactly one. It completely abandons the puzzle-solving core of the franchise.

VERDICT:
It is a fine, high-octane game, but it is incredibly short and suffers from annoying cut content. Structurally and chronologically, this feels like it should have been packaged as a $30 standalone DLC for the RE2 Remake rather than a full-priced release. Catch it on a deep sale.

The black sheep of the remakes. However, don't let that foreboding title turn you away from Resident Evil 3 Remake. This was the second Resident Evil game I had ever played (coming from RE2R) and I had a ton of fun on my first playthrough, probably the most fun I've had while playing a Resident Evil game. Nemesis was terrifying during his encounters. I really enjoyed playing as Jill and Carlos. Running around Raccoon City and figuring out how to progress was tedious, but it paid off. Of course, now that I've played RE3 OG and seen what we could have had... I must say that it's a huge let down. RE3R is an insanely short game compared to its sister game, RE2R, and even it's original. It cut recognizable locations like the Clocktower and the Park, and it really lacks a lot of substance. One of my least favorite aspects of RE3R is the fact that they try so hard to push Nikolai as the "real" bad guy opposed to Nemesis, the bio-engineered super soldier that was sent to kill you. The handling of Nemesis overall was poor. The game immediately strips him of any inkling that he is highly intelligent by turning him into a dog beast, and then a literal mush monster by the end of the game. RE3R had a lot of potential, and unfortunately I don't think we'll see a proper remake of RE3 for a long time.

Resident Evil 3 Remake is a good game. The problem is that it is also a remake of Resident Evil 3: Nemesis.

The original was the first horror game I ever played and I finished it so many times that I could probably still speedrun parts of it today. That obviously comes with expectations.

The game starts incredibly strong. Nemesis feels great. The boss fights are fun and they captured his persistence surprisingly well. The guy simply refuses to accept that I have other places to be.

Unfortunately, the further the game goes, the more obvious it becomes how much content is missing. Entire areas are gone. Enemy types are gone. The giant worm is gone, which still feels like a missed opportunity because that could have been a spectacular fight in the remake.

One thing I did like was the expanded hospital section. Carlos gets a lot more screen time and I enjoyed that change. To be honest, the hospital was never one of my favorite parts of the original anyway.

That said, Jill and Carlos never felt quite right to me. Not bad, just different. Maybe I have played the original too many times, but something felt off.

Story-wise, it is pretty much what I expected, minus all the missing parts. The graphics look great and the gunplay is excellent as usual. I also really liked the new evade mechanics, although they make the game noticeably easier once you get the timing down.

On my first playthrough, I played on the standard difficulty and kept saving ammunition because I expected the game to be longer and tougher than it actually was. By the end, I had so much ammo left that I could have probably finished the game a second time.

One thing that stood out in a bad way were distant zombies. On maximum settings they sometimes looked like they were animated in stop motion, which was a bit distracting.

My biggest issue is probably the length. I completed every challenge, unlocked every achievement and basically did everything the game had to offer in less than 20 hours.

Resident Evil 3 Remake is not a bad game. If it had been a completely new Resident Evil title, I would probably like it a lot more. As a remake of Nemesis though, it is hard not to think about everything that could have been.
The game is actually good, but unfortunately a lot of content was cut, so the playthrough isn’t as long as expected. Still, I’d recommend it.

I paid £3.49 for this game on Steam and it's been a few years since I've last played RE3R on my PS5. I have to say, for £3.49, this game has a great 1:1 pound to hour ratio and I would highly recommend it at that sale price.

On that note, this game is far too short and feels far too DLC to even consider to be a full video game especially with the cut content from the original. It took me 3:05 hours to complete not including the cutscenes and that is literally at the most normal speed imaginable (but admittedly I HAVE played this game before but that's beside the point). Any experienced gamer will get through this game in very short time and that is the exact reason why you should only ever buy it on a 85%+ sale. It is however a very fun game with rapid pacing and some very cinematic moments that are simply cool, as well as some pretty good horror moments that take notes from RE2R.

If you enjoy RE2R, this will likely disappoint as a sequel but if you think of it as a hidden DLC instead - you'll probably feel very satisfied.

Honestly, I liked this game more than RE2R. It still has many of the same flaws, but at least they finally added a dodge mechanic. The dodge itself is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ though. With the camera being so close to the character, it feels clunky to use. Still, it's better than not having a dodge mechanic at all.

There are also a couple of areas where I think RE3R improves on RE2R. The reduced focus on puzzles made the pacing much smoother for me. I like puzzles in games, don't get me wrong, but RE2R crossed the line from engaging to tedious. RE3R cuts them entirely which is a shame but between not having them and having too many of them, I'd pick the 1st option. 

As for the stalker guy, I have to admit Nemesis is clunkier than Mr. X when it comes to avoiding his attacks, but I personally enjoyed Nemesis more, felt like the devs gave him some character in a way than just simple "aura" as they did for Mr. X, even though I have to admit that they could have reduced the amount of boss battles with Nemesis. They should have added another mini boss instead.

I won't be writing a longer review like I did for RE2R, so here's the problem in a few words: they wanted to make a horror game, but they implemented the action elements very poorly.

Still, if on sale, buy it, it's worth the 8 euros.

RE3 has become one of my more favorite RE games. I really enjoyed the faster-paced action horror side of resident evil. The visuals are very near photo realistic, and a crumbling racoon city is at its best in this game. Compared to RE2 where you're confined to the interiors of the iconic RCPD station. As Jill, you're outside, trying to stay alive in the different backdrops of the city, feeling like you're apart of the chaos of a falling Racoon city. Combat and movement feels a lot more fluid compared to RE2 remake, like the perfect polishing on an already great system. Dodging, No knife durability, an enhanced tyrant, as well as the levels' designs were all great additions that gave RE3 its on distant identity apart from RE2.

Jill Valentine is my favorite protagonist, Carlos is my favorite side character of the whole franchise. Jill is equally badass as she is endearing, and she's determined as hell. I loved following her lore as one of the few remaining STARS left investigating and dedicated to bringing down Umbrella. Carlos is the perfect foil to Jill, as a UBCS, Carlos finds himself in internal conflict as he's introduced to Jill, who is pessimistic about the motivations of Umbrella, and as they are forced into working together, they become an unstoppable dynamic duo who play very well off each other.

Nemesis is a big highlight, though my favorite pursier type is still Mr. X, My favorite boss battles easily go to Nemesis. His presence still adds pressure and instantly elevate the stakes. The boss battles are some of the coolest action set pieces and most outlandish spectacles I've ever had the pleasure to experience. Definitely memorable.

That said, the game's biggest weakness is its length. If RE2 was overbearing in it's length, then RE3 is just too rushed. From what I understand, there was a lot of cut content from the OG game that never made it to the remake. Despite never have played that game, I really do feel it as there are moments where there are more questions than answers, and levels that felt a bit rushed/streamlined.

My favorite of the RE: engine game so far, but would've been perfect if there was more time given to the story to be fleshed out

This is my first Resident Evil game, my only prior exposure to the franchise being the first movie featuring Milla Jovovich, which I liked but didn't like enough to watch all the movies.

But this game is simply incredible. Being a newcomer, I can't compare it to the other RE games, and for those who say this game is short, it certainly is, even I managed to beat it under 7 hours. But personally, being short is not a bad thing at all, in fact, I would hope all games were this short, though only if they know what they're doing like RE3.

So structure-wise, this game is like three movies, each complete with mini-acts, which makes the whole experience extremely enjoyable. One would think, before one jumps into the series that all the complex area exploration and survival horror would make the game, if not dull, moody and slow to say the least, but nothing could be further from the truth. The game is like a roller coaster ride, thanks to well-designed areas and sequences inserted at just the right moments that really spiced things up.

While the complexity of the areas certainly means this is hardly a linear game, it still managed to be extremely cinematic, thanks to the points I mentioned above. It is overall simply an incredibly tight, tense, fun and enjoyable experience.

Only buy this game when it's on sale. The amount of content in the game is not worth 40 dollars. In comparison, RE2 remake is also 40 dollars yet it took me 13 extra hours to beat it. Besides the price and shortness of the game, it is good. A lot of OG Resident Evil fans are upset that the chase scenes are limited because in the original Nemesis would show up at random. However, I don't like being chased therefore I like this mechanic. I wish the game was fully remade for RE3 like how RE2 was. It's a fun game, but only worth it when it costs less.

Definitely preferred RE2. RE3 seemed to emphasize combat more with the dodge mechanic which I liked for boss fights but not so much for standard zombies. I feel they really ramped up the difficulty to compensate for this mechanic. There's also a bunch of stronger, but not boss enemies in this game that are frankly annoying as hell with one-shot potential. Didn't expect to get hit by a 3-hit combo in a Resident Evil game. 

Anyways, I think this focus on combat isn't horrible, but it's not for me (and mind you, I love action games). The tension from running out of resources and strategically thinking about how to proceed was fun in RE2. I went into this game with a strategy I used in RE2: try to rush past zombies rather than kill them to save ammo. Went terribly, as you can see from my playtime. The game gives you a ton of ammo compared to RE2, and I did so much better after I realized this. I think it would've been interesting if the Carlos sections were more combat-focused while Jill sections played more like RE2. 

The other aspects of the game were alright, pretty standard RE stuff, and I'm sure many already heard the issues around the game saying it's a bad remake and all that. But overall, not a bad deal if you can buy it for 5-10 dollars.

Shorter than the other RE remakes, but about the same length as the classic RE3. They've refined the basic gameplay formula with this one. It plays excellently.

Some other reviews complain that they cut locations & puzzles from the classic game. IMO, for each location and puzzle they cut, they replaced it with something equally as good if not better. So it doesn't really feel like they cut things for budget, but, rather, that they refined and tweaked the flow of the overall game to better match the narrative they were trying to tell. Expanding in some areas (more detailed hospital to sell the idea of a "ground zero"), and cutting areas that didn't feed that narrative in the original game (the park).

That said, I do love that they still kept references to all those locations in-game, and still followed the basic skeleton of the original story. There are several locations in this new game that have almost exactly the same layout as in the original (usually areas that you pass through quickly, so you'd miss it if you didn't know what to look for).

One complaint: they don't do as good a job with Nemesis as in the original. While the big set-pieces are great, the aspect of Nemesis I loved about the original is that he could appear almost at random while you were out exploring, forcing you to think about the layout of the map and how to get to your destination while avoiding him. Also, the loot he drops for optionally taking him down isn't as interesting as in the original game. Which is a shame because the game does have some interesting items it could drop, but they're actually part of the unlockable point system, rather than part of the core game. I'd have loved if knocking out Nemesis a third time gave you the Samurai Edge pistol (a recreation of the M92F from the classic game), or Jill's RE1 costume, rather than having to unlock those via the points system.

It is not worth it at full price. I would say that there is enough replay value due to the other difficulties you can play. I'm currently trying to go for an S Rank run on all difficulties... and yes, including Inferno.

In addition, it is quite an efficient game to 100% because the game is incredibly short. Other than that, this game is mostly just fine as a Resident Evil game. The game still offers challenging gameplay in some areas, mostly through resource management. Figuring out how much ammo you need to save, which enemies to avoid, and how to stock up efficiently so you do not end up soft-locking yourself at a boss fight can be quite engaging.

The dialogue in this game is bad to an extent, but I have come to accept that Resident Evil has always had its fair share of cliché and cheesy dialogue. Funny enough, one of my favourite games of all time is Resident Evil 4 Remake, so I can't say much lol.

The game's narrative is okay. It is mostly just chase scene after chase scene after chase scene. However, I believe the original also focused on that element, but with better intention. It made Nemesis an actual terrifying villain rather than just some annoying foe you have to deal with to progress through the game.

The game still explores themes of corporate greed, resilience, and survival against inevitability. It is about dealing with problems that seem impossible—not necessarily defeating them, but overcoming them and learning how to face the world's larger issues. The game also masterfully (kind of) transitions from urban and community-centred areas to a large industrial complex. It creates a contrast between the human world and the artificial world.

Homes are replaced by laboratories. Communities are replaced by corporations working together not just for survival, but for financial gain through human exploitation. Nature has been turned into a testing ground for Umbrella, and humanity itself can now be viewed as a bioweapon due to the large-scale weaponisation seen throughout the Resident Evil series. An example of this is Nemesis, who was created as a weapon to hunt down the remaining S.T.A.R.S. members.

Yet overall, the game is just fine. I have not played the original, but I can say that this remake is simply okay on the surface. Sure, I had fun, and I do not mind replaying it to earn all the achievements and beat every difficulty. However, if you are looking for a true Resident Evil remake experience, play Resident Evil 2 or Resident Evil 4. Those remakes are far better than this one.

Final Score: 6/10

Resident Evil 3 is a thrilling action-horror experience that successfully reimagines a classic game while delivering fast-paced gameplay and memorable set pieces. Playing as Jill Valentine as she struggles to escape the doomed Raccoon City is both exciting and tense, especially thanks to the constant threat of Nemesis, one of the series' most iconic monsters.

The game's visuals are excellent, with detailed environments, impressive character models, and atmospheric lighting that bring the city to life. Jill is a strong and likable protagonist, and her interactions with Carlos Oliveira add personality and heart to the story. The voice acting and cinematic presentation help make the narrative engaging from beginning to end.

Combat feels smooth and responsive, with the dodge mechanic adding a satisfying layer of skill that rewards good timing. Resource management remains important, but the game leans more heavily into action than some other entries in the series. The hospital section, in particular, stands out as one of the strongest and most intense parts of the game.

While some fans may be disappointed that certain locations and features from the original game were cut or shortened, the remake still offers an enjoyable and polished experience. Its shorter length encourages multiple playthroughs, and the higher difficulty modes provide a rewarding challenge for players looking to master the game.

Overall, Resident Evil 3 is a fun and exciting entry in the franchise. It may not reach the same heights as Resident Evil 2 Remake, but it delivers strong characters, excellent gameplay, and plenty of memorable moments. For both newcomers and longtime fans, it is well worth playing.

After earning the Platinum Trophy and completing every possible challenge, the one thing that truly stands out is how well Nemesis was designed. With modern-day graphics, Nemesis looks absolutely phenomenal.
The story is, of course, the same as in the original Resident Evil 3, but the remake removed many of the memorable and sentimental moments that made the original so special. Even the scenes and elements that were kept and reworked barely resemble their counterparts from the original game. Some locations are so heavily redesigned that you wouldn't even recognize them unless you stopped and took a careful look around.
Although this is considered one of the weaker remakes in the Resident Evil series, that doesn't mean it's a bad game. It simply could have been handled much better in a way that would have reminded longtime fans of the original of their childhood memories and all the great moments they experienced while playing it.
Capcom really shows no mercy when it comes to the Platinum Trophy, as the achievements in this game are extremely demanding. You have to replay the game many times to unlock everything.
A new difficulty level is unlocked with each completed playthrough, and you need to finish the game on all difficulty levels to earn the Platinum Trophy.
Fortunately, the game can be completed in about two hours—or even less—once you've played through it a few times.

Score: 6.5/10

Resident Evil 3 had a lot of potential, but unfortunately it feels disappointing compared to other games in the series. Developed by Capcom, the remake looks visually impressive, yet it lacks the depth and tension that made previous Resident Evil titles so memorable.

One of the biggest problems is the game’s length. The campaign is extremely short and can easily be completed in only a few hours. Many important areas and moments from the original 1999 version were removed, which made longtime fans feel that the remake was rushed and incomplete.

The horror atmosphere is also weaker than expected. Unlike Resident Evil 2, which focused heavily on survival and tension, this game leans too much into action. Ammunition and resources are more abundant, reducing the feeling of fear and desperation that defines survival horror.

Nemesis, who should have been the most terrifying part of the game, is another disappointment. While he looks intimidating, most of his appearances are heavily scripted and predictable. He never creates the same constant pressure and fear that Mr. X created in Resident Evil 2.

The gameplay itself is still fun, but it feels too linear and simplified. Exploration is limited, puzzles are easy, and many sections feel more like an action movie than a horror experience. Even though the graphics and character models are excellent, visuals alone cannot compensate for the lack of content and replay value.

Overall, Resident Evil 3 is not a terrible game, but it feels like a missed opportunity. It delivers solid action and good presentation, yet fails to capture the depth, tension, and survival horror magic that fans expected from a Resident Evil remake.

This is a remake of Resident Evil 3- 1999, unlike the other remakes in the series it is not fantastic.

RE3 remake takes the bones that RE2 remake built and expands upon them, the same combat and ammo system returns with an added dodge mechanic from the original. As well as adding the shop to the series, where by completing challenges you get points to spend on bonus items. Save enough you can even get the infinite rocket launcher early.

The story is solidly done when adapting the original, many of the characters are well acted and voiced. Jill and Nikolai in particular were the greatest standouts, I would say Nikolai is better overall than the original.

Aside from the sheer amount cut content the biggest problem by far is Nemesis himself. In the original game he could appear out of nowhere and follow you consistently, he was also extremely difficult to take down. In this game he appears only at scripted moments, he is easy to take down with a single grenade and he is also the only boss fight in the entire game. I hope you like fighting him the first time, cause there's four more of them!

Also the difficulties are not balanced at all, they give you the magnum early on harder difficulties cause they know how ridiculous it is. There is an achievement for beating the hardest difficulty, where the hardest point is the final boss that demand literal frame perfect inputs or you get insta killed.

This also takes the cake as one of the shortest games in the series, even shorter than the original RE1. The game can be completed within an hour and a half if you do it right.

In 13 hours, I controlled Jill, wearing a strapless top, rolling stylishly through the fire-ravaged streets of Raccoon City countless times, and blasted pursuers to pieces with an infinite rocket launcher. 
Resident Evil 3 Remake is a game with "very long strengths but a very small base." It has the series' most satisfying action mechanics and top-notch audiovisual performance, yet falls into an awkward position: it's too short, too linear, and too unlike a full-fledged mainline Resident Evil game. It feels more like a large, high-definition DLC, or a "fun little game" released by Capcom to maintain the IP's popularity. 

If you get it at a "90%/80% off" discount (tens of dollars), it's absolutely a worthwhile weekend popcorn experience; but if, like me, you bought it at full price at launch with high expectations, you'll most likely finish it and look at empty streets, cursing, "Capcom, give me a refund!"

Biggest disappointment in the history of remakes.

★★★★☆ — “First time meeting Nemesis… and it turns out he’s DLC‑sized too.”

Playing the Resident Evil 3 Remake for the first time — without ever having owned a PS2 and therefore never having met the original Nemesis — was a genuinely cool experience. The atmosphere is great, the graphics look fantastic, and Jill carries the whole thing with the confidence of someone who refuses to let Raccoon City ruin her hair day.

But let’s be honest: this remake feels less like a full standalone entry and more like an action‑heavy expansion pack to the Resident Evil 2 Remake. It’s streamlined, fast, and fun… but also noticeably trimmed down. Areas are smaller, pacing is tighter, and the whole game moves like Capcom was speedrunning their own story. Nemesis is intimidating, sure, but he shows up with the energy of a guy who has a bus to catch.

Still, the presentation is top‑tier. The lighting, the city chaos, the creature designs — all excellent. The story is decent, the action hits hard, and the whole thing plays smoothly enough that I never felt bored, just occasionally wishing there was more of it.

A great ride, just a short one.

4/5 — A stylish, punchy, slightly too‑trimmed companion to the RE2 Remake.

[h1]Resident Evil 3 (2020)[/h1]

[h2]Graphics:[/h2]
☑️ Beautiful
☐ Good
☐ Decent
☐ Bad
☐ Don't look too long at it

Everything is great: textures, lighting, zombie and character models, animations, location design — no complaints, pure visual enjoyment. I especially want to point out the attention to detail, like the damage system, where zombies show injuries that match the type of damage actually dealt, instead of generic patterns. The regeneration animation on the white zombies looks impressively cinematic.

[h2]Gameplay:[/h2]
☐ Very good
☑️ Good
☐ It's just gameplay
☐ Mehh
☐ Just don't

Despite the general "sameness" — similar, sometimes identical locations, fully identical mechanics — the game doesn't feel like an expansion or a clone of RE2. It has its own identity, thanks to its pacing and characters. It feels especially fresh in contrast to its predecessor: not better, just different. There's more shooting, and accordingly, more resources. The horror element is noticeably reduced, since the main characters themselves pose more of a threat to enemies, and you can feel that. There are no puzzles to slow down the pace, but backtracking is still present — despite the limited set and size of locations, running back and forth through them never gets boring, because each trip comes with something new: a new tool that opens up new options, a special monster, or a tense chase from a pursuer.

Nemesis deserves a special mention — a modified version of the Tyrant. It's no longer a slow pursuer, but a fast and extremely dangerous hunting machine. The balance between the threat it poses and your ability to handle it is handled really well.

RE3 sacrifices both the horror and survival elements for the sake of pacing, but it does so smartly, gaining a new identity in the process — now the player isn't just running away, but chasing something too.

[h2]Story:[/h2]
☐ Lovely
☐ Good
☑️ Average
☑️ Some lore
☐ No Story

A typical action movie plot: unkillable main characters, soldiers facing off against zombies and monsters while trying to get to the truth. That's not a compliment, but it's not an insult either. A story like this fully does its job of holding your attention, and you even get attached to the charismatic characters — or, on the flip side, feel genuine disgust toward them. The game also doesn't forget it's part of the RE series, throwing in more information about Umbrella and the viruses. A great entry in the franchise.

[h2]Audio:[/h2]
☐ Eargasm
☑️ Good
☐ Just Ok
☐ Bad

Great sound design: sharp gunfire, distinct running/walking sounds across different surfaces, zombie growls and monster roars, well-cast character voices that bring out their personalities. One of the few games I deliberately played with headphones just to enjoy the sound. I can't really judge the music — nothing stuck with me.

[h2]Difficulty:[/h2]
☑️ Easy
☐ Easy to learn / Hard to master
☐ Difficult
☐ Dark Souls

I played on medium difficulty, and the biggest challenge was the combination of camera and controls. Ammo and healing items are always plentiful if you actually explore the locations instead of just running through them — by the end, I even had a surplus. Big respect for the flexible save system: I saved almost every minute, which made me feel comfortable.

But the camera and the "side dodge" move are a nightmare, especially in big boss fights. Key attack moments often don't fit on screen, or the opposite — your character can fall out of view, and turning the camera to get a better look is far from convenient. The game is technically third-person, but at times it feels worse than first-person.

The biggest difficulty, though, is the sluggish, stiff, almost deliberately slowed-down movement of the main characters. Both Carlos and Jill, despite being elite special forces operatives, would lose a race against a zombie with rotting legs. Still, you can get used to that quirk. The side dodge as the only way to avoid attacks, though, is a mystery I never solved: it seems to have an insane delay both before the dodge animation starts and before you regain control of your character afterward (at least, that's how it feels). As a result, doing a dodge makes you look like you're slowing down — and you're also stuck, since you can't cancel it — while enemies around you keep moving at full speed. That caused some serious frustration and was the only real cause of my deaths.

[h2]Game Time:[/h2]
☑️ Long enough for a cup of coffee
☐ Short
☐ Average
☐ Long
☐ To infinity and beyond

6 hours, 20/32 achievements. The game feels like a good action movie: zero filler, maximum action.

[h1]Overall:[/h1]
Score: 8/10 — visually, audio-wise, and technically, an excellent action shooter for a few evenings. The game knows exactly what it wants to be, and nails it.

So I'd heard this remake was horrible and shouldn't exist and the original was superior so since I was on my Resident Evil journey playing them for the first time I decided to play the original RE3 first. The major complaints I'd heard was the remake being much more linear and Nemesis being more scripted than his original counterpart and yeah I see the linearity argument, but nemesis felt pretty scripted in the original to where I was guessing (correctly most of the time) when he'd show up chase me for a few rooms and then seemingly vanish. My main complaint with the remake is that it's too short at just under 6 hours for my first playthrough for full price unless you're going for 100% which will involve replaying the campaign a lot in which case you'll probably get your hours worth even then I still kind of doubt it. The camera also will get you killed in some boss fights so it's best to accept it and move on. Aside from that Jill and Carlos are a likeable duo, resource management was always tense in my first playthrough, and the action packed gameplay was well suited for the level design.  All in all I do recommend the game. I had fun clearing through it in a week and may go for an S rank before moving on to RE4, but buy it on sale if you're going to get it. $40 US is highway robbery!

I don't mind perfect dodge/counter timing mechanics if it's well-implemented, but this isn't it. You have to practice and reload the game a lot to get the hang of it for each different enemy, and it gets in the way of the survival horror aspect of it. For regular zombies it's mostly telegraphed enough, but for other faster/poorly telegraphed attacks it's just frustrating.

Nemesis is more annoying than Mr. X, and is significantly less cheese-able. Where Mr. X might be a nuisance and make you loop in a different direction to get to your destination, early Nemesis will insist upon himself by closing gaps of any distance and make the dodging mechanic mandatory. With Nemesis being able to suddenly close distance and attack at any given moment, you are obligated to keep your camera on him behind you while you blindly run forward, which is not well thought-out as gameplay goes. Nemesis is also less a stalker enemy and more of an obstacle in action set pieces; an obstacle that can be skipped at the cost of a grenade. From easy to hardcore Nemesis generally gets easier the more stages of evolution he goes through, which is increasingly underwhelming. On Nightmare difficulty though, stage 4 nemesis is a cheap stunlocking sh!tshow that will combo you to death and send you back to the beginning of the fight if you miss perfect dodging almost any attack, and be increasingly annoyed at the repeated parts of unskippable animation with the railgun. Is the game free of cheese though? Not really, the RE2r zombie jukes still works just fine, and enemies still have doorway/stairway thresholds from which they will de-aggro and return to positions.

Route choices from the original RE3 is removed, which compounds with the game not having that much content to begin with (2-4h), and part of it is reused from RE2. Plus, most characters including Nemesis seems poorly implemented compared to the original.

If you want to play an RE game, there are better options. Don't buy this one unless heavily discounted.

[b][i]  Another flawless 100% completion ribbon added to the collection. While it takes a faster, more action-heavy route compared to the slow-burn survival roots of RE2, the adrenaline of a collapsing city never lets you catch your breath. Managing your resources is still crucial, but here, the tension comes from the sheer relentlessness of the environment.

The dark, chaotic streets of Raccoon City feel alive with terror, and the dodge mechanic becomes your best friend when you're cornered by a horde with a clicking, empty gun. Nemesis is an absolute force of nature—being chased by a towering bioweapon that can crash through walls at any second turns every dark alley into a high-stakes panic attack. Jill Valentine is an absolute badass, and blasting through the chaos to master every difficulty and record felt incredibly satisfying. Short, explosive, and rewarding to fully conquer.

This game is like Pokémon. You have to beat The Elite Four and then the Champion Mother Miranda.

For me Village feels like a proper AAA High Budget game and I can see the attention to detail in every area. It gives the true vibes of European horror. I loved my first run of it and it is extremely fun, but flawed. Its strengths are in its setting and characters, but the logic linking these strengths together is shoddy at best.

In other words, it feels a lot like a grab bag of random sh*t thrown into pieces of different Resident Evil games. Vampires, werewolves, abandoned village. Why not? Castle Dimitrescu is visually stunning and hosts the best characters in the game, but it is deceivingly linear and simplistic. Beneviento is horrifying on the first playthrough, but it loses all tension and becomes utterly flaccid after that. Moreau's swamp is just one long-ish action set piece followed by a decent boss fight. Heisenberg's domain is actually the most dense in the game, and features the most classic RE design in the game IMO, but it's set amidst a backdrop that feels dreary and difficult to navigate, bringing this section down a lot.

Gameplay felt stacked against itself too. RE7 brought in a lot of elements that allowed the series combat to return to a more classic sensibility, while still feeling modern. Importantly, combat was discouraged in RE7. If you can evade enemies, you should. RE8 brings over some of these design sensibilities, like having to plant yourself and aim to narrow your reticle and increase your critical hit chance. Yet, like RE4, RE8 centers around a more combat-focused design. Arenas are more wide open, filled with nimble enemies. Killing them awards you currency you can use to upgrade your kit. And yet base traits like slow movement speed, claustrophobic FOV, and that damn planting yourself mechanic are still in the game.

So even though the game's design feels like it is trying so hard to get you to enjoy the combat, a la RE4, the actual mechanical aspects of combat design in this game remain unpleasant and feel like they discourage combat. In simpler terms: The game feels like it wants you to stop and fight whenever you can, but the combat just isn't that fun. That being said, its art design (and graphical fidelity overall) is absolutely gorgeous & the gameplay itself is really good. The first time experiencing Beneviento's house is some of the most unnerving sh*t that RE's had in years. The Duke is a pretty likable iteration of the Merchant and in general I enjoyed the influence RE4 had on this game. I really liked the shift into more industrial body horror in Heisenberg's section. Enemies were all (minus the Samca) fun to fight, with Lycans being the highlight. 

Nonetheless, I still rate this game highly. It was an interesting experiment that I don't think paid off super well, but it was worth exploring. It gave us some iconic locales and villains in the series, and still manages to be an entertaining if mediocre action romp. I would've preferred a game set entirely in Dimitrescu's castle that was designed with the horror of RE7 in mind, but I can't really complain about what we got. What's there left to say? Lady D is hot, the baby part is the scariest thing and the factory goes on a little too long. Overall, it's an 7.5/10 for me

This game has confidence.

Not the fake kind. Not the kind where a horror game jumps out from behind a door every five minutes and expects applause.

Real confidence.

The kind that looks you in the eye, smiles, and knows you're walking deeper into the nightmare willingly.

I respect that.

The village is perfect. Cold. Isolated. Rotten all the way to its foundations. Every building feels like it survived something horrible and is patiently waiting for the next disaster to arrive.

Then you do.

And suddenly every monster, every cultist, every grotesque thing hiding in the dark gets the opportunity to make the worst decision of its life.

The Lords are incredible.

They don't feel like bosses.

They feel like rulers of their own little worlds, each convinced they're untouchable. Each convinced they're the most terrifying thing you'll encounter.

I enjoyed proving otherwise.

Lady Dimitrescu towering over everyone in her castle, acting like fear itself should be enough to stop you.

Heisenberg throwing metal and madness around like a man who mistook insanity for strategy.

The entire village packed with creatures that look like nature finally gave up trying to make sense.

Beautiful.

And Ethan...

I love Ethan.

Not because he's fearless.

Because he keeps going anyway.

Every normal person would have turned around hours ago. Every sane person would have looked at the first monster and decided this wasn't their problem.

Ethan just keeps walking.

There's something deeply entertaining about that level of stubbornness.

The atmosphere is what really makes the game special though.

You never feel safe.

Even when you're winning.

Even when you're armed.

Even when you've cleared an entire area.

The game always leaves this tiny voice in the back of your head asking:

"What if something worse is waiting?"

Usually it is.

That's the magic.

Every time you think you've reached the bottom of the nightmare, Village opens another door and reveals an even deeper layer of insanity hiding underneath.

By the end, I wasn't scared.

I was impressed.

Elegant madness. Beautiful horror.

A masterpiece that understands monsters aren't frightening because they're stronger than you.

They're frightening because they still think they have a chance.

RE Village is a really decent game. I played it again after recently finishing the new RE9: Requiem, and gotta say I think in some aspects in comparison, this is a better game: weapon customization, level design, and the puzzles overall are better here than RE9. Having said that, I have a huge gripe with Ethan as the main protagonist. To put it shortly, and without spoiling anything, he is no classic RE protagonist. He doesn't hav the charisma of Chris, the coolness of Leon, the charm of Jill, or the attraction of Claire. For me, this alone brings the game a bit lower than it probably deserves in my personal RE ranking. But that's also a matter of taste. All in all, I definitely recommend it for a playthrough or two, and it hits almost all the other marks as a solid RE game.

This was a huge missed opportunity to make a franchise defining trilogy. RE7 ended with Eveline, a child* raised as a bioweapon who desperately wanted a family. RE8 introduced Rose, another mold child who grew up with the loving family Eveline never had.  They are mirror simultaneously sharing a unique understanding of what its like to be part of the mold that they alone share.  A final game exploring that contrast where Eveline resents Rose for something neither of them had any control over is narrative gold. 

Instead, Capcom apparently decided that what RE7, the game that saved the franchise, was really missing was vampires, werewolves, giant zombie dragons, mech fights and airstrikes, as if the creative lead suffered an aneurysm while reviewing player survey data from five-year-olds.
