[h1] Why you should or should not play Resident Evil Village [/h1]

[b] What is Resident Evil Village? [/b]

In this game you play as our friend Ethan Winters as we traverse this complex world where nothing is really as it seems. 

[b] Quick Pros [/b]

✔ Satisfying progression system
✔ Unique mechanics
✔ Easy to learn controls
✔ Deep gameplay systems
✔ Smooth combat mechanics
✔ Strategic gameplay options
✔ Lots of content to explore
✔ Interesting story
✔ Memorable characters
✔ Good world building
✔ Emotional moments
✔ Beautiful environments
✔ Strong visual style
✔ Detailed world design
✔ Smooth animations
✔ Creative art direction
✔ Immersive atmosphere

[b] Quick Cons [/b]

✘ Limited replay value
✘  Heavy microtransactions
✘ DLC feels overpriced

[b] Initial Thoughts: [/b]

The reason I did not fill out this category is because I originally played this a long time ago and my 'initial thoughts' are no longer 'initial.'

[b] Beginner Player Friendly?  [/b]

I would argue this game is not beginner player friendly as if you are playing this game you have skipped the rest of the franchise and important links to this story as this is a part two to the Resident Evil Biohazard story. There is a small tutorial that will teach you how to use the controls however. Though I would recommend playing the other games before you dive into this one. 

[b] Game Play: [/b]

In this game you play as Ethan who has just discovered his daughter has been kidnapped by the same forces that took his girlfriend hostage in the previous game. In this game however you need to fight numerous bosses, upgrade your weapons and abilitys and solve puzzles in order to get your daughter back.

[b] Graphics: [/b]

As always Resident Evil tops the charts with their incredible graphics. They are realistic and genuienly can make you feel disgusted because of said realisim. Everything is designed wonderfully and I love that they have included the art and 3D models with the game of all the characters you interact with just so you can see how much effort they genuienly put into the game. I do not think this game would run very well on lower end systems however even on lower graphic settings. 

[b] Community: [/b]

The Resident Evil community has been a promonent one in the gaming world for years. They have many games and having been producing said games since the early 2000's. Though having a large community comes with its quirks and can sometimes also lead to some more not as kind people. However, majority of the people I have personally interacted with on this game are kind and helpful.

[b] How long can you play? [/b]

To fully complete this game it took me around 13 hours. However, if you are a completionest then this game would probably take you around 80 hours total to get all the achievements. I would argue that this game does not have a lot of replayability unless you enjoy replaying games. I struggle to replay games only because I feel like once I have played a game once I have expierenced the game in its entirety. 

[b] My Opinion: [/b]

In my opinion Resident Evil Village was incredible when it came out, I remember taking time off of work so that I could really take my time and play this game. I have and will always have a special place in my heart for these games. 

[b] Overall Review [/b]

★ 10/10 - An incredible story with Ethan and his damn well crazy will to go into the craziest situations.

[spoiler] The story here  is one of its weakest points. The biggest problem is how forced the plot feels, especially with Chris  hiding the truth from Ethan for no good reason. If Chris had simply told Ethan from the beginning that Mia was actually Mother Miranda in disguise, most of the drama would have fallen apart. Instead, the game makes Chris act mysterious and irrational just to protect a twist, which feels like lazy writing rather than a natural story choice.

Because of this, Village feels less like a true numbered Resident Evil sequel and more like a gothic fantasy spin-off. Mia is wasted, Mother Miranda is underdeveloped, the connection to Umbrella feels forced, and the tone jumps between horror, action, fantasy, and melodrama without a strong foundation. The game has memorable moments, but the narrative becomes weak when you think about it too much. As a side story about Ethan Winters, it can work. As Resident Evil 8, it feels disappointing.[/spoiler]

And what I expected from the sequel to RE7 was something more like the previous game, not all this weird stuff with vampires and werewolves. A lot of people say, “Oh, but in the classic trilogy there were giant fleas, giant spiders, and a Tyrant with an RPG,” but even so, everything revolved around the T-virus, G-virus, and so on. Now this game has brought together a universe that has nothing to do with zombies. Although the game is fun, it really disappointed me; I thought it would be much more like RE7, but here it took a different turn. 

The scenes are comical and carnival-like, and the fact that it explains the origin of the Megamycete and its connection to Umbrella—in my view—is just a way to make a game that’s easier to please the public with sexy vampires to get the nerds hyped, and all of this is a big marketing ploy to sell the game. If this game had a different name, I’d give it a good rating, thinking it has nothing to do with Umbrella—just a universe of vampires and zombies stemming from an unknown origin—but as a Resident Evil title, I can’t accept this game, and the fact that it’s a numbered entry is a huge insult. I still consider RE5 a game much more in line with the Resident Evil storyline involving Wesker and Oroboros than RE8. I enjoyed the game, but I can’t recommend it as a numbered Resident Evil title. And to those who disagree with me, I’m sorry—this is my opinion, and I’ve been a fan of the Resident Evil franchise since 1999, when I got my PS1.

I think there are games that are much better for Resident Evil lore but have been more overlooked, like Resident Evil 0. Aside from those ridiculous leech things, the lore and atmosphere of Resident Evil 0 are much better than RE8’s, but RE8 seems to have sparked a crazy fan frenzy because of Lady Dimitrescu—lots of people cosplay as her and dress up like her. It seems like that’s what made people fall madly in love with this game, or at least those who are new to the franchise. Unfortunately, I can’t recommend this game, even though I find it fun—but I pretend it’s not Resident Evil at all, but rather a game about vampires and lycans; that’s when it actually gets interesting.

It’s the same thing that happened when they added ghosts to Silent Hill 4—do you remember the criticism back then? A ridiculous plot with a shift in setting. Companies do this because if they want to change the characters in a franchise, it’s much easier to just add “Resident Evil 8” to the title than to make a whole new game—for example, “Lycans vs. Vampires” lol, you get my point?

Resident Evil is a series that has seen lot of change over time, moving back and forth along the spectrum between action and horror. Resident Evil 8, or Village, is perhaps the perfect mid-point between the two genres in this series. 

Village is clearly inspired heavily by Resident Evil 4, with its setting in a strange village, a more mystical story, and more of an emphasis on action. Even smaller details like the merchant, and even sound effects. I actually think Village strikes a better balance than RE4 though. RE4 is excellent, but it loses some of the horror edge that the series had been known for. Having rediscovered its horror roots in RE7, Village arguably does an even better job with the horror side, while also having much more compelling action. The gunplay is much more varied and satisfying than in RE7, and there are some truly terrifying moments that top any in RE7 for me, and certainly any of RE4. 

There's also plenty of the usual backtracking and puzzles that are a core part of the Resident Evil experience. This game more than any other in the series made me want to thoroughly explore the map.

Resident Evil at its best is also known for its 'pursuer' enemies. In RE2 it was Mr X, in RE3 Nemesis, and in RE7 Jack Baker. Other games in the series have dabbled with the idea but not to the same degree. While I still think that RE2 Remake's Mr X is the pinnacle of this idea in horror games, Lady Dimitrescu for me comes a solid second place from what I've played. The way she stalks the corridors of the castle is very reminiscent of Mr X, and that portion of the game was very memorable for me. 

Village's story is pretty good. It can be a bit melodramatic at times, but its solid. Lore though? The lore is very good. I won't spoil, but this game ties the series together nicely, and fans of the series will appreciate that. 

Overall, I'd say this is one the absolute best games in the series, and these days it tends to be a steal on sale. Definitely worth playing.

Resident Evil Village is overall an excellent game, and a truly massive improvement over RE7, which I thought was mediocre. RE7 was Capcom's first true FPS game, and it really showed in the final product with how bad the core game design was. Movement and shooting mechanics were both bad in RE7, but have improved to adequate in RE8. Enemy design and variety were truly abysmal in RE7 but in Village the enemy design is actually great and variety is very good with numerous types of zombies that have multiple types of weapons each as well as armored enemies and some straight up monsters. On top of that, enemies actually move around and try to dodge your bullets! I think the combat is a level below RE4, but absolutely blows away the combat from any other game in the series. They took a page from RE4 with the merchant and progression mechanics which, again, are a massive improvement on the shallow systems present in the prior game. The one gameplay area RE7 did excel in was the boss fights and RE8 matches it in every way and doesn’t have a limp anti climactic finale, which was a big issue in RE7. The final boss fight in Village was the hardest IMO and a good final challenge for the game.   

The level design remains very good in RE8, with interesting areas to explore and quite a few creepy and scary areas to send some dread shivering down your spine. The art design and atmosphere are excellent, like they are in every Resident Evil game, and the FPP makes the game immersive and the horror more impactful compared to the TPP of most of the Resident Evil series. House Beneviento is the most horrifying area I have ever encountered in a video game, and there are numerous other sequences in the game that ratchet up the dread for the player. Capcom has done a good job of tension building that culminates in real scares where the player has to quickly decide what they are going to do. They have struck the balance almost perfectly between frustrating deaths, which lowers horror due to repetition, and making the game too easy which would put the player at ease throughout the game. There is also a decent selection of puzzles that do an adequate job of helping pace the game, though they tend to be on the easy side.  

I like the story and think they did a good job finishing the tale of Ethan Winters that started in RE7. The writing and voice acting are good, with the campy style that Resident Evil is well known for. RE8 is better integrated into the series as a whole, but they keep the focus on Ethan trying to rescue his daughter and never go overboard with the lore dumps. They also did a good job with the main villains, who each have distinct visual design and personalities which match their individual areas as well. I like the variety the game has with each successive area being a different flavor of survival horror and House Beneviento just being a straight up horror experience. I also appreciate how they incorporated the Chris Redfield section as the penultimate level rather than a (free) DLC like in the prior game. The somewhat of a twist ending is effective as well. 

Overall, I would give RE Village an 9/10 and think it is one of the best Survival Horror games of all time. The game runs great and is a reasonable length for a full priced AAA game (about 15 hours for a first playthrough.) For me, only RE4 Remake and the Dead Space remake stand above it. I think of all the Resident Evil games, RE8 combines action gameplay with real horror sequences most effectively, as RE4 is more of a straight up action game.

Resident Evil: Village 

I don't think there has ever been a game in my life that I wanted to love more, but I just can't. Village wants to be the ultimate RE experience that offers something for everyone—good puzzles for fans of the original concept, great gunplay for fans of RE4 and its successors, and solid horror for fans of modern RE games. In the end, we got a final product that suffers from a severe identity crisis and completely fails on every front.

[+] 
• Visually stunning 
• Memorable villain designs 
• The Merchant is interesting both in design and as a character 
• Multiple types of scares, a few solid horror sequences

[-] 
• Completely linear main progression 
• Terrible puzzles; everything is handed to the player at the end of linear, scripted sequences, leaving no room for exploration 
• Too many villains completely ruined the quality of each individual one; wasted potential 
• A story that feels like it was rewritten multiple times, as if the creators themselves didn't know what they wanted; the protagonist is still terrible 
• Complete butchering of Chris as a character
• Call of Duty segment 
• Total loss of inventory management
• Shamefully low enemy variety considering the game's scale and weapon arsenal 
• A couple of comically bad boss battles 

Ultimately, RE: Village is a game that disappointed me when it first released, but now, after a replay, it has fallen even lower in my eyes. I know how loved this game is, and I understand why, but for me, this is a game that tried to do everything and accomplished practically nothing.

Listen to me very carefully: Resident Evil Village is a flawless, adrenaline-soaked masterpiece. It doesn’t just respect your time; it aggressively grabs you by the throat from the opening menu and drags you through a breathtaking, chaotic, hyper-stylized hellscape that you will never want to leave.

This isn't just a game. It is a cinematic, survival-horror opera where every single frame belongs in a museum and every single gunshot feels like a religious experience.
I love every single atom of this game. The atmosphere is immaculate, the villains are iconic cultural phenomena, and the gameplay loop is flawless.

Resident Evil Village is a towering achievement in gaming history and a mandatory experience for anyone who claims to love video games. Stop reading this, buy the game, turn off the lights, crank the audio, and witness absolute perfection.

Resident Evil Village is an exceptional experience that brilliantly balances horror and action. The game continues the story of Ethan Winters in an exciting and touching way.

Strengths:

World Design: Incredible diversity between areas makes exploration fun and unrepetitive.
Combat System: Huge improvement in mechanics with the addition of the Duke as a merchant, adding fun and mystery to weapon development.
Story: A clever connection to the history of the series, especially in revealing the origins of the Umbrella Company.
Note: Despite mastering the powers of the Lords and explaining them biologically, I am not completely convinced by Heisenberg's abilities; It felt more like magic than science, which broke the realism of the series a bit.

Add Shadows of Rose:
An excellent addition and a perfect and poignant conclusion to the story of the Winters family, completely worth experiencing.

I have a few grips with RE8 compared to RE7 and RE9 but overall I enjoyed the game for what it is. It's a lot more action oriented than RE7 with stronger weapons, enemies that come in larger numbers and more aggressive. 
That said the game has a few things it really should have made it clear for you at the start. It's still got horror moments but overall more action than 7.

The game does a poor job explaining some mechanics. Some of these can spare you a lot of headache knowing ahead of time. 

No story spoiler but if you want to figure the game out on your own then stop reading from this point.


 [spoiler] 1) The game does a bad job teaching you how to actually push an enemy. It tells you to use the block button but fail to mention the trigger requirement. You need to hit block again after successfully blocking to trigger a push back. It's not a perfect parry system like RE4R or RE9 where its timing based. This will save you from being pinned into a corner on higher difficulty without burning through ammo.

2) DO NOT sell any meat, fish, poultry you find. They are RE8's version of health/defense/speed upgrades. The issue is that is that the game start letting you find these meat way before you can actually use them. This causes some players to either throw them out when their inventory is full or sell them. Because up to that point everything the game gives you is either ammo/heal or stuff to be sold. Second is the game loves putting them in places you can't return with one of the spots being long before you even realize what the meat is used for. Specifically you can easily miss and be locked out of a bunch of fishes required for upgrades.

3) New weapons you find are straight up upgrades, not side grades like some RE games. A fully upgraded weapon will be weaker than a version you find later in game. Any weapon you sell can be purchased back at twice the price, which makes selling later weapons detrimental for NG+ as some would take entire rerun of the story just to buy back.  [/spoiler]

Resident Evil Village suffers from the exact same syndrome as RE7: an absolutely incredible first half followed by a severely uninspired second half. However, Village is honest with you. It doesn't market itself as pure survival horror. This is fundamentally a first-person Resident Evil 4, and when it fires on all cylinders, it’s a fantastic action-horror ride.

THE HIGHS (Base Game):

The First Half: The opening village section is incredibly intense and serves as a fantastic modern successor to RE4. Moving into Lady Dimitrescu's Castle feels like the most polished, classic Resident Evil section of the game.

Donna Beneviento: When the game takes risks, it pays off beautifully. Her segment strips away your power and relies on pure psychological horror.

The Narrative Payoff: The ending to Ethan Winters' story is genuinely beautiful. The twist that he actually died to Jack Baker in RE7 and has been a walking Mold corpse this entire time ("his corpse just caught up with Ethan") is a genius retcon. Killing off a protagonist takes guts, and Capcom nailed his conclusion.

THE LOWS (The Second Half Drop-Off):

Broken Difficulty Scaling: The opening village ambush is the hardest the base game ever gets. Once you unlock the Duke and start upgrading weapons, the difficulty evaporates because enemy health pools simply do not scale with your massive arsenal.

Bland Lords: Moreau is a bland, infuriatingly annoying boss. Heisenberg is essentially a failed "Krauser" clone giving the tired "imagine what we could accomplish together" speech, leading into a horribly bloated Factory section.

Mother Miranda: For a character who canonically inspired Oswell E. Spencer, her presence is barely felt. Worse, her boss fight has no escalating mutations—you just fight one stationary form and she goes down.

The Chris Redfield Section: Pure padding. It throws you into a linear action shooting gallery that actively breaks the pacing. Everything he does could have been told in a cutscene.

The Merchant Dynamic: The Duke is fun, but Ethan talks to him way too much. The original RE4 Merchant was an unexplained, otherworldly gag. Using the Duke as a lore exposition dump ruins that mystique.

THE SAVING GRACE: SHADOWS OF ROSE DLC (9.5/10)
Shadows of Rose is immensely better than almost everything in the base game and legitimately brings back pure survival horror. It is lengthier than End of Zoe and Not a Hero combined, and the shift to third-person works brilliantly to ground the tension.

The Economy of Vulnerability: Rose is just a kid with no military training. She isn't a bulky tank like Ethan, so when enemies corner you, you actually get hurt. Her powers are strictly limited and defensive, forcing you to respect the physical space of the encounters.

The Dollhouse: An absolute masterclass in level design. It heavily channels the Scarecrow nightmare sections from the Batman: Arkham games, completely stripping your comfort zones away.

The Only Flaw: 90% of the DLC is amazing, but it falls off in the last 10%. Pitting you against Mother Miranda again for an incredibly easy final boss fight abandons the desperate survival loop that made the rest of the expansion so perfect.

VERDICT:
Capcom clearly used the base game of Village to test their RE Engine mechanics for the RE4 Remake, front-loading their best ideas and padding the second half. However, the Shadows of Rose DLC completely redeems the package. It is a phenomenal, terrifying return to form and a perfect conclusion to the Winters storyline.

Resident Evil Village is a great game, with excellent graphics, good gameplay, and a very enjoyable campaign. Overall, I really liked the experience.

My main issues are with some parts of the story and certain characters, which could have been better developed. The game has good ideas, but I feel that some of them could have been used better.

Another point that bothered me a little was the lack of identity in the weapons. They feel very standardized, and usually the next weapon is simply better than the previous one. In Resident Evil 4, for example, each weapon had its own unique advantage when fully upgraded, and I missed that here.

Even with these points, Resident Evil Village is still a very good game, beautiful, fun, and full of great moments. I also really enjoyed the Mercenaries mode, which adds replay value after the campaign.

In short: it is a great game and definitely worth playing, even with some issues in the story and weapon variety.

Resident Evil Village é um ótimo jogo, com gráficos excelentes, boa jogabilidade e uma campanha bem divertida. No geral, gostei bastante da experiência.

Outro ponto que me incomodou um pouco foi a falta de identidade das armas. Elas são muito padronizadas, e normalmente a próxima arma é simplesmente melhor que a anterior. Em Resident Evil 4, por exemplo, cada arma tinha um diferencial próprio quando era melhorada ao máximo, e senti falta disso aqui.

Mesmo com esses pontos, Resident Evil Village continua sendo um jogo muito bom, bonito, divertido e com ótimos momentos. Também gostei bastante do modo Mercenários, que aumenta o fator replay depois da campanha.

Resumindo: é um ótimo jogo e vale muito a pena, mesmo com algumas ressalvas na história e na variedade das armas.

I wish I could go for mixed. It's probably worth playing but the encounter design is specifically crafted to annoy you and waste your time in many ways. You're running from a boss, theres a ladder, you cant reach it, you go to the other side, you go back, theres a little lock, you hit it, now you can go. The lock is just there to annoy you and waste your time. This is the most minor possible example.

Others would be areas locking you out at arbitrary moments when you trigger a boss fight blind, and now you missed the items in that area until NG+. Yeah, not once but separate points in the game, after every boss. Actually it is designed that way, to push you into replaying the game even if you'd rather collect everything in one go.

Something more petty and annoying, every time you kill moreau's slime walls, it takes like 4 knife slashes. Except of course, the slime wall that appears on the boat that is timed and you have to get off the boat or die. That slime wall takes 6 slashes, purely designed that way so you stop after 4 slashes and question whether it is breakable or  not.

The map is nice and areas are red if they have items or blue if they are finished. Except for when they dont change color at all, its just a white terrain map, but there are items there, rendering the map kind of useless since it can't be trusted.. Like were you just too lazy to put this feature on the map of the drained fish monster area? and the outdoor areas? Inconsistent design. It's nice that the fishing spots are marked on the map. Oh, but wait, the most important one with the finest fish and 2 other fish, they didnt bother to mark that one??

The difficulty and tension in this game is also largely artificial and fabricated, not really something new to RE games, but worse in 7 and this one than any others. whole hour long sections of the game are just walking (or running) simulators where you are basically doing QTEs without the button presses, or you have no weapons to fight, or can't damage the boss. This game is trying to be a movie, but like an extremely long, tedious, and often boring movie of like 30 hours, when 3 would have actually been a really good movie.

We can talk about the game journalist yellow paint slathered everywhere, but that's a dead horse with Crapcom at this point. But why is there clearly yellow indicated paint slatherd on random things like ropes that you cant interact with or cut or anything? It's like they were trying to hide that they were using game journalist paint by painting a couple random other things with it, again completely defeating the purpose of what they are doing.

The visuals, the actual story, characters (most at least hesienberg is homoerotic and annoying, totally overrated. He was unbearable as Astarion and promptly got murdered in every BG3 playthrough I did. Equally unbearable here. Maybe theres a mod to put only heisenberg as the Japanese VA, I didnt bother to check but that would be the play for sure. The slob reddit mod boss just could be erased from the game along with the mutant baby and it'd be better off) is mostly good, the actual gameplay is fun when you get to engage in it and aren't just doing scripted event things.

Resident Evil 8: Village is a survival horror game and a direct sequel to Resident Evil 7: Biohazard, in which Ethan Winters searches for his kidnapped daughter in a mysterious village filled with strange creatures. Since I really enjoyed the previous game, I was glad to get to play as Ethan Winters again and in first-person perspective. I heard things about it regarding how different this game is from the previous games of the series, and of course, the “dommy mommy” character that is the Lady. So with that in mind, while I don’t think it’s as good as the previous game, I do think it’s about as good as Resident Evil 4 Remake.

I was not expecting the game to be that different, even if it reminds me of Resident Evil 4 to the point of having a merchant archetype of a character. I honestly really like how refreshing it feels and looks, that is leaning into the fantasy aesthetic, but still keeps the heart and feel to the Resident Evil franchise. I like the fact that this game gave Ethan more of a personality in this than the previous game, where he has a journal for himself, and he has a lot more characters to interact with, and more dialogue to spare. He doesn’t want to forget the past and learn from it, while his wife Mia wants to forget it and move on. He honestly became one of my favorite main characters in a Resident Evil game that was fastfor me to grow to really like him. The graphics look so smooth that it looks better here with better face textures to show than Biohazard did. The bosses, whom I know get criticized, are still a lot of fun for me to face, and are satisfying when it’s all over. The lore in this is adding a lot more than I thought to the universe of Resident Evil, which I generally didn’t know I was gonna be able to know at all that I was very grateful that it’s actually very interesting. The character development that is effective in the previous game is still here, and I really appreciate the creators putting effort into gamers to actually care and feel for these people. The story keeps itself unpredictable, which leads me to feel surprised and upset over what I’ve been seeing. 

The action is where things get tricky for fans because it’s increased in this game, and that’s something that people are either ok or not ok with. Did I find the horror elements scary? Not really, so the action elements are honestly kind of needed, which I did find a lot of fun, especially when it’s getting closer to the final act, where things go absolutely crazy, and I was loving it. The things that I wasn’t particularly fond of are the puzzles and the final boss. With the puzzles, most of them are fine, but it’s the extra puzzles involving a ball that are absolutely terrible. The controls are janky, and the flow of the ball’s movement is all over the place, which was driving me absolutely crazy. While I didn’t find the final boss in Biohazard, this one I had more of an issue because it just falls short of being an effective final boss that I felt was honestly underwhelming. But other than that, Resident Evil 8: Village is such a fun game that I have one more extra piece of content to talk about before getting into Requiem.

My journey though Village was bumpy at first. Originally when it first came out I made it through the first section of the game and then life happened. I had a little bit of trouble associating it with resident evil. Then, I went back and played through 7 first. Gave Ethan a chance and really emersed myself in his character, his life, his problems. Once I went though 7 again, I jumped back into Village. This game takes a more Action/Horror vibe than its predecessor, but that is not a bad thing. If you enjoy Resident Evil 4 (And I do. Lord, I really do.) then you will find that this entire game is a love letter to it's fans. Very action packed with quite a few tense moments. Ethan is, not just a normal guy anymore. He had been through hell in the first game and since then, he has been trained and in a way prepped for the event's of Village. 

This game offers a lot of good additional content as well. If you pick up the Shadows of Rose DLC, or get the Gold Edition, then you can experience a fun story involving Ethan's daughter. The mercenaries mode is fun as well. 

My advice is to play 7 before this game. If only just for Ethan's introduction and simply because the game is great. Then transition into Village. I would also like to note that these games go on sale quite often and you can even pick up the "Gold Editions" on a good deal a lot. Wait for these moments and pick it up. Overall. I did really love the game. I completed several playthroughs on all difficulties, (Including Village of Shadows.) and collected every achievement adding it to my "Perfect Games" category here on Steam. As for all of these games, they are crafted so well for the most part. Take your time, get the experience that you are intended too and enjoy it. For me, this one is a solid 8.5 out of 10.

I have a major soft spot for RE8 since it was my first Resident Evil game. But if I'm being honest with myself, it's not the best one. RE4 is the best when it comes to action, RE7 is the best horror game, and RE2 is the best Resident Evil horror game (it makes sense if you've played it).

But man, RE8 is absolutely goated. No other Resident Evil game leans this hard into gothic horror. You've got sexy vampires, werewolves, fish monsters, Beneviento (I swear the RE community doesn't give her enough love), and "straight" Astarion—all wrapped up in a bloody snow-covered village. By the end of the game, I felt bad for Ethan Winters. He's just a normal guy in the Resident Evil universe, constantly being jerked around by outside forces that keep trying to take his family away from him.

This game has some of the best villains in the franchise (except for Wesker), and it's absolutely tragic that Capcom hasn't come up with a plot device to bring them back.

One major complaint I have with the game, though, is the pacing. I know Resident Evil games are usually short, but Village felt like it needed another 5–10 hours of content. You defeat the bosses way too quickly, and I wanted to dive deeper into the psyches of the Four Lords. I also wish we had gotten more content involving the villagers. There was so much potential with this game that felt wasted. 

Heres to hoping that one day, Capcom brings back these villains into the franchise.

Resident Evil Village is easily one of the best Resident Evil games I've ever played. Capcom absolutely cooked with this one.

The story starts strong and never lets go. What begins as Ethan Winters trying to live a normal life after the events of Resident Evil 7 quickly turns into a brutal and emotional journey to save his daughter. The plot is filled with mystery, tension, and one of the biggest twists in the entire franchise. Ethan went from being "just the guy from RE7" to one of my favorite Resident Evil protagonists. His determination throughout the game and the revelations surrounding his character make his story unforgettable.

The characters are fantastic. Ethan carries the game, but characters like Chris Redfield, Mother Miranda, Lady Dimitrescu, Karl Heisenberg, and The Duke all leave a strong impression. Every area has its own unique villain and atmosphere, making the adventure constantly feel fresh and memorable.

Graphically, Resident Evil Village is stunning. The environments, lighting, character models, and overall atmosphere are some of the best I've seen in a horror game. The village itself feels alive, mysterious, and genuinely creepy. Even years after release, the game still looks incredible thanks to Capcom's RE Engine.

What impressed me the most is the optimization. The game looks amazing while still running incredibly well on a wide range of hardware. It's rare to see a game with visuals this good perform this smoothly.

Resident Evil Village perfectly combines horror, action, exploration, memorable characters, and emotional storytelling. It takes the foundation of Resident Evil 7 and expands it into something even bigger. From the opening moments to the ending, I was hooked.

10/10. Peak Resident Evil. Peak Ethan Winters. Peak Capcom.

[h1]NO WINTER IS COLDER THAN ETHAN![/h1]

I had been planning to experience the conclusion of Ethan Winters' story for a while. Personally, [b] Resident Evil 7: Biohazard [/b] has always been my favorite - I watched gameplay of it from day one - whereas [b]Resident Evil Village[/b] didn't initially catch my attention as much. Outside of the flooded internet trends and edits of Lady Dimitrescu (a fan favorite for absolutely no obvious reasons, of course), I didn't actually know much about the plot itself, aside from how it ended. However, finally jumping into the game completely changed my perspective.

Even though I still prefer the gritty, claustrophobic feel of the previous game, [b]Resident Evil Village[/b] delivers incredible art design and ambience. The medieval castle scenery and the snowy village overrun by Lycans keep a constant, heavy tension resting on your shoulders. The horror peaks beautifully in specific segments. The Beneviento House is a masterpiece of psychological horror, where being chased by the mutated giant fetus in the dark is easily one of the most terrifying sequences in the franchise. Furthermore, in the [b]Shadows of Rose[/b] DLC, the "don't blink" mannequin doll sequence provides an absolutely stellar, heart-pounding horror experience. 

My only real critique lies in a habit [b]Resident Evil[/b] DLCs tend to have, which is reusing base game locations. For me, backtracking through the exact same environments can feel a bit like a lazy, looped gameplay formula. Despite that, the narrative value still holds up. One of my absolute favorite creative choices is the mystery surrounding Ethan's face. Even though we all know what he looks like now due to mods, the fact that the developers intentionally hide his face in the vanilla game works beautifully. It serves as a powerful metaphor for a father's selfless, unconditional love for his daughter. By keeping his face hidden, Ethan becomes an avatar for that universal protective instinct, which creates incredible character development. This sense of mystery always caught my eye.

In the end, this was a deeply satisfying and emotional finale for Ethan Winters. He started without a massive background and was never one of the original, legacy OG characters, but he earned immense respect across these two titles. I love that Capcom tried something genuinely different, shifted their perspective, and the results came out fire. The father's story is now done, indeed...

Ethan... may you finally rest in peace. A winter will never hurt you again!

Definitely recommended. It's really entertaining to play. We get to see a direct sequel of RE7, continuing the story of the GOATED ETHAN WINTERS. I don't have much concerns for RE Village as a standalone game, though I do have some as it being a part of the RE franchise.

In terms of story, as a standalone game or as a direct sequel to RE7, the story of RE Village is really great - thrilling journey, interesting villains, fascinating ending, and ETHAN WINTERS. I really like Ethan Winters, he's easily one of the best characters in the franchise for sure. Although as part of the established "sci-fi bioweapon" storyline of the franchise, RE Village has veered too much from it, weirdly going into "magic" territory. The game's story basically forces the "scientific explanations" for the villains and their abilities. But man, we got Magneto who can transform into a Decepticon, we got Maleficent, ladies that can disintegrate into flies, and then Rose's powers in the DLC. Usually, in the previous RE games, it's just a different form of BOW and grotesque mutations, but RE Village is far from those. 

In terms of gameplay, it is full of action with a bit of horror elements in some parts. It was fun defeating each lord of the village and then finally the main antagonist. The puzzles are also really well-made and entertaining to solve. It also feels close to RE4 Remake, given the similar merchant shop and weapon upgrade system.

Overall, as a standalone game, it's an easy 10/10, it was really fun. Personally, it's just behind RE7 and RE4 Remake as my favorites in the franchise. As a Resident Evil game, it's an 8/10, given my concerns above. The DLC is a must-play too, Rose was awesome there.

Resident Evil Village is one of the greatest games I have ever played. Absolute cinema.

This game feels like the perfect fusion of Resident Evil 4 and Resident Evil 7. It has the progression, exploration, merchant system, and action pacing of RE4, while also carrying the horror, atmosphere, emotional storytelling, and psychological tension of RE7.

The atmosphere is INSANE. The way the village changes as the story progresses, the snow reducing, the environments evolving, the feeling that the world itself is reacting to your progression — it all adds so much immersion.

Every area feels unique:
the castle, Beneviento house, Moreau section, Heisenberg’s factory — all completely different flavors of horror and storytelling.

And the way Capcom connected everything back to the older Resident Evil lore, Umbrella, Spencer, and the franchise history was honestly genius. At first the game feels disconnected from classic Resident Evil, but slowly everything clicks together in such a satisfying way.

Ethan Winters is also one of the most underrated protagonists in gaming. He feels human. Not some invincible superhero, just a guy desperately trying to survive and protect his family. That emotional grounding is what makes the story hit so hard.

I played this game alongside my friend by syncing our progression together on our laptops, and the experience became unforgettable. We literally played till 1 AM discussing every reveal, every boss fight, every twist, and every insane cinematic moment.

The Heisenberg factory section especially felt overwhelming in the best possible way. We had to stop and continue the next day because of how intense the experience became.

This game is not just “a horror game.”
It is survival horror, action, psychological horror, emotional drama, gothic fantasy, and blockbuster cinema combined together.

RE4 may still be the revolutionary gameplay masterpiece, but for me, RE7 and especially RE8 are on another level narratively and atmospherically.

Even if this game was not called Resident Evil and was released as a completely new franchise, I would still praise it the exact same way.

Absolute masterpiece.
Absolute cinema.

Resident Evil Village feels like a dark fairytale disguised as a survival horror game.

This game somehow balances horror, action, mystery, emotional storytelling, and absolute insanity all at the same time — and it works surprisingly well.

From the moment the game starts, the atmosphere pulls you in. The snowy village, the abandoned houses, the distant sounds, the gothic architecture — everything feels eerie and immersive. Capcom absolutely nailed the visual design here. Even years later, this game still looks incredible.

And then there’s Castle Dimitrescu.

Walking through those giant halls while trying not to get hunted down by a 9-foot vampire woman is somehow both terrifying and stressful 😭

But what makes Village special is how varied the experience feels. Every major area has its own personality:

Castle Dimitrescu = gothic horror
House Beneviento = psychological nightmare fuel 💀
Moreau’s section = creepy sadness
Heisenberg’s factory = industrial chaos

The game constantly changes its tone without losing its identity, and that keeps the experience fresh from beginning to end.

Gameplay feels fantastic too. Combat is satisfying, weapons feel impactful, exploration is rewarding, and upgrading your gear through the Duke creates that addictive “just one more upgrade” feeling.

Speaking of the Duke:

“What’re ya buyin’?”
Wrong game.
Still legendary energy 😂

Ethan Winters also became a much stronger protagonist here compared to Resident Evil 7. His determination to protect his daughter genuinely gives the story emotional weight beneath all the horror and madness.

And honestly, House Beneviento deserves special mention because that section was one of the most terrifying experiences I’ve had in gaming in YEARS.

If you know… you know. 👶💀

The sound design is incredible too. Quiet footsteps, distant noises, creaking wood, sudden screams — this game understands how to build tension without constantly relying on cheap jumpscares.

Of course, it’s not perfect:

Some action-heavy sections feel less “survival horror”
Certain boss fights can be frustrating
The story becomes wonderfully ridiculous near the end 😂
And yes, inventory management will make you feel like you’re solving advanced mathematics with shotgun shells

But overall?

Resident Evil Village is one of the best modern Resident Evil games and one of the most memorable horror experiences in recent years.

It’s creepy, beautiful, emotional, intense, weird, and completely unforgettable.

Verdict:
A fantastic blend of survival horror and action with amazing atmosphere, memorable villains, emotional storytelling, and enough nightmare fuel to make you check dark corners in real life 🌑

10/10 would panic while opening doors again 🚪💀

Resident Evil Village is an excellent continuation of Resident Evil 7, showing just how much the franchise has grown while still respecting its roots. While Resident Evil 7 focused heavily on pure survival horror and a claustrophobic atmosphere, Village expands the formula with larger environments, more action, and a wider variety of enemies without completely abandoning the horror elements that made its predecessor so memorable.

One of the game's strongest aspects is Ethan Winters. In Resident Evil 7, Ethan felt like an ordinary person thrown into an unimaginable nightmare. In Village, his character is developed much further, giving players a stronger emotional connection to his journey and motivations. By the end of the game, Ethan becomes one of the most memorable protagonists in the series.

The return of Chris Redfield was another highlight. Seeing one of Resident Evil's classic characters play a major role alongside Ethan helped bridge the gap between the newer and older generations of the franchise. Chris's presence added weight to the story and reminded fans of the series' long history.

The Four Lords were also a fantastic addition. Each had a unique personality, setting, and style of horror that kept the game fresh throughout. Lady Dimitrescu, Donna Beneviento, Salvatore Moreau, and Karl Heisenberg all brought something different to the experience, making the village feel like a collection of interconnected nightmares.

My only real criticism is that some boss encounters relied too much on the game directly providing the solution. It would have been even more satisfying if Ethan had to figure out certain weaknesses and strategies on his own, allowing players to feel more involved in overcoming the challenges.

Despite that minor issue, Resident Evil Village successfully balances horror, action, storytelling, and fan service. The emotional ending ties Ethan's story together beautifully and leaves a lasting impact. It's a worthy successor to Resident Evil 7 and one of the strongest entries in the franchise.

Resident Evil Village is a mixed bag that swings between excellent atmosphere and questionable design choices.

On the positive side, the game looks incredible. The environments are detailed, creepy, and sometimes genuinely unsettling. The Village itself is a strong central hub, and certain sections (especially Castle Dimitrescu) deliver some of the best modern Resident Evil moments.

But the consistency just isn’t there. The game constantly shifts tone—from survival horror to full-on action shooter—and not always in a good way. Some sections feel like they belong in a different game entirely. The horror tension fades too quickly once you get comfortable with the combat.

The story is serviceable but nothing special, and some villains feel more like gimmicks than real threats. It tries hard to be memorable, but only a few characters actually stick.

Gameplay is solid but safe. Gunplay works, crafting is fine, but it rarely evolves in meaningful ways. Once you figure out the loop, it becomes predictable.

Overall, it’s a visually impressive and occasionally great Resident Evil entry that never fully commits to being either pure horror or pure action—and that holds it back.

Final verdict: 7/10 — good, but inconsistent and overhyped in places.

I finished Resident Evil Village in a few sittings because I genuinely wanted to know what happened next. That's probably the biggest compliment I can give it.

The story starts off simple enough, but the deeper you get, the more it expands the world of Resident Evil in ways I wasn't expecting. There are plenty of crazy moments, but somehow it never feels completely disconnected from the series. It answers questions from Resident Evil 7 while creating a bunch of new ones along the way.

What surprised me most was Ethan. In RE7 he felt more like a regular guy caught in a nightmare. In Village, he becomes a character I actually cared about. His determination to keep going no matter how insane things get made the ending hit much harder than I expected.

The villains are memorable, each with their own personality and story, and I spent a lot of time reading notes and documents because I wanted to learn more about the world rather than just rushing to the next objective.

The final few hours were fantastic. There were twists I didn't see coming, answers that finally clicked into place, and an ending that genuinely stuck with me after the credits rolled.

Not every part of the story is perfect, and Resident Evil still has that signature of what next to come on the next series.

This ♥♥♥♥ slapped. Mechanically rewarding, great at building tension and cutting it loose at just the right moment. Excellent blend of horror and combat. Reads as such a thoughtful iteration on what RE4 was doing, like a skilled musician revisiting their earlier work with new skills to add to the mix. Lots of segments sort of rhyme with the 2006 classic. Biggest difference between RE8 and 4 is tonal, even though they're mechanically aligned: I found RE8 could actually scare me at times, and it plays its campier segments more straight than RE4. I think we all love the bombastic stupidity of RE4, but the more even tone of RE8 I think actually makes the more outlandish moments, like the famous "glue your hand back on", pop even more, especially given the adrenaline fueled escape that moment follows.

The combat is solid, the pacing is solid; no individual set piece or section overstays its welcome. Even simpler Lords are still great at the sort of spectacle environmental encounters that feels like a trademark of action RE gameplay. I've been playing a lot of Resident Evil this year, and this might be my favourite one. Weds the survival horror and action elements of the series in the most cohesive way I feel I've seen the franchise do *since* the 2006 classic. RE8 always keeps you on that razors edge when it comes to resource management, while having some the funnest, most fluid combat in the series. Banger title. Hell yeah. Storybook fantasy played straight as horror. Hell yeah.

It all begins like a Tim Burton-esque fairy tale and ends with you piloting an mini-tank to fight a scrap-metal golem while parrying attacks with a chainsaw...

Okay. Fair enough.

Resident Evil Village was the last drop of creativity Capcom managed to squeeze out of the franchise's reinvention in Resident Evil 7 before they struck gold with the remakes and essentially gave up on doing anything particularly meaningful. 

While the series is in a much better place than it was after the fiasco of Resident Evil 6, after Requiem it feels clear that the well has run dry and that they need to shake things up again.

But that's a topic for another day.

Village (or RE8, if you prefer) is more or less the equivalent of injecting some Resident Evil 4 into its predecessor. It retains the core foundation established by RE7—which combined an adapted version of the franchise's traditional survival horror formula with then-current genre trends, nods to classic horror films, and a touch of self-aware humor—but pivots toward a more arcade-like approach, placing greater emphasis on combat and scripted set pieces.

While it still preserves much of the slow pacing and dark, claustrophobic environments of the previous game, this time you're given a full arsenal and thrown against a significantly larger number of enemies, with the game actively encouraging you to kill them in exchange for resources and treasure.

Enemy interactions aren't quite as rich, and resources remain scarce enough to encourage strategic weapon usage and discourage shooting the walls, but the final body count would make Old Testament Leon smile with approval.

Your adventure takes you through familiar environments while occasionally venturing down roads the franchise had never really explored before.

Abandoning the usual body-horror abominations, the game embraces classic monsters: werewolves, horny vampires, murderous dolls, a giant incel fish, and even a flamboyant gay inventor with mommy issues.

Oh—and at least one reference to the Great Old Ones. They finally paid the Lovecraft tax.

The titular village functions as an interconnected hub linking several self-contained areas, each offering a distinct experience. These range from a spooky castle inhabited by a very tall woman whom you always seem to accidentally run toward for some reason, to a psychological torture escape room that I would definitely recommend letting your kids play.

As is customary for the series, the game is exceptionally well put together. The pacing is excellent, the balance is remarkably tight, and the variety of situations remains fresh throughout its relatively short runtime.

Some sequences are familiar franchise staples—as you'll notice almost immediately—but many others give the game a distinct identity of its own. It's an amalgamation of ideas that have worked throughout the series, executed through a modernized lens.

Even though a few asset flips occasionally stand out in ways that clash with the surrounding atmosphere, the presentation is top-tier. Great attention to detail contributes to environmental storytelling and memorable vistas.

Unfortunately, collectibles still blend into the scenery, forcing you to constantly check the map to make sure you haven't missed anything. And the first signs of the infamous yellow juice were beginning to appear.

While I do miss a more prominent soundtrack, the sound design (except for the weapons, oddly enough) is sublime. An auditory dessert. A festival of creaks and crunchy snaps that make every environment feel alive.

All of this comes together to create a highly immersive and captivating atmosphere, though not everything works perfectly. 

The game's attempts to generate tension sometimes involve stripping the player of their tools and preventing them from engaging with situations through the game's core mechanics. The artificiality of some of these sequences becomes painfully obvious on repeat playthroughs, causing them to lose their immersive appeal and turn into tedious interruptions. 

There are a LOT of interruptions in this game.

That escape room I mentioned? It's one of my least favorite sections in the entire game.

I can appreciate how its mildly disturbing content leaves a strong impression on many people, but it does absolutely nothing for me because I've seen that kind of sequence done elsewhere before and can spot the trick from a mile away. 

I know, for example, that I'm far safer there than I was during the game's tutorial, which genuinely put me in a desperate situation. It's pretty obvious that executing this kind of horror within the actual rules of the game works much better.

Thankfully, it's not a major issue. Still, I wouldn't be surprised if it played some role in my reluctance to revisit the game more frequently.

That leaves us with the story.

Well, it's pretty stupid, as expected...

Resident Evil 7 almost—perhaps for the first time in the franchise's history—managed to tell an interesting story. It moved away from the nonsense of an evil and absurdly incompetent megacorporation constantly conducting experiments, failing miserably, and requiring brain-damaged action heroes to fix everything by blowing it up. Instead, it focused on an isolated setting and leaned into more supernatural and unsettling elements, centering around what appeared to be an ordinary family held hostage and transformed into monsters by the influence of a malevolent entity.
 
Ethan was just an everyman with a mundane motivation caught in surreal circumstances. His more human reactions made those situations far easier to digest, even if they could never truly be taken seriously.

The whole thing had all the subtlety of a broken nose, but at least it was intriguing.

The "vaccine" this time felt more like a ritual than a scientific solution. It was weird and exciting.

And it had a sense of humor. It poked fun at franchise clichés that had already become internal jokes among fans, incorporated countless movie references, and was packed with clever lines of dry humor.

Then, right near the finish line, they pivoted back to the usual stupidity.

Evil corporation. Again. 

The Illuminati are apparently involved now. 

A super-virus.

Sorry, "mold."

Whatever.

At this point, nothing is surprising anymore.

And Village? Well, you no longer have entrails literally shoved into your face, but the setting is far more accommodating for this kind of ridiculous story to flourish. The game still maintains that balance—now sharper than ever—between the campy and the serious. 

The humor never overwhelms the experience, but the narrative constantly leaps through absurd concepts that provide exactly the right level of levity.

And this time around, the developers seem far more confident about it.

Ethan may still be an ordinary guy with ordinary motivations caught in extraordinary situations, but he knows what he's doing now. And this time he has to dismantle a cult of monsters in a remote village whose inhabitants worship a lady with very poor management skills.

Even so, it's undeniably ridiculous to hear those Halloween creatures referred to as "bioweapons."

At least this time they aren't even trying to fool you.

It's a memorable journey, albeit one that makes absolutely no sense, filled with interesting locations to explore, fun combat sequences, simple puzzles to solve, and cool boss fights. It's exactly what any fan of the franchise could want, only with a slightly fresher and more creative flavor.

It's a good game. Not very replayable, though.

You get the feeling that they have nothing left to offer within this formula.

And that the best path forward would probably be reinventing themselves all over again.

[h2] This is a weird game. While it's not perfect like RE4, it's also not a completely bad game. It's fun and enjoyable to play, either solo or in co-op. But as cool as the game looks and as fun as it is, I can't stop wondering: why the downgrade to almost every mechanic RE4 introduced? [/h2]

Before reading this evaluation, it is important to note that I played it solo and in Veteran mode. I read that in coop it is pretty fun but I played every other RE games in solo so I wanted to keep the same experience with this one, which was in hindsight a terrible choice.

Even though the beginning of the game was pretty fun, the second half makes it impossible to recommand it. In this one you lose all the atmosphere and horror aspect you can find in the previous RE games in the main saga, but while I find it a bit sad (because RE 4 managed to keep it with more action) I was already aware of this so I can't really complain. RE5 is basically a corridor shooter with an AI or with an other player, there is no backtracking as in RE4 or every other RE, you don't even have a proper map, just a minimap for each chapter.
In Veteran mode, enemies are just HP bag that came in waves. It was not really a problem during the first half, but the more you progress in the chapters, the more it is unsufferable, honorable mention for the part where at least 15 lickers attack you at the same time and that you need at least 3 acid grenades to kill one of those A**HOLES (their weakness btw). Even with the bullet-proof vest, enemies with rifles are annoying as HELL and it is not fun at all to fight them. The exploration is nonexistent and I don't even want to talk about the 3 or 4 last boss fights that were non sense.

On top of that, Sheva's AI is close to 30 IQ, she sometimes runs into a closed space with a bunch of enemies and close the door to be sure you can't help her, then die (????).
The guns feel like they're made of foam, the feeling is really mid so I didn't even have a great experience with the action part of the game (which is 95% percent of the experience in solo).

In conclusion, I understand that in coop the game can be fun, but in solo and in Veteran mode it is absolute bullsh*t. But I guess my bad for not playing it in normal (to be honest, I should have looked into that it is really my fault for this) and having no friend willing to play this.

👍 Recommended (mostly with a friend)

Resident Evil 5 is honestly way too overhated. It’s definitely more action-focused than horror, but I still had a lot of fun with it, especially in co-op.

The game is at its best when you play with someone else. Running through the campaign together, sharing items, fighting bosses, and just laughing at the chaos makes the experience way more enjoyable. Solo is okay, but co-op is where the game really shines.

There’s also a surprising amount of content. Mercenaries mode is super fun, the DLCs are cool, and unlocking weapons, costumes, filters, and extras gives you a reason to keep playing.

That said, the controls and camera definitely feel old and weird sometimes. Not being able to move while shooting/reloading can be frustrating at first, and some boss fights are honestly pretty annoying. The inventory system is also tiny, so you’ll spend time managing items constantly.

Still, the gunplay is satisfying, Wesker absolutely carries the story, and overall the game is just a fun co-op action experience if you don’t expect pure horror like older Resident Evil games.

I’d recommend it mainly if you have a friend to play with — it makes the game 10x better.

Resident Evil 5 is the ultimate boulder-punching simulator. If you have ever looked at a solid, multi-ton volcanic rock and thought, "I want to uppercut that," then this is the game for you. While the horror elements of the older games are gone, it completely makes up for it with peak action, hilarious co-op chaos, and Chris Redfield's absurdly massive biceps. The inventory management can be annoying and Sheva's AI will occasionally watch you die, but stopping Albert Wesker’s plans for "Complete Global Saturation" through pure geological violence makes it all worth it. Buy it for the co-op, stay for the rock-punching finale. 10/10, Wesker and that boulder never stood a chance.

Resident Evil 5 is a fun and action-heavy entry in the series, but it moves further away from the survival horror roots that made earlier games so memorable.

The gameplay focuses a lot more on action, with more enemies, bigger set pieces, and a stronger emphasis on shooting. While it can be enjoyable, it also feels more limited and less engaging compared to other entries in the series.

The co-op is easily the highlight of the game. Working together, managing resources, and taking on enemies as a team adds a lot to the experience and makes it more fun than playing solo.

However, the game loses a lot of the horror atmosphere. It feels more like a generic action game than a true survival horror experience, which may disappoint fans of the series.

Overall, Resident Evil 5 is decent, especially in co-op, but it doesn’t fully live up to what the series is known for. 

Gameplay: 6/10 Fine | Overall: 7/10 Good (With A Friend)

Lore-wise, Resident Evil 5 is actually a really solid entry in the series. As much as I love the more standalone and experimental titles, I really liked that this game essentially serves as the big finale for the relationship between Chris, Jill and Wesker. I already loved the dynamic between Chris and Wesker in the earlier games and seeing that storyline reach its conclusion here was genuinely satisfying. Especially Wesker is still one of the best villains in the entire franchise for me.

Unfortunately, the game completely suffers from the lack of horror. Even by the end of Resident Evil 4 the series was already becoming much more action focused, which I didn’t really like back then either. Resident Evil 5 takes it even further and feels like it is almost entirely constant action. There is barely any survival horror left. Throughout the whole game I never really felt scared or had that uncomfortable tension that older Resident Evil games did so well. Instead you are basically just shooting enemies all the time, which gets repetitive pretty quickly.

I played it in singleplayer and Sheva’s AI is honestly pretty mediocre. Some situations became more frustrating than challenging because of it. I can definitely imagine the game being a lot more fun in co-op though, especially just laughing together at Matrix Wesker, completely over the top boulder punching Chris or some of the weird enemies.

Resident Evil 5 is the point where you really see how far the series has drifted away from classic survival horror. As an action game it mostly works but at the same time you can clearly see the path that eventually led to Resident Evil 6, which for me was the complete downfall of the franchise.

What’s especially disappointing is that some parts of the game clearly had potential for horror. The opening section alone could have worked so much better at night and many of the later areas could have been way more oppressive. Capcom actually showed with Lost in Nightmares that they were still capable of creating proper horror atmosphere in this era. Instead it often felt like the series was intentionally pushed further and further into action in order to appeal to a wider audience.

That being said, the game still has its strong moments. The story around Chris, Jill and Wesker was really enjoyable and even though Resident Evil 5 is far away from my favorite entries, it still has its place in the series and is definitely worth playing.

I have to apologize to RE5 before it gets a remake!

I will admit that I was one of those who was deeply disappointed the first time they laid their hands on RE5 after RE4. This game is paradoxical in itself, as it represents Capcom's most commercially successful creation, and at the same time probably the most hated game in the series.

I would say that my initial disappointment came from the fact that I wanted to play a better RE4. Of course, I didn't get it either then or now, since it's hard to top one of the best games of all time, but I have to admit to myself, and to you, dear reader who is hesitating whether to try this game after entering the series through modern iterations and remakes, that RE5 really has a lot of quality to offer.

RE5 combines classic tank controls with modern shooter pacing—meaning you still stand still to shoot, but everything around you moves significantly faster. It takes RE4's fundamentals and expands them into a surprisingly deep, beat-’em-up hybrid. While RE4 only had 2 melee trigger points (head and shin), RE5 introduces a third (arms) and makes positioning crucial. For example, shooting a Majini in the knee opens them up for a massive uppercut from the front, but standing behind them triggers a neck breaker for an instant kill.

Because the inventory operates in real-time, you can open it and combine ammo with your weapon during a melee animation. This completely bypasses the physical reload animation, keeping the combat 100% active. Always use menu-reloading, its so fun and so well integrated in core gameplay. As for the combat mechanics themselves, I think that RE5 is the pinnacle of the series in that sense.

Speaking of which, I can't help but say a few words about the mercenaries mod, which is certainly the best iteration of it we've seen and it's totally worth buying this game for. When we take into account how good and fun the combat is first of all, add to that a huge (really huge) number of characters and maps that we can play with plus an almost perfect scoring system that doesn't fall apart at high scores - you can see why I have so many words of praise.

The story and narrative are certainly not strong points, but Mr. I escaped from the matrix - Wesker - makes the whole thing quite cool, which is a key element of the RE series.

I've said a lot of good things, but don't think the game doesn't have its flaws - oh, there are some. Ai is a problem, but not to the extent that is talked about (unless you play on professional mode), the level design could have been better, the sections where the enemies have weapons are bad, etc.

Ultimately the question is whether to buy this game - my answer is a big YES. All the praise I've said aside, I think the price is justified by the sheer amount of content and the fact that it's a great co-op experience. I'm glad I realized the fundamental qualities this game has before it gets a remake and before everyone starts talking about how good it actually was!

For the most part, I enjoyed playing this game. The plot is solid, and it effectively mirrors the damaging nature of colonialism on Africa. However, this message is severely weakened by the portrayal of the Indigenous African villagers, which they are depicted as individuals who are primitive, senseless, and barbaric. Apparently, the development team went to Africa to avoid perpetuating misconceptions in the game, but I feel like this was all for naught because they failed to avoid using both behavioral and physical stereotypes of African people.

In terms of combat, I enjoyed the various and unique weapons (e.g. guns, grenades, rifles, tasers, etc.) I did not enjoy the melee actions, as it was easy to miss hitting the enemy or not even perform the action at all. The inventory system was somewhat annoying to deal with, but I did like the uniqueness of its layout and how you had the opportunity to purchase items before the start of each separate chapter. I have heard from various people that Sheva's AI sucked, which is true. Mine somewhat listened to me, but during the later chapters, I had trouble progressing through some stages due to her running off, not running at all, getting in my way of shooting, or ignoring my calls (e.g. The chapter where Chris and Sheva had to face off two chainsaw men, that was hell on earth).

In the case of the DLCs, I have only completed Untold Stories and am halfway through Desperate Escape. Untold Stories gave great context to the exigence of RE5, and I enjoyed the atmosphere of the Spencer estate. I also liked the mobs, and it briefly made me feel terror, something that the main game fell short on. Fighting Wesker was for the most part enjoyable, even though several times he would stop and stand there before chasing after Chris or Jill. 

Despite various criticisms by me and other people about this game, for the most part, it is a crucial game to play to understand the origins of Umbrella and various characters in the overarching plot. I would definitely like to see a remake of this game.

Even though I'm recommending this game, I would only say so for a couple of reasons:
1. You're a fan of the Resident Evil series and you want to finish all of the mainline games. This isn't a good introduction to the series for people who are new to Resident Evil. 
2. You can get it during one of the Steam sales (I paid $5 recently, and it's not worth the full price in 2026).

As far as the gameplay goes, RE 5 is one of the action oriented games similar to 4 and 6. It's basically a third person shooter where you take out groups of enemies and have occasional boss fights and lite puzzle solving. You have a partner throughout the entire game, and it becomes obvious pretty early on that this game was designed with co-op in mind. 

It's decent for the most part, but playing in a single player campaign on a modern PC can get very annoying at times. The AI of your partner isn't that helpful most of the time, some of the boss fights have some kind of gimmick you have to figure out to defeat them, and a couple of the boss fights are almost impossible on higher framerates unless you just take them out with a rocket blast. The controls are also pretty atrocious compared to the newer RE engine games like the 2 through 4 remakes. I had to turn my mouse sensitivity way up for the game to feel normal. 

After finishing my first playthrough, the game just felt kind of mediocre for the most part. If you want a better third person shooter today, you can certainly find several of them. Was it worth the $5 I spent? Probably considering the first playthrough took me about 12 hours. However, this is not a game I will revisit much in the future or look forward to playing an entire campaign of all 6 chapters again.

Playing this with a friend was really fun actually. I thought the whole generation 7 visual style and corny writing would throw me off but it didn't bother me too much. 

Gameplay wise it's about as good as RE4 was, so it was very engaging and kinetic. The added element of a player 2 does a lot for the explosive insanity this era of Resident Evil is characterised by. 

The story is alright, I'm not crazy about how Resident Evil games increasingly convolute their story so they can force a connection to previous games. Speaking about forcing connections to a previous game; Jill Valentine does nothing here and this story does nothing for her. 
I appreciate following up on the Wesker plot point that had been dangling since Code Veronica but Jill is not utilised well here and the skin tight body suit, weird cat fighting style and mind control implant only serves to take away any agency she might have had in the plot and sexualise her in a way that feels shameless. Bleached hair is a weird choice but I like the idea Wesker just wanted them to match.
Chris and Wesker have as much sexual tension as ever though.

Anyway in conclusion, this game is very good. When the inevitable remake comes I hope they improve the story, Jill is so utterly wasted here in a way that felt weird.

Lastly, there should've been a merchant like in RE4 over just buying stuff at the end of chapters and they shouldn't've gutted the gem system and used such an ass inventory system (get rid of the explicit chapter system, too, as it sucks in these games, but still keep elements of replayability through mission selecting, or something, as mentioned earlier as a positive).

This is the the most arcade feeling Resident Evil in the mainline series, heavily focused on co-op mechanics while ditching puzzles and exploration. While those co-op mechanics are implemented incredibly well, the solo experience can be a grueling pain. The boss fights in particular are designed with co-op in mind, which become unnecessarily frustrating and tedious when you have to depend on an AI companion. 

That said, the game is a pretty well paced 9ish hour experience with a good variety of locations and encounters. The Africa setting and history behind the progenitor virus is great. The combat flow is similar to RE4, mixing in gunplay and melee strikes. Though at times the game becomes a cover shooter, and a few chapters become on-rail shooters. And RE5's mercenary is filled with a ton of stages and characters to play as.

For Steam Deckers to get rid of freezes:
1) Properties of game -> Compatibility, check the box and select the newest "Proton GE"
2) Paste this into Launch options: PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 PROTON_NO_FSYNC=1 %command%
3) Launch game, press Deck's button (...), select Battery, turn on "Use per-game profile" and set 60FPS (60Hz)
4) In game settings, make sure to have V-Sync on and reduce FPS to 60
5) You can restart the game, sometimes it launches on second try after changing all these settings. Enjoy!

🧟‍♂️🔫 Resident Evil 5 Gold Edition Review 🔫🧟‍♂️

🕹 Length: 12–16 hours (main campaign) | Much more with Mercenaries & replaying co-op
💰 Price vs. Content: Very good, especially if played with a friend
⚙ Optimization: Generally solid, though some sections can bug at high FPS
🎮 Difficulty: Moderate; co-op makes the experience much more manageable and fun

🎮 Gameplay

Resident Evil 5 takes the series further into action-focused gameplay, moving away from traditional survival horror in favor of larger combat encounters, heavier weapon customization, and cooperative mechanics.

You play as Chris Redfield, joined by Sheva Alomar, investigating a biological threat in Africa. The campaign is clearly designed around 2-player co-op, and honestly, that’s where the game truly shines. Playing alone with AI can feel awkward at times, but with another player, the experience becomes much more enjoyable and chaotic in the best way possible.

Compared to earlier Resident Evil games, the horror atmosphere is toned down significantly. There are still tense moments and memorable enemies, but the focus is much more on action, shooting, and set pieces. Even so, it remains a genuinely fun game — just not one of the strongest entries in terms of horror.

The game also includes The Mercenaries, one of the best extra modes in the series. It can also be played in 2-player co-op, turning it into an addictive arcade-style mode focused on score chasing, combos, and mastering characters and maps. It adds a huge amount of replayability.

🧩 DLC Content – Untold Stories Bundle

The Gold Edition includes the Untold Stories DLC pack, adding extra story content and modes that expand the experience considerably.

🏚️ Lost in Nightmares

A prequel chapter where Chris and Jill investigate Spencer’s mansion before the events of RE5.

✔ Much more horror-focused than the main game
✔ Strong classic Resident Evil vibes
✔ Atmospheric mansion setting with puzzles and tension
✔ One of the best parts of the DLC package

🔥 Desperate Escape

An action-heavy side story starring Josh Stone and Jill Valentine during the finale of the main campaign.

✔ Fast-paced combat and chaotic fights
✔ Fun in co-op
✔ Expands the ending events of RE5

🎭 Additional Mercenaries Content

The DLC also adds:
✔ New playable characters
✔ Extra costumes
✔ Additional Mercenaries Reunion content

This greatly increases replayability for players who enjoy the arcade-style gameplay.

✅ The Good:
✔ Excellent co-op experience
✔ Fun combat and satisfying weapon upgrades
✔ Mercenaries mode is highly replayable and entertaining
✔ Good amount of content and replay value
✔ Memorable bosses and action sequences
✔ Untold Stories DLC adds both horror and extra action content

❌ The Bad:
✖ Much less horror-focused than classic Resident Evil
✖ AI partner can be frustrating when playing solo
✖ Some sections lean too heavily into action spectacle
✖ A few technical bugs tied to high FPS

💻 Technical Aspects

🎨 Graphics: Still looks surprisingly solid, with strong lighting and environments
🎶 Sound: Good soundtrack and impactful combat audio
🐞 Bugs: Some sections can break or behave strangely at very high framerates, forcing players to limit the game to 30 or 60 FPS to progress properly

⭐ Final Verdict

Resident Evil 5 may not be one of the scariest or most beloved entries in the franchise, but it’s still a really fun co-op action game. The campaign works far better with another player, and modes like Mercenaries add a ton of replay value.

The Untold Stories DLC also helps balance the experience by bringing back some classic horror atmosphere with Lost in Nightmares, while Desperate Escape doubles down on explosive action.

It sacrifices horror for action, but if you approach it as a cooperative shooter set in the Resident Evil universe, it’s an easy game to enjoy.

👉 Rating: 8/10 🔥🧟‍♂️
