Resident Evil 5 was my gateway into the Resident Evil games, so I will always have a soft spot for it.

My ex was the one who introduced me to the series and dragged me through the co-op gameplay, and honestly, it was all over for me after that. This was the game that opened the door, shoved me inside, handed me a gun, and said, “Good luck, babe.”

The co-op is what made it for me. Resident Evil 5 is just so much more fun with another person. Running around together, sharing ammo, saving each other, panicking in real time, yelling about inventory space, and trying to survive while everything gets increasingly more dramatic. It is chaotic in the best way.

It may not be the scariest Resident Evil game, but it is fun, action-packed, and full of ridiculous moments that make it memorable. The story is dramatic, the combat is satisfying, and the whole thing has that specific late-2000s action-horror energy where subtlety was clearly left in another zip code.

And then there is Chris.

Chris Redfield. Chris Boulder Punching Redfield. Chris “I have never skipped arm day in my life” Redfield.

Simply daddy.

I said what I said.

Resident Evil 5 may be divisive, but for me, it will always matter because it is the game that got me into the series. It gave me co-op chaos, big dumb action, infected nonsense, and a main character built like he could bench press my problems.

It is not perfect, but it is special to me.

And yes, the boulder punching is iconic. No further notes.

[h1] 7/10. Decent solo, most likely much better with a friend. Played on normal difficulty [/h1]

[b] Pros: [/b]
+ Story
+ Combat
+ Graphics

The story is fine, I didn't see anything [i] really [/i] wrong with it. However, it is not without flaws. I think the biggest flaw is that (imo) it is just kind of... bland. You could describe it as a piece of unseasoned chicken. When you bite into the chicken, all you really taste is chicken. You like chicken, so you will still eat it. Yet when you're done, you can't help but wish that there was something more than just chicken. 

The combat is very similar to RE4s, but some small expansions like more options when it comes to melee moves. If you liked RE4s combat, you will like this version too.

As for the graphics, it has the traditional piss filter that every damn game had during this time period. Sometimes it has bits of excessive bloom and lens flare, especially when looking at the sun or the sky during the outside parts of the game. But RE5 still looks great all things considered. Textures look nice, cutscenes look nice, the different locations in the game look good.

[b] Cons: [/b]
- Bossfights
- QTEs 
- Inventory system

The bossfights in this game aren't very good. None of them were memorable, not even the Wesker ones (spoilers ahead). [spoiler] The first "fight" against Wesker (& Jill) isn't even really a fight. You have to survive for 7 minutes and can do so by hiding. Hiding from him while Sheva distracts Jill isn't even challenging one bit, so you can just be on your phone while you wait for the next cutscene to trigger. The second one is a bit better, here you an actually fight Wesker. But it is over incredibly quickly anyway. And the third fight at the volcano just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sucks, the game does not give any hint whatsoever that you can finish it instantly with a QTE when you stagger Wesker. [/spoiler] 
Also one of the bosses is literally broken in this port, the 5-2 one. You can't actually defeat it with the weapon that the game gives you. Instead you'll have to use an RPG or some other way to get around the problem.

And the QTEs in this game are very infuriating. What I dislike is not the fact that they are in the game, but rather how they function. A + D as a combination makes sense. You'll have your fingers over these keys 99% of the time. But F + V too? Why??? And the time you have to react during these QTEs is sometimes 0. You'll be in a bossfight and suddenly get a QTE for half a second, making it difficult to react if it isn't A + D. 

And lastly, the inventory system. I did not like this inventory system very much. You have nine slots, and your partner also has nine slots. There are no upgrades that you can buy, so in your first playthrough you will be struggling with space. And worst of all, you can't sort your inventory outside of the sections inbetween chapters where you can buy and upgrade your guns. This means that you may end up in a situation where you've found a new gun but it ends up in the ninth slot and now you have to press 9 to equip it. It's not terrible, but sure is annoying.

As for co-op, I can't say anything about that ATM. But it is probably better to play this game with a friend, as Sheva's AI can be quite frustrating.

I honestly think Resident Evil 5 is overhated. It's different from the classic survival horror style, but that doesn't make it a bad game. The combat is fun, the pacing is good, and there are plenty of memorable moments.

The biggest reason I enjoyed it is the co-op. Playing with a friend makes the experience so much better, turning even the craziest fights into fun memories. I can understand why some fans wanted more horror, but if you judge it for what it is instead of what you wanted it to be, it's a great game.

It may not be the best Resident Evil, but it's definitely one of the most underrated.

My rating: 7/10.

When I was younger, I used to play Resident Evil 5 all the time, and it quickly became one of my favorite games. From the moment it first came out, I was hooked—the action, the co-op gameplay, and the intense atmosphere all made it stand out to me. It wasn’t just a game I played; it was an experience I kept coming back to.

Even now, years later, I still love Resident Evil 5 just as much. It brings back a lot of great memories, and I appreciate it even more for what it offered at the time. Whether it was playing through the story or teaming up with someone, it’s a game that has stuck with me and remains one of my all-time favorites

Now this review is a bit biased since I played this game by my lonesome but holy hell does this thing show its age in sections.

You have the classic obscenely tanky enemies, 500 enemies in tight spaces but with the addition of a terrible inventory system that leaves you discarding 90% of the stuff you find. This game also LOVES its one-shots. I do not :(

My biggest gripe with this game though is how nothing happens for around 80% of the story. Why is this the case? no clue but you don't even really have a lead to go off of other than "get to some guy so he can tell you SOME info". it's just really really boring and the characters do not help it. They're just passable, but in a game that's already incredibly boring, they do not help me not fall asleep at all.

The bossfights here are actually pretty cool but the first uroboros fight is bugged to the point where I had to sell my entire inventory just to get a rocket launcher to insta-kill it. The good thing is that this game is probably really fun in co-op. I mean the levels that don't completely suck are actually fairly imaginative and a joy to play through.

I hope we get a remake of this soon since the last parts of the game are genuinely great.

Ну что ж. Пришло время собрать воедино мои воспоминания и попробовать описать, что же я получил в процессе прохождения этого замечательного программного продукта под названием [b]Resident Evil 5[/b].

Проходил я игру вместе с другом, потому что мне было интересно, как вообще разработчики планировали реализовать геймплей для двоих и как это должно ощущаться на практике. Что ж, могу однозначно подтвердить: [b]играть в неё в одиночку — не лучшее решение[/b].

Пока что просто выкачу свои мысли и расскажу, что мне зашло, а что нет в этой части на сложности [b]«Ветеран»[/b].

[hr][/hr]

Самым главным препятствием в этой игре будет скука. Потому что как только ты осваиваешься с управлением, которое на персональных компьютерах выглядит довольно непривычно для современного игрока, игра превращается в попытку побегать по локации, быстро собрать предметы и расходники, а затем начать разваливать кабины окружающих тебя заражённых африканцев.

И сам процесс разваливания кабин лично для меня не является чем-то неприятным ровно до того момента, пока не возникает ситуация, которая провоцирует тебя не на выживание, а на методичный отстрел всего, что движется.

[hr][/hr]

Причём если в начале игры мы с товарищем долго пытались освоить способы выживания в закрытых локациях, пока вокруг нас постоянно спавнились волны противников, то к концу прохождения уже просто чётко разделили обязанности. Например, я ходил со снайперской винтовкой и револьвером, а он — с пистолетом-пулемётом и дробовиком. Его задачей было отстреливать всё, что подходит близко, моей — устранять дальние угрозы и противников, у которых можно было легко вычислить уязвимое место и получить повышенный критический урон.

Как оказалось, попытка выживания в подобных ситуациях со временем приводит к тому, что спавнящиеся жирные противники начинают вызывать лишь уныние, и вы уже банально от скуки вдвоём начинаете сами искать себе приключения.

[hr][/hr]

Единственным таким приключением во второй половине игры стали два пулемётчика на корабле, которые заставили нас немного подумать и изрядно пострадать от перезапусков. Пушечное мясо очень легко рассредотачивало наше внимание, а способа надёжно контролировать противников и не подпускать их к нашим тушкам мы тогда придумать не смогли.

И эта ситуация возникла только потому, что мы недостаточно хорошо изучили локацию и не сразу поняли, какие вообще возможности для прохождения она предоставляет. Вместо этого мы просто пытались идти напролом и сражаться в лоб.

[hr][/hr]

Самым же смехотворным моментом можно назвать битву с Вескером на островке посреди лавы. Там мы совершенно не поняли задумку геймдизайнера и пытались наносить ему урон до того момента, пока он не взберётся на круговую арену.

Выпустив в него практически весь боезапас, пытаясь приманить его к краю платформы и думая, что там должен сработать какой-то сюжетный триггер для сбрасывания босса в лаву, мы всё-таки додумались подняться на арену, заскриптовать кат-сцену и осознать, что только что потратили абсолютно весь боезапас и теперь нам банально нечем его убивать.

В итоге первое прохождение закончилось тем, что мы забивали финального босса ножом.

[b]Ну и забили.[/b]

[hr][/hr]

Если попытаться подытожить, то [b]Resident Evil 5[/b] для меня оказалась игрой, которая почти полностью держится на кооперативе. Не на хорроре, не на выживании, не на напряжении и даже не на постановке, а именно на том, что рядом с тобой есть живой человек.

И в этом, кажется, главный парадокс этой части. Как самостоятельное приключение она довольно быстро превращается в скучную мясорубку. Но как совместное прохождение она всё-таки оставляет после себя набор идиотских, смешных и странно запоминающихся ситуаций.

a lot of people point out that this game leans too much into action and not enough into survival horror, and i think thats a fair analysis, it feels like a collection of mediocre boss fights strung together with an interesting setting, and a few wave-based combat sections in between. however, despite not really having any horror elements present, it still feels distinctly resident evil in how it plays

the bigger issue in my opinion is how poorly executed it all feels

despite releasing on pc, this doesn't feel like something designed with one in mind. the sensitivity is an absolute nightmare, somehow it manages to be both too slow and too fast simultaneously, depending on the action you are trying to do, and there's no way to fine tune one without making the other feel even worse

the boss fights seem to have one-maybe-two stages designed for them each, and in a lot of cases they will repeat those stages 3-5 times with no clear sign of progression until suddenly the fight is over. in the event of a fight with a giant crab creature, there is a specific action you are expected to take to progress, and if you don't do it / don't do it in the way the game specifically wants, you will just cycle through endlessly (i burned around 20 minutes doing this before finally figuring it out by accidentally stumbling upon a hidden button prompt)

and a lot of the combat encounters just feel bad, about an hour ago i went through a hallway with like 20 lickers that felt designed purely to eat up all the extra ammunition i gained in the section prior, and it just felt lame! had it been skipped and a few extra enemies been placed in the room following, the overall experience would have been improved


despite this, i do think the game offers a good experience for the most part, and there's something to be said about the RE charm that keeps drawing me back into this, it all just kind of falls into place with a compelling enough story and enough little environmental puzzles to make it feel worth pushing through some of the jank

it is also becoming incredibly apparent to me the more i play that this game in particular had a strong impact on how The Evil Within series came into existence - TEW2 is one of my favourite survival horror releases and i belive it shares a lot of DNA with RE5

[h1] SEVEN MINUTES. SEVEN MINUTES IS ALL I CAN SPARE TO PLAY RESIDENT EVIL 5 [/h1]


[b]Resident Evil 5[/b] is one of those games I had been hyped for forever but never actually got the chance to experience until now. A huge reason for that was Albert Wesker. Seeing one of the series’ most iconic characters make his grand return here immediately made this game feel important to me, especially after learning more about the lore from the previous titles.

Lately, I’ve basically been having a full “Resident Evil month,” sitting down and playing through the series one game after another nonstop. Sometimes I even managed to finish some of the shorter titles within a single day. Going through the franchise this way has honestly been a really fun experience because you start noticing how everything connects together over time.

That being said, I still ran into the same issue I had with several of the older [b]Resident Evil[/b]  games - the tank controls and outdated gameplay mechanics. I understand these games were revolutionary back in the day, but by modern standards some of the movement and combat systems can feel clunky and difficult to fully enjoy, at least for me personally.

What really kept me invested was the lore. I loved learning more about the origins of the T-Virus and how everything traces back to the Progenitor Virus and the African flower. Seeing how human greed, corruption, and obsession twisted scientific discoveries into biological weapons was genuinely interesting. The [b]Resident Evil[/b] universe always does a great job showing how different people keep creating their own variations of these viruses in pursuit of power and control - and somehow everything eventually turns into another horrifying giant mutation you have to put down by the end.

What also makes Wesker such an entertaining villain is how he constantly feels like he’s behind everything in the [b]Resident Evil[/b] universe. The man is basically collecting viruses like Infinity Stones, always chasing the next ultimate lifeform or world-ending weapon while secretly plotting something in the background.
Despite some outdated mechanics, I still had a really good time with [b]RE5[/b]. The action, the co-op focus, the story connections, and especially Wesker’s presence made the experience memorable. And honestly, after seeing what Capcom achieved with the recent remakes, this is another title that feels like it has massive potential for a modern reimagining.

I know I’ve mentioned remakes many times throughout my [b]Resident Evil[/b] reviews, but I genuinely believe this is the best way for newer players to experience these stories. The originals already have something special at their core - the remakes simply help bring that potential fully to life!
And in the end, Mr. Wesker really gave us seven minutes of pure suffering just to leave afterward and call us “disappointing” like we ruined his evening... :((

Pretty rough, conceptually, and execution-wise. 

Aside from co-op, which in 2009 was not a novel thing at all, hardly even for Resident Evil, given Outbreak 1 & 2, this game has nothing going for it. 

If you think a piss-filter shanty-town generic third person shooter with sub-par design and controls for the time sounds fun, then pick this one up on sale. I played for the first time with a friend a few years back, same with RE6, and under the ideal use-case (with a friend), the game was still boring and mid and we both felt validated in skipping it back in the day.

Story & Characters:
It's an RE game. Both pretty bullshitty, stupid and fun. Just about what you'd expect really, no weird surprises in any good or bad direction. Cheesy dialogue & bad voice acting left and right, with a very funnily overdone story. 6/10

Character Movement & Gameplay Mechanics:
Hasn't aged well to sum it up. I just played the game for the first time and the controls are frustrating at best. I unironically feel like the original Ocarina of Time controlled smoother than this game. But then again: I feel like it really wasn't made for mouse and keyboard at all. I tried a section of the game with controller and it did feel much better!

It feels a bit wasted to upgrade any weapon at all when 2 chapters later you just find a better version of the same weapon or you can just buy a better one. I'm sure that System works much better in ng+, considering most RE games are built for being beat multiple times, but for a single playthrough the weapon system really felt anythign but rewarding. I do like the partner mechanic however. Despite how clunky it actually may be, it often feels oddly fluid. I experienced the entire game in singleplayer, but I'm sure this game is lots of fun when playing as 2. But even here there are many issues. The partner often doesn't move very intuitively. In a bossfight in chapter 6.2 for example Sheva for some reason doesn't move AT ALL unless being told to and even then she will return right back to her spot where she's stuck at. 3.5/10

Combat & Encounter design:
There's many places in this game, where I was very impressed with how combat was designed and euqally as many if not more places, where I was dumbfounded at how not thought through it seemed. Bosses go from "very creative" to "hold you gun into their face for 5 minutes". Regular encounters go from, "shoot two guys" to "This is like 40 enemies. Good luck figuring out which of them drop amo". I would average the score at a 5 here, but the creativity in many places really does save it for me. 6/10

Environmental design:
While not very pretty, the game has tons of diversity when it comes to design, especially considering the game is mostly one singular setting. The environment actually feels well buitlt for and around the game. Many places look and are very "video gamie", but that's just about the only complaint I have. Pretty good actually, but nothing too special. 8/10

Music & Audio:
The music is decent, but nothing special and most weapons don't really feel as impactful when shooting them as they should in my opinion. What I like most is all the sounds the UI makes, but I feel like in most other places the audio could have been better. It's not bad enough to negatively impact the experiece however and the music is still at the very least good in the places where there is music. So still a solid 6/10

Pacing:
This might be a me-issue, but I do NOT feel like I've only played this game for 11 hours. There where many places in the game where I entered a loading zone and just HOPED that the chapter I'm in was finally over.  I don't even feel like the game did have bad pacing however. I do still feel like many chapters could have had a couple rooms less at the very least. Many encounters did drag the game a lot. I do have to complimnt the checkpoint system however. They always do feel very well places. 5.5/10

Over all I feel like the age of the game is holding back many of it's aspects, but despite me really not enjoying MANY parts of the game, I would never say it's a bad game. The characters and world around them are very charming and the ideas just work most of the time.

Many many action sequences are downright impressive still, even by todays standards and like any other RE game the replay value is (probably) insane. If tis game ever gets a remake, it will probably be very good. Like this, it's just decent I feel. But likable. 

Total 5.8/10

It's definitely a different Resident Evil game, but still fun overall, playing with a friend adds a lot to the experience.

Resident Evil 5 Base Game 6.5/10

Resident Evil 5 is among my least favorite numbered Resident Evil games, alongside Resident Evil 6 and Resident Evil Village.

Lost in Nightmares DLC 10/10

But anyway, after the success of Resident Evil 4 and its shift in style, Capcom decided to add even more action into RE5 in order to attract a broader audience that didn’t usually play Resident Evil. Commercially, it worked very well. However, the original RE4 had already disappointed many longtime fans because it changed the franchise too much, replacing zombies with armed enemies. Still, RE4 Original managed to keep some horror elements, and the over-the-top action was mostly concentrated in the island section, where enemies carried RPGs and assault rifles — something many fans already found ridiculous and unnecessary.

But RE5 feels like the island section of RE4 stretched across the entire game. The action is exaggerated and excessive, almost like a Gears of War game.

So what are the negative points of this game in my opinion?

• Too much action
• Horrible yellow filter
• Almost entirely during daytime
• Very few traditional B.O.W.s
• An interesting plot that wasn’t fully explored

I honestly believe RE5 had the potential to become one of the best games in the franchise, because it’s one of the first Resident Evil games, alongside CODE: Veronica, that tried to develop a more elaborate storyline. Even though RE4 is extremely famous, its story is still very cliché — an agent rescuing the president’s daughter.

Here, however, you continue Wesker’s and Umbrella’s storylines. We finally see that Spencer was a complete lunatic, and I think the entire plot is extremely important to the overall lore. 

Contains Resident Evil Requiem spoilers:
[spoiler]It even shows that Spencer remained arrogant right up until his death, which at first seems to contradict the events of Resident Evil Requiem. It would make sense if Spencer truly wanted revenge against Wesker and The Connections by using Elpis to destroy the B.O.W.s and everything tied to their betrayal, but that’s a topic better left for Resident Evil Requiem itself.[/spoiler]


Things that could be improved in a Remake:

I believe Capcom always wanted to make it clear that we would never have something like Resident Evil 1 again in terms of pure horror, and that action was there to stay. I also think action itself doesn’t necessarily betray the franchise’s identity, especially since in this game we are facing Umbrella’s most successful experiment: Albert Wesker.

Honestly, I think this game had everything it needed to surpass Resident Evil 4, but some decisions simply weren’t handled very well.

Why the Lost in Nightmares DLC 10/10

And this is exactly why I think the Lost in Nightmares DLC is one of the best parts of Resident Evil 5.
For me, this DLC feels much more like classic Resident Evil than the main campaign itself. The atmosphere inside the Spencer Mansion, the darker environments, the slower pacing, the exploration, the puzzles, and the constant tension reminded me a lot of Resident Evil 1 Remake.

Ironically, I think Lost in Nightmares proves that Capcom still knew perfectly well how to create survival horror during the action era of Resident Evil. That’s why I always feel a little disappointed with the direction RE5 took, because this DLC clearly shows the potential the game had.

I honestly loved the mansion sections. The silence, the lighting, the creepy atmosphere, and the feeling of isolation made the experience feel genuinely tense again. Even Wesker himself feels more intimidating here because the game is not constantly throwing explosions and action sequences at you every five minutes.

I also think this DLC is extremely important for the lore. Seeing Spencer, learning more about his ideology, and witnessing the final confrontation between him and Wesker gave much more emotional and narrative weight to the story. It made Umbrella’s downfall and Wesker’s evolution feel far more meaningful.

For me, Lost in Nightmares feels like a glimpse of what Resident Evil 5 could have been if Capcom had balanced horror and action more carefully. I’m not against action in Resident Evil, especially when the main antagonist is Albert Wesker, Umbrella’s most successful experiment. But I think the main campaign relied too much on nonstop action, while the DLC showed a much better balance between tension, atmosphere, exploration, and combat.

Honestly, if the entire game had followed the tone of Lost in Nightmares while still keeping Wesker as the central villain, I truly believe Resident Evil 5 could have surpassed Resident Evil 4 and become one of the greatest games in the franchise.

Resident Evil 5 — Great Story, Frustrating Controls

I’ve always loved the Resident Evil series for its lore and storyline, but Resident Evil 5 is one of the hardest entries for me to actually enjoy playing.

The game itself has a lot going for it — memorable characters, co-op gameplay, and an important place in the overall story of the franchise. It also keeps that action-horror atmosphere the series became known for during that era.

But the controls… they feel terrible today. Movement and aiming are clunky and awkward, making combat more frustrating than fun at times. Instead of feeling tense in a good survival-horror way, the gameplay often just feels uncomfortable.

I still appreciate Resident Evil 5 for its story and its place in the series, but the outdated controls make it difficult to return to nowadays.

I want you to know that this will (and probably IS) a good game in the series. But it's age is not doing it a favor to say the least.

Look, RE6 is 13 years old now, and if we go by graphics, they were and still are appealing to me, but of course, they are battling against time, which is unforgiving.

Then the controls. There is a tutorial where you can learn the controls, but they are not really binded good on default. And sometimes I don't really feel in control of the character, as it's just doing it's own thing.

I'm gonna play through some campaigns of it, because it'S not TOO bad, but RE6 needs a remake NOW.
As mush as I wanted to say that re6 version Leon is my fav, I would like to dedicate this review to the forever goat Piers. [spoiler]rest easy buddy.[/spoiler]

RE6 has to be the worst entry in this revered series, but it is still the one I've had the most fun with, mostly due to the amount of time I spent yelling [b]"HELENAAAAAAA"[/b]! xD That aspect aside, there were just sooooooo many memorable moments that I won't soon forget.

In our first session, I watched Leon get bullseye targeted by a heat-seeking missile-ambulance that came out of nowhere... YOU JUST HAD TO SEE IT OKAY!? Even the silliest things like dropping from the top of a ladder onto Leon's toe and watching him stumble back 10 feet, having AI-controller Chris throw himself onto Sherry whilst she was climbing the ladder taking her clean out, or the time Leon decided he is strong enough to absorb a hail of bullets to protect Ada... BULLETS DUDE!!! God, how the heck was this game marketed as Resident Evil hahaha? It was just too much all the time, literally!

Getting crushed by a tank twice? Check. Being chainsawed into pieces by the same boss ten times prompting a rage quit? Check. Being too slow to hide in a box so the boss just stands and watches your entry animation from 1m away whilst aura farming before utterly decimating you? Check, Lol. Even sprinting up an icy hill ended with me in stitches > a sniper laser suddenly trained on me > dove to dodge the shot > immediately found myself sledding 100m back down the ice, almost taking out my partner in the process haha. Or even the time I thought a camera turret was an enemy, when it was actually just my friend trolling, in control and shooting at me.

And then there was the snowmobile section where I elected to admire the beautiful scenery (a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ avalanche Lol), and 2 seconds later found myself having driven straight off a cliff [i](eyes on the road at all times, FFS Jerome)[/i]!!! Can't forget the motorbike chase where I was too busy giving multiple hugs to bother shooting the horde of zombies, or that time I became Darth Sidious and, because of this game's god-awful camera angle transitions, ended up firing my lightning strike about 100 degrees off target—which somehow led to the floor collapsing and killing us both as punishment, Lol.

There's no way I would've enjoyed this in single-player—absolutely none. This is a co-op game in every single way. Without a friend, the dated controls, repetitive missions, and jank would make anyone quit within an hour, but with someone else along for the ride, I promise you'll have one of the funniest gaming experiences in years.

Resident Evil 6 is at this point only famous for being cheeks, deemed almost downright unplayable by critics for years. I'm here today to tell you, that those critics are right... and also friendless losers.

Yes this game is bad, undoubtedly so. All of the 4 (yes, 4) different campaigns all have glaring gameplay and story problems- It's so ambitious it's laughable. 

However, playing this piece of ♥♥♥♥ with my little brother has been some of the most fun gaming I have ever had, and because of that, I am now the proud owner of a copy of this game with the secret cutscene unlocked, I have truly peaked. 

If you, like the critics, are a friendless loser, do not- EVER- purchase this game. But if you, like me, have no job and a little brother with too much free time, what are you still doing reading this review. Go crazy.

The shift toward action didn't do this game any favors. It still feels just as clunky to control as the previous two entries, except now it's missing the things that made them interesting (at least in my opinion): inventory management, merchants, and the horror atmosphere

The co-op is decent (ALTHOUGH THERE ARE BUGS IN SHERRY BIRKIN'S CAMPAIGN!!!), with the exception of Ada's campaign. Your partner is basically a nobody who doesn't exist in cutscenes and can't even open a damn door

In short, every family has its black sheep. You can safely skip this crap

[h1] 6/10 mid af. Only worth playing with a friend so they can suffer with you. [/h1]

Resident Evil 6 is a decent co-op experience featuring 4 character campaigns. They're all 5 chapters in length and interwoven. The quality between these 4 campaigns are very inconsistent, making two of them only worth playing. The game's flaws severely drag it down, such as the trash inventory system, but it's still playable. The combat in RE6 is very fun because of the unique movement and gunplay mechanics. Each character has their own loadout preferences and melee combos, which keeps the gameplay loop fresh. 

[h1] Leon & Helena Campaign 1/10 [/h1] 
Everything wrong with this game is present in Leon's route. This SH1TTY campaign takes 7 hours to beat! It features, abysmal boss fights, AWFUL pacing, running segments, terrible storytelling, and trash level & enemy design. This route seriously feels like L4D and GOW mashed together. To make matters worse Leon's (2005) character was assassinated, and Helena is just some bland plot device that's difficult to care for.[spoiler]Leon is unfunny, miserable, and physically weaker.[/spoiler] Easily the worst route in the game, the only good part is the frame that triggers the ending. Genuinely the worst thing I've played in my life.

[h1] Chris & Piers Campaign 7/10 [/h1] 
Very solid campaign, it's one I recommend playing. Piers is a goated character, but chapter 4 and 5 are rough pacing wise. Chapter 5 has trash running segments and the final boss can be difficult to beat, it requires great resource management and coordination to take down. This campaign gets very emotional and is essential to Chris's character development. (4-5 hours playtime)

[h1] Jake & Sherry Campaign 7/10 [/h1] 
The most fun route to experience, great pacing highly recommend playing it. This takes around 3-4 hours to finish. A BOW that behaves like Nemesis chases you throughout the entire story. Jake and Sherry are well written characters and their story has clear objectives that are easy to follow. This route unfortunately has the worst driving and stealth segments.

[h1] Ada Campaign 4/10 [/h1] 
Has puzzles and stealth segments. it's the shortest campaign in the game. Low rating because you have to deal with Leon's bs at the end plus, the campaign lacks replay value.

TL;DR: Action packed co-op experience featuring aggressive combat. Only play Chris and Sherry campaign. Mercenaries is excellent too, worth buying if on sale.

[h1] Infamously Mid Together, Infamously Bad Alone [/h1]
If you're an RE fan, or even just someone who knows the reputation of this game, you probably know this game for being simultaneously a Michael Bay film, Mission Impossible, a GOW wannabe, a COD clone, and hundreds of other things. It is indisputably the black sheep of the whole franchise. And as a matter of fact, it is exactly that. At it's conception, RE6 was the most ambitious and unique successor of all it's predecessors.  There's 40 hours of content in the campaign, split into 4 different POVs, split into 4 different playable characters, split into 8 different branching storylines with its own plot points, characters and events, all blended eventually into one drink at the climax of the game (China) (yes, seriously), all united with random chase scenes that happen every other 10 minutes, a QTE almost everywhere you go, and cuts from gameplay to cutscene at the end of every checkpoint, and of course, an on rails section shooting gallery every other chapter ending. The best way to ultimately critique the game, ultimately, is on three major metrics:

[h2] Story [/h2]
The story of RE6 is definitely one of the stories of all time. In fact, it is a video game story you can play. There are plot points. All those things exist, and are executed on. The problem is, of course, that the chef went from starting to cook to being shut down by the FDA for crafting whatever it is this story is trying to convey. The story of RE6 is one that is both simultaneously happening to all characters at the same time and also happening to only one specific group of characters. Unsurprisingly, it ended up being a complete and total disaster. For example, Leon's story consists of doing classical RE work of shooting freaks in a infected, presidential summit, to then killing the literal president, then proceeding to find a cathedral, in which you discover a missing sister who mutates to a naked woman, to crashing a Boeing 737 in the middle of a Chinese street, to fighting in a moving subway, then being eventually lead to a giant skyscraper where you fight a literal dinosaur that mutates into a fly. It's the story a 9 year old kid would make for a creative writing class, rather than anything you could legitimately take seriously, or try to immerse yourself in. Oh, and don't forget to add the part where technically, as you do all those things, the other playable characters (Chris, Jake, Ada) are all doing their own thing simultaneously which is affecting Leon's plot. In the most concise way to conclude this point, it's a giant mess that combines pretty much all the plot points you can think studios were adding back in 2010s action games. A fight with a literal God in Chris's campaign (yes, seriously), incredible plot armor from literally every protagonist, and a whole ass cloning plotline from Ada's POV, zombies, and a world ending threat. And none of it is compelling on any single character. In an attempt to make a one of a kind story, it ended up being a blended up, nonsensical Wattpad fanfic that takes everything that is happening far too seriously to ever be even seen unironically. While Chris is carpet bombing Neo Umbrella forces from an F16 in an Aircraft Carrier in China who is ready to launch a Biological ICBM, Ada is sneaking around in a submarine, Jake is LARPing MW2 Cliffhanger, and Leon is playing Temple Run. It's ridiculous. 

[h2] Gameplay [/h2]
To play RE6 is to play elements that were meant for another game. You can do cartwheels, take cover, fall on your back and roll around on the floor, run and slide, parry, do signature executions on downed enemies, and if your playing with Jake, run at dudes and use your fists on them. As unique and special as those are (especially here), it does the game no real favors. Of what good is adding a Gears of War 3rd person cover system, if it is practically useless, aside from the hardest difficulty of the game? (Because the zombies (with guns for some reason) one shot you there.) Of what use is being allowed to roll on the floor, when it does nothing for you? (You still get hit if you roll around on the floor, so by rolling around, you just get yourself hit for free.) Of what use is being allowed to do a 'quick shot' by quickly spamming your left and right triggers? (you can still miss with it, and it does no more damage or anything special more than you shooting normally would). You get my point. There's a ton of these one-off odd additions that don't really add much for your standard, normal gameplay. There's a Stamina system (that is redundant, because you have infinite sprint, and only serves to force your character become unable to pistol whip (which does pitiful damage), or use melee weapons, which only Leon, Jake, Chris, Sherry have, so if you play coop, and play as any of the side characters which are not these, you legit have no means to defend yourself if you run out of ammo). You randomly trip over (YES IM SERIOUS) over dead bodies if you sprint over them, limping for a few seconds, or you can also trip over yourself if your partner in the story lands near you. Again, why does this exist? How does this improve the gameplay? Good question. On top of all this, you have no inventory system, no crafting, just a 'row' of vertical slots that fill out with ammo and weapons, and maybe you will throw away some grenades or the worthless remote explosives to make space for more ammo. And the cherry on top, healing alone in this game is its own QTE. You have to select the green herb, put it in a case, AND THEN be able to heal. Why not just be able to click a keybind instantly from the heals you have and heal instantly? Also good question. Then, of course, there's the hundreds of QTEs that snap you out of gameplay, the infamous gameplay-cut-to-cutscenes, which can sometimes snap into a 1s instant QTE, or a random chase scene. And if you haven't had enough of that, there is no actual pause menu. Instead, you pull out your phone (in real time, so you can still get attacked), and instead pausing is it's own, seperate keybind. In short, the gameplay is a mix of random additions, mediocre 3rd person shooting, and bizarre design elements. 

[h2] Coop[/h2]
Probably the only real reason anyone should play this, this and RE5 are the only real coop games one can play with your buddies. This certainly takes the cake, though as a 'so-bad-its-good' mood. Though, do not expect RE5 Sheva-Chris levels of Coop, as Capcom doesn't even let you share weapons, ammo, or heals, or any of the sort. (And Ada's campaign is literally broken in coop.) The most that you can do with your buddy is healing next to him, which heals him like it did in RE5. It is a journey of bizarre expectations at seeing what batsh*t crazy stuff characters will do. Will we get another instant QTE here? Will we fight a god underwater with Electro powers? Will we copilot F-16's and drop Cluster bombs on Chinese Aircraft Carriers? That is where this game shines.

[h3] Conclusion- its mid [/h3]
In short, RE6 isn't necessarily bad. It's just mid. The shooting is competent. The gameplay is tolerable, if mediocre/boring after you do one campaign. The story is laughably bad, but if you take it with a "The Room" flavor, then you can probably swallow it. Nobody in their right mind should pay 19.99$ for it, however. If you can somehow gaslight someone to play this game fully, both buy it on sale, and you both can tolerate the journey, then it's a good bit of cheap fun.

RE6 is all over the place.
The quick time events are awful.
Anything involving driving a vehicle is frustrating.
The chase sequences are over done.
The story is simultaneously stupid and taken way too seriously.
The attempts of horror are pathetic. 
Inventory management feels out of place and annoying since this is an action game through and through. 
The skill tree system didn't feel like it added much to the gameplay. 
Character movement and aiming (with a controller) felt a little floaty.
The cover system is kind of pointless and weird to use.
The herb system is also weird.
Yet despite all these criticisms the combat in this game can be a great time. 
There's a surprising amount of depth to it. 
Unfortunately the combat doesn't get to shine fully in the campaign.
In the mercenaries mode however it gets to show its true potential.
If you've enjoy the more action oriented RE titles my advice would be to play through the campaigns once then jump into mercenaries and see why RE6 has a small passionate group of defenders. 
Hot take but with the success of the RE4 Remake and Leon sections in RE9 Capcom has shown they've perfected the action horror that they attempted to do here and I'd love to see them remake RE6 with the experience they have now.

My first Resident Evil game that I bought and finished. When I played it on the switch mid 2023, I instantly fell in love with the series. This game inspired me to get Resident Evil 5, which I also adore.

Under almost no circumstances should you ever play this game. Whether you’re a new RE fan or if you’re just curious about this game for whatever reason, it’s best to just read the cliff notes version of this game’s story and move on to something else. Even if you have a friend willing to play any coop game with you, just don’t. It’s not even serviceable as an experience so bad that it’s funny. As a matter of fact, this likely is the only game in the history of the medium that is somehow not elevated by the simple inclusion of coop.

I unfortunately dragged a poor soul to play this game with me thinking the game might be at least fun as a coop experience. Boy, was I wrong.

There are 4 different campaigns in this game, with each having returning characters being accompanied by a new face. Leon is accompanied by Helena, Chris with Piers, Sherry with Jake, and Ada with a figment of her imagination. They all intersect with each other’s storylines at certain points, which is fine. Ambitious even. But playing each campaign individually just feels like a slogfest, where most chapters just feel like they drag on needlessly and overstay their welcome. Often times, the gameplay got interrupted every 10 seconds (no really, sometimes the game took us off a cutscene and then put us back into a new one as soon we take 5 steps forward) just to show a pointless cutscene, like maybe the camera pointing at an obvious path. The characters themselves just often make questionable choices, feel out of character sometimes (Leon in most of his campaign), or just have a moment of awkwardness. For example, [spoiler] there is a moment in Sherry’s campaign where she gets rescued by Ada Wong just as Sherry was facing certain death and gets reunited with Jake. Ada Wong swings away like SpiderMan. Both Sherry and Jake proceed to never acknowledge that ever happening and simply continue the story without even saying so much as a simple “thank you” or even questioning anything that just happened. It’s like nothing ever happened. [/spoiler]

As for the regular gameplay, this game marks the first time in the mainline series where you can aim and move. Unfortunately, that’s where the positives end. Shooting at enemies sometimes does not feel responsive whatsoever. About 75% of the enemies just transform after killing them the first time, in which case they either gain a very specific weakness or become bullet sponges. Nonetheless, it just becomes a chore to clear out an area at times. As for QTEs, there is nothing else I can say about the QTEs that hasn’t been said in other reviews. Like what the hell kinda combination is R + Space? What do you mean I only have a femtosecond to press that combination? All I can say is just don’t take your hands off your controller/keyboard if you’re in a cutscene. There are also moments in the campaign (particularly Chris’s second half of the campaign and most of Ada’s) where it is apparent that they were running dry in their budget so they start slowing down the rate of cutscenes shown or just reusing assets/scenes freely. 
(Speaking of Ada’s campaign, it is obvious that it was meant to be a single player experience but then Capcom remembered halfway through developing her campaign that the rest of the game is actually co-op and so they just added some nameless, faceless goon that is somehow able to teleport himself to wherever Ada’s position is. Doesn’t even appear in cutscenes too.)

While all those gameplay features make for some ♥♥♥♥ experiences, the biggest offender by far has to be the escape sequences. In every campaign, there is at least one escape sequence designed with the sole purpose of giving the player an aneurysm. Sometimes the camera angles changes by itself which screws over the direction your character is going, leading to your death. Or there is an obstacle that you’re barely able to see that completely stops your entire momentum, leading to your death. Or the collision with your co-op partner causes you to slow down significantly, leading to your death. Or… you get the point. And they leave little room for mistakes so get ready to restart at least once if it’s a lengthy sequence. Easily the worst escape sequence is in Chris’s campaign when you’re in a harbor and you must run on various platforms nonstop or you get blown up. The game decides to give you such limited space to run that you’ll be constantly bumping into your co-op partner. In addition, if you graze the invisible wall, there’ll be one single polygon of obstruction protruding out from that wall that causes you to stop running entirely. But that’s not all, if you hold the run button/key, you’ll likely mount a ledge… and then dismount that same ledge immediately after. Death. I know that’s only one segment, but that segment singehandedly left such a bitter taste in our mouths that we just felt completely miserable even after getting past that part. 

It should also be noted that if you still do intend to play the game because you’re a masochist, then there are a few bugs that can affect the flow of the game. For example, your cursor can disappear in the inventory menu, making it difficult to operate it. Another example, in Sherry’s campaign (maybe chapter 3 or 4?), Jake has to release the bars to a door so Sherry can proceed into an alley. For whatever reason, this causes Jake to become catatonic when you get him to interact with another prompt, soft locking your game right there. What worked for us was resetting the whole game and giving it another try. If that doesn’t work, try switching characters and give that a try. Alternatively, you can just stop playing the game entirely and play/do something else actually worth your time. Actually, just do that anyways. Watching the cutscenes on Youtube will save your sanity. I mean, this game almost killed off the entire franchise for a reason.

I haven’t even gone more in depth with the other problems this game has but you’ll have to trust me on this when I say avoid this game at all costs. Never in my life have I ever felt so excited to get a game off my SSD.

Well it's better than I thought it was going to be. It's clearly a big budget effort. Whoever directed this game thought they were making the most epic Resident Evil game of all time. If nothing else, I appreciate how the story is structured, and how the 4 separate campaigns provide different perspectives of the same events, and how the cutscenes overlap. There's some talented narrative crafting on display in that respect. I just wish the story itself was more interesting. 

Other than that, the game is boring, not all that scary, too long, and way more of a clunky, frantic action game than an oppressive survival horror game. Leon's campaign feels like a Left 4 Dead knockoff, running from building to building, fighting a zombie outbreak with groups of survivors. But Chris and Sherry's campaigns feel like a bad Call of Duty game where you're stuck in some Eastern European war zone fighting slightly infected soldiers who know how to use gun turrets. You're never alone. You are always well armed. And things are always blowing up. There's flaws in the camera system--it's pulled in too close behind you and a lot of the character movements are too jerky and disorientating. There is far too much screen shake taking place, like every single time a large monster takes a step as it chases you around for 15 minutes which makes the game a pain to look at. And the checkpoint system is confusing. Not all checkpoints are saved, so I might be stuck in an area, dying repeatedly, give up for the night, but when I come back to it the next day the game loads further back from my last checkpoint resulting in lost progress. The indicator that an actual save is taking place vs a soft checkpoint isn't obvious at first, but I figured it out at like hour 20 (wait for the "Saving Game" indication as opposed to "Checkpoint Reached"). 

Technically the game looks stunning in spots, with bright neon lighting and great character models that rival the remakes, but in other areas things look comedically bad with flat textures meant to represent clothes on a clothesline or food on a kitchen counter. Also, for some damned reason, on my Bazzite PC the game wanted to download a full 4gb shader file every single time i booted my PC up. Super annoying. 

Only recommended for the biggest Resident Evil fans. Which I guess I am. I had some fun with it.

I really loved watching this game as I was playing it. Truly one of the best experiences I have had playing a Resident Evil game. RE6 > RE9. That stupid Grace girl is always stuttering and it really gets on my nerves, unlike the CHAD PIERECE MORGAN (chris's companion).

The controls are slightly better than in RE5, but I realized one thing: for older Resident Evil games (like RE1, RE5, RE6), it's better to play on an easier difficulty until we get proper remakes with modern and comfortable controls.

If you don't want to suffer because of the old gameplay mechanics and just want to enjoy the story, I recommend playing on Easy difficulty. It allows you to focus on the atmosphere, characters, and the overall experience instead of fighting the controls.

Overall, the game is great. It has a fun story, everything feels like classic Resident Evil, and compared to RE5, this game finally brings back some horror elements instead of being just a pure action shooter.

While I do think this game is super action focused and less like a resident evil game, I had fun regardless. This game is really enjoyable to play with friends on co-op and its better that way than with the AI. Encountering the other playable characters mid game is also pretty cool and you can feel the plot start coming together when you play all the campaigns. 
The controls in this game are pretty confusing though, they seriously overcomplicated the inventory menu, and its also annoying how you can't pause the game at all when you try to get out the settings menu since you can still get attacked with everything in the background continuing as normal. You'll also get a lot of dramatic action sequences where they force fixed camera angles for visual effects or even a moving camera which is a nightmare to control when you're suddenly being chased.
Opinion on each campaign:
1. Leon & Helena:
The beginning part of this felt the most like a resident evil game at the university they escape. It has a decent story but the section in the caves tends to really drag, it becomes more interesting once leon and helena make it to China, but the action part of the game started to take over and feels more like I'm playing Rush Hour 2. All in all, I give it 6.5/10
2. Chris and Piers:
So far I have been the most frustrated with this campaign. This feels more like I'm playing Call of Duty, and compared to Leons campaign, this had far more long crazy action sequences that each took literally 15 tries each to complete while playing on co-op, like chasing Ada in a car, running on a boat yard before the panels collapse, running from a giant water monster before the balcony pieces collapse. The controls made them especially unbearable to finish.
The only redeeming quality I would say is the emotional ending with piers with the loss of Chris' other men, and a couple of fun bosses to fight. But all in all I wouldn't want to return to this one. 4/10
Haven't played Sherry and Ada's campaigns yet, but I definitely think this game is worth playing, and I recommend doing so with another friend.

The multiplayer and bonus gamemodes is where this game really shines.
You have Mercenaries which is an action packed kill everything in the time limit mode which Resi fans know all too well. It plays super well in Resi 6, almost like thats what the gameplay was actually centered around instead of the campaign.
Survivors is a weird FFA where 6 players load in and try to be the last one standing. Dead players come back to life as a zombie and if they kill a survivor they respawn and get a second chance to be the last one standing.
Predator is a 1v5 gamemode where one player takes the role of the Ustunak who is basically a massive tyrant. The 5 survivors must work together to kill him and the more carnage the Ustunak causes the more points they gain. If a survivor is downed they lose points. Each player gets a turn to get the most points as Ustunak and to keep them when they become a survivor.
Onslaught is a 1v1 gamemode that at its core is just a 1v1 Mercenaries gamemode. Both players must keep up combos to send enemies to their opponents side. The winner is decided by who got the most score by killing enemies or whoever is the last man standing.
Lastly we have Siege, which is my least favourite gamemode just because I couldnt get balanced teams as i could only get 4 friends on. Siege is a 3v3 gamemode between BOWs and Agents. The Agents are tasked with protecting an NPC BSSA Agent while the BOWs try to kill them. The teams then swap depending on if the BOWs took down the NPC or if the Agents actually protected him.

At the end of the day Resident Evil 6 was let down by its campaign. The multiplayer and bonus modes are the real meat on its bones as they all work really well with what the devs actually designed in its gameplay. Its supposed to be chaotic action.

It's no secret that I enjoy myself a good Resident Evil game every now and then. It's not my favorite Capcom IP per se, but the unique mix of horror and action throughout the franchise, as well as (some) of the characters is what made me stick with the series for so long. Granted, it wasn't until the late 2010s that I actually started playing these games myself, as I watched my mom play them growing up (which would partially explain the guy I am now), but better late than never, as they say. I still need to get my ass in gear and play the classic, more horror-focused titles, but the ones I did play are pretty good. RE4's self explanatory, RE7 is fantastic and honestly the best horror entry in the series, and 8 is also pretty good, etc, etc. Except for this review's subject matter...RE6...

The campaigns make me want to drink myself into a stupor since that'll be the only time they're bearable. Constantly ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ with your movement speed, enemies that take way too many damn bullets to kill even if you engage with the finer mechanics of the combat system to the point it'd make Fallout 3's endgame enemies blush, pointless QTEs in a hollow attempt to make the game more "cinematic" (Granted, RE4 and 5 have these too, so it's not an original sin. It is much worse here, tho), an atrocious tutorial that is much more focused on its Michael Bayisms than it is on teaching you how to play the damn game (On top of it literally being a scene ripped from near the end of Leon's campaign), horridly obtuse UI, ammo starvation (In what is debatably still the most action-heavy RE game in the franchise, btw), and a control scheme that will take getting used to. Characters and story are the usual in this RE, with the only real differences being in how said story is told and how nothing most of said new characters are (Outside of Jake whose mere existence raises so many questions...)

"Gee, Unoilrig, it sounds like you despise the main game with all your heart." And you're absolutely right, schizophrenic voice in my head...BUT there is one shining light in the darkness, and that light is this game's take on the classic Mercenaries mode. Being designed by actual human beings that want you to have fun and experience the admittedly pretty good combat for what it is (The fact that it's the only version of Mercs that's unlocked from the start is telling), it's the only reason why I haven't uninstalled the game. Enemies that normally take a trillion hits to kill have reasonable health values, and the more terribly designed ones (Looking at you, Shrieker) either don't show up at all, or are rare and have said reasonable health values if they do. Nearly every stage is fun to curbstomp zombies/Ja'vo into the concrete in whilst juggling between keeping the clock running and resupplying. While every character shares similar control schemes, they all have unique loadouts, costumes, and wacky-ass wrestling moves to differentiate them.

There is a surprising amount of things you can do here, from dodging an attack and popping a few heads on the ground before getting back up, sliding to break hourglasses without being stuck in a long animation and avoiding those accursed centipede arm guys, pushing enemies off cliffs and into bottomless pits, weapon-specific takedowns for the zombies, quick shots if an enemy is right up your grill, parrying (which funnily enough, became a mainstay in RE4R onward, albiet in a different, knife-oriented way), and...quick-mixing herbs because ♥♥♥♥ the inventory system. I wish Capcom kept this combat system around, polished it into a fine sculpture, and used it in something like a Hunter: The Reckoning game. That'd be ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sick.

Now, does this make up for-HELL NO! Much as I like this take on Mercenaries, it only makes up 10% or less of the total experience, and some problems that plague the campaign carry over into this mode, too. I only really recommend this game if you're looking for a Mercs fix or if you and some unfortunate soul you dragged into this mess hate yourself enough to play through the campaigns in co-op, and even then, only if it's on sale because this game just simply isn't worth the 20 bones Capcom's asking for.

Otherwise, play literally any other Resident Evil game, especially either version of 4 and 5 if you're on team action. I really should write a positive review at some point...

🧟‍♂️💥 Resident Evil 6 Review 💥🧟‍♂️

🕹 Length: 20–30+ hours (all campaigns)
💰 Price vs. Content: Huge amount of content, especially in co-op
⚙ Optimization: Solid overall; runs well on modern systems
🎮 Difficulty: Easy to moderate; more focused on action than survival

🎮 Gameplay

Resident Evil 6 is easily the most divisive mainline game in the franchise. Instead of focusing on horror, it goes fully into over-the-top action, delivering explosions, giant boss fights, cinematic sequences, and nonstop shooting.

The game features 4 different campaigns, each with its own playable characters and tone:

Leon S. Kennedy & Helena Harper → More horror-inspired
Chris Redfield & Piers Nivans → Military action-heavy
Jake Muller & Sherry Birkin → Action/adventure with melee focus
Ada Wong → Solo espionage-style campaign

In theory, the multiple perspectives sound ambitious. In practice… the stories are honestly pretty weak. None of them are particularly memorable, and the game constantly repeats scenes, bosses, and events between campaigns. By the end, you’ve often replayed the same situations from slightly different angles multiple times.

That said, here’s the weird thing: the game becomes genuinely entertaining when played with a friend.

The absurd action, ridiculous plot moments, and chaotic gameplay create a “so bad it’s fun” kind of experience. You’ll laugh at the nonsense, joke during the insane cutscenes, and still have a surprisingly good time because the co-op carries the entire game.

And honestly? That’s the key:
👉 I only recommend Resident Evil 6 if you play it with someone else.

⚠️ Ada Campaign Problem

Ada’s campaign introduces a huge flaw in co-op.

If one player chooses Ada, the second player becomes a generic character called “Agent.” The problem is that Agent barely feels like a real character:

No personality
No autonomy in story moments
Limited interaction in cutscenes
Feels disconnected from the campaign entirely

The player controlling Agent often ends up feeling useless or bored compared to the other campaigns where both characters actually matter.

🔥 Extra Modes

🎭 Mercenaries

The classic mode returns, but in standard Mercenaries the pacing can feel strangely slow. Enemies sometimes appear too spread out, making matches less exciting than they should be.

💀 Mercenaries No Mercy

THIS is where the fun begins.

This mode becomes pure chaos:
✔ Around 10 maps
✔ Massive enemy density
✔ Up to 300 enemies per match
✔ Extremely fast and frenetic gameplay

It’s one of the most entertaining parts of the game and honestly far more fun than standard Mercenaries.

🌐 Other Multiplayer Modes

Resident Evil 6 also launched with extra online modes, but by 2026 many of them are basically empty, making matchmaking difficult unless you organize games with friends.

✅ The Good:
✔ Massive amount of content
✔ Very fun in co-op
✔ 4 campaigns with different characters and gameplay styles
✔ Mercenaries No Mercy is chaotic and addictive
✔ Surprisingly entertaining because of how absurd it gets

❌ The Bad:
✖ Weak and messy storylines
✖ Constantly repeated bosses and scenes
✖ Barely feels like survival horror
✖ Ada co-op is terrible for the second player
✖ Many online modes are inactive today

💻 Technical Aspects

🎨 Graphics: Still decent visually with strong animations and cinematic presentation
🎶 Sound: Good action soundtrack and solid voice acting
🐞 Bugs: Stable overall; no major technical problems today

⭐ Final Verdict

Resident Evil 6 is probably the weakest mainline Resident Evil game — but weirdly, it can still be a lot of fun.

The campaigns are repetitive, the stories aren’t very good, and the game completely abandons horror for Hollywood-style action. But when played with a friend, the absurdity becomes part of the charm. You laugh, suffer through the ridiculous moments together, and somehow end up enjoying the chaos.

Solo? It’s rough.
Co-op? Surprisingly entertaining.

👉 Rating: 6.5/10 (with co-op) 🧟‍♂️💥

Resident Evil 6 is ridiculous, dramatic, messy, and honestly? I had fun.

Is it peak horror? No. Is it subtle? Absolutely not. This game said “Resident Evil, but make it louder,” and then threw explosions, monsters, motorcycle chases, dramatic camera angles, and several emotional men into a blender.

And then there is Jake Muller.

Enough said.

I love Leon Kennedy, obviously. Leon is Leon. The man has earned his place. But Jake gets extra brownie points because he is voiced by Troy Baker, aka my favorite voice actor. And that voice? Listen. I am only human.

Jake’s campaign was easily one of the biggest highlights for me. His attitude, his backstory, his chemistry with Sherry, the whole “angry problem child with complicated bloodline drama” thing. It worked. He had that exact kind of broody, sarcastic, emotionally constipated energy that makes a character way more fun to follow than he probably has any right to be.

The gameplay is chaotic, and sometimes the game feels like it is doing five different things at once, but co-op makes it so much more enjoyable. Running through the campaigns with someone else, yelling through the chaos, trying to survive the absolute nonsense on screen, and pretending the plot is not completely unhinged is part of the charm.

Resident Evil 6 is not the scariest or most focused game in the series, but it is entertaining. It is big, dumb, dramatic action-horror with familiar faces, wild campaigns, and enough chaos to keep things moving.

Also, Jake Muller.

Troy Baker.

[h1]Resident Evil 6[/h1]
[h3]Great Survival Horror Shooter[/h3]
[h3]Worth a try![/h3] 

Resident Evil 6 was the final chapter in the series' action-oriented phase, which began with Resident Evil 4. For many years, this game stood as the conclusion of the main characters and events that had been building up throughout the series. Honestly, its narrative may not be the game's strongest aspect, as it occasionally becomes excessively exaggerated and chaotic, but the closure this game gives to its characters is surprisingly well done, making it a rewarding and fun experience nonetheless.

[h3]Gameplay[/h3]
This title excels at delivering fast-paced action gameplay, alongside new combat mechanics and a greatly expanded movement system that grants more freedom and flexibility while exploring and engaging enemies. The addition of evasive rolls, quick shots, crawling, and cover-based shooting addressed one of the series' most common issues, which was the restrictive movement and lack of flexibility during combat. It is an aspect that often goes underappreciated despite having a significant impact on the overall gameplay experience. 

The interconnected multi-campaign structure felt unique and very ambitious. However, it could have been better by making character interactions feel more organic and less reliant on coincidences, while avoiding its frequent deus ex machina moments or fan service scenes. On the other hand, it blends many elements from previous entries, such as quick-time events, inventory management, cooperative gameplay, melee combat, puzzle-solving events, and the over-the-shoulder shooting aiming introduced since Resident Evil 4.

Resident Evil 6 has arguably the best Mercenaries mode in the series, as it makes excellent use of the game's enhanced mobility and combat systems, making every match feel fast, enjoyable and engaging. 

[h3]Art and World Design[/h3]
The redesigns of returning characters were one of the game's strengths, as their more mature and restrained appearances helped reinforce the passage of time and the sense that these characters had continued to change and grow off-screen. 

Besides that, the enemy design is really outstanding, as it mixes grotesque biological mutations with disturbingly familiar human features. This results in an interesting variety of mutants and semi-human enemies to encounter. The former are driven by a more aggressive nature, while the latter possess more sophisticated AI capable of coordinating  attacks, setting up ambushes, and overwhelming the player in groups, something that was rarely seen in previous entries.

Then there's the chapter and level design. Each chapter lasts approximately an hour and takes players through a wide variety of locations with straightforward puzzles, new enemies, and weapons. The game's atmosphere is remarkably good, featuring realistic graphics, better lighting effects, unique landmarks, detailed environments, and environmental storytelling that help it stand out from the previous entries.

[h3]Music and Sound effects[/h3]
The soundtrack is perhaps the least memorable aspect of the game. Honestly, it features very few tracks that truly stand out, with the Mercenaries theme being a notable exception thanks to its unique and catchy nature. On the other hand, the sound design remains just as strong and consistent as that of its action-oriented predecessors.

This is the most fun I've ever had playing a videogame. 

The gameplay and related mechanics were enjoyable. You have a variety of dodge buttons, plus the option to simply sprint to dodge. Handling the inventory space is not confusing, though not a tetris game either. I do like tetris, but for a game intended to be played with a partner, the simple two-layer (one for weapons/heals & one for hand-held explosives) interface they designed just made sense. They really prioritized speed and accessablity, and it shows. Quick-time events the game pushes on you were difficult when you weren't expecting them, but did not break immersion or cause further frustration and instead added a funny challenge. There were also twin prompts that needed both players, which I'm glad were added. I need to know which one of the main characters is the macho and which one gets to be pillow princessed up and around. That Piers guy was glad too, I'm sure. Driving in this game, like in many games, just required for you to get used to the controls and it was smooth sailing from there. The boss fights were typical boss fights, where you had to observe attack patterns and weakspots to get to the gist of it. The only exception to that was Leon's final boss. Still don't know how I beat that guy.

For the best playthrough experience, this should definitely be played with a partner. I enjoyed this game immensely because I got to play with a friend who was as invested as I was in icons like Leon, Ada and Chris, but also ready to explore new faces like Piers Nivans and Helena Harper. The way the stories overlapped and certain cutscenes were repeated were always a point of compelling discussion and we both loved putting our on spin on the way our characters interacted between cutscenes. Not only was our friendship a catalyst for the best possible playthrough ever, we grew even closer because of it. Your enjoyment of this game might rely purely on who you're playing with.

The way the fixed camera angles were positioned during a few escape scenes made little sense and erred off the flow of the story. Namely these 2 times: 
In Chris's campaign at the very end, while running away from the final boss up a spiraling platform. Suddenly having to switch for forward W to backward S because the developers decided this would be a cool shot of the boss (which I'm only assuming happened) got me killed more times than I can count.
In Jake's campaign Jake and Sherry have to run away from Ustanak in a straight line with the camera positioned in front of them, facing them and the monster behind them. That scene ends with them having to climb a ladder that was out of the frame because of the perspective. Or would end, if you first found that ladder.
But this is really not a big deal. 8/10 times everything controlled nicely or it was just a matter of endurance.

It's.. peculiar, that despite this being a co-op game, the last campaign was clearly designed to be played alone. In Ada's campaign the extra agent does not show up in cutscenes at all, cannot interact with any doors or puzzles, and does not have to be present when opening up doors leading to loading screens as the secondary protagonist (unlike in every other campaign). He's like Ada's imaginary friend. I played him, and had lots of fun messing around while my friend playing Ada was trying to puzzle the ♥♥♥♥ out of everything. Still, peculiar...

This is not the most cinematic game out there. The graphics are good, but not on the same level as the engine Capcom uses today. The lighting and rendering of the characters look visually very pleasing. It's rather the environments that sometimes feel flat. Overall we didn't encounter any bugs, though the camera freaks out a little if you try looking up while sprinting.

This game was so fun that we had to set a time limit for each session. After playing a game for too long jokes don't flow that easily and we quieted down, and that's not how I wanted to play this game! It was always a pleasure to open up resi6 and get immersed in the story, culture and characters.

I think this game's fun factor is the co-op. In some aspects, it's a good sequel to RE5, but...

Some gameplay decisions, especially the choice of camera angles, make me want to nuke Japan for the 3rd time. The Chris chases are some of the biggest crap I've ever seen in 2010s games. It still feels nice, though. I like the movement; it's very, how do you say it, Michael Bay-ish in a good way.

The story, yeah, it's not great, BUT it's not ♥♥♥♥, IMHO. It tried something that was then retconned by RE7, but I like the 3 storylines that play the same story from different angles. It shouldn't have been separate; it should have been all together in one campaign, just switching between characters at some major events. It would have been very nice, even kinda GOATed. But why is it ♥♥♥♥ separated? Because when you play LEON -> CHRIS -> JAKE, then Jake's story is f1cking dogsh1t because all the plot points are spoiled and it's not interesting.

SO DO YOU WANT TO START PLAYING THIS CONTROVERSIAL GAME?

GO

1. JAKE
2. CHRIS
3. LEON

Leon last because, trust me, the story will be more satisfying.

This game has an identity crisis, it doesn´t know if it wants to be horror, edgy, action, cringe or serious. It mixes together all of this but doesn´t flesh out anything in particular. To start with the positives, Id´s say the control scheme and overall gameplay are genuinely so strong it´s crazy. Gameplay and combat is smooth, fast paced and fun, paired with the epic dodges  and rolling on the ground. Art direction is also a very strong point , just looks good. Shame it´s so dark, would be nice to see anything. The separated campaigns are an interesting approach, Sherry´s one is the best, definitely.
Her chemistry with Jake is easily the best part, paired with the story overall. Another shame is that the main villain, Nemesis from wish, is kinda undercooked, understandable with the campaign split. It would be much better if the entirety of RE6 would be just Sherry´s story. 
The positives are there, the game can be fun (definitely coop), but the negatives outweight the good stuff.
The inventory in this game makes sense with the gameplay, but it also robs the game of any kind of pause button. Skillpoint system is undercooked, bossfights are nothing to write home about (Leon´ms final boss), story is just a mess all around, lack of weapon upgrades, and the most important negative, you can trip on a zombie.
The biggest issue is the identitty crisis, and the worst part is the story. Leon´s story is generic "zombie action movie", you take Leon out and it falls flat. Chris´s is a mess, action all around without a breath to take, in a bad way, the story is the best part, it was very good and emotional, but the final bossfight took years off of my life. Sherry´s is the best, and Ada was supposed to tie it all together, which it did so partially. BUT, Ada had the best partner, THE legendary goat.
Overall, the cons slightly outweight the pros, but I had fun at the end. As I made clear, the most replayable part is Sherry.

the chris campaign was definitely a lot more action heavy, reminded me a lot of re5 where it just feels like a bunch of boss fights strung together. i think it was a pretty significant step up in gameplay from 5, and i appreciated the variety of different gameplay mechanics a lot, but it often fell short for me. most notably, i felt like i often lacked ammunition, despite ending each chapter with a high ranking + accuracy rating, so i know i wasn't wasting all that much. the final chapter was filled with some of the worst sections i have ever experienced. moving up the elevator and being shot from all angles / blasted with rockets was a miserable experience, only saved by the fact i enabled a skill that allowed my partner to heal me extra when i go down. followed by multiple boring chase sequences that were *just* strict enough on timing that i had to replay them once or twice before beating them, and the worst final boss fight i have ever experienced, this was a really bad way to end the campaign. also forgot to mention earlier, but chapter 1 just felt like i was committing hate crimes against the local asian community. big step down from leons campaign, i appreciate the added narrative context and how it interweaves with that storyline. also some of the enemies in this campaign were just ridiculous like they behave in a way that the games weapons were not really designed to deal with effectively imo

on to adas campaign now, a little confused as to who her partner will be because she's very much a lone wolf, but even more confused if its a solo campaign in what is largely up to this point a co-op experience

she has no partner! thank goodness. i dont know what the experience is like playing it multiplayer, but thats not my problem. adas campaign was an absolute joy, a shorter, spy themed adventure with a bit more of a focus on stealth in parts than the others. the bus train chainsaw boss fight is one of the worst things this game has to offer, but beyond that it was an absolute thrill, didn't overstay its welcome, and managed to introduce / elaborate on a few plot threads that i felt were pretty meaningful in tying up the game


this was a weird experience, 3/4 campaigns were entirely action, and it only felt like the game really found its stride somewhere in the middle of the final two. i think there is a lot this game does wrong, and i understand why it was so controversial. re7 was definitely a much needed course correction after this, but this wasn't as bad as people claim. the story is a mess, a few of the boss fights are basically just you running around until the game decides you are ready to actually fight, and there are a lot of strange QTE arrangements that probably didnt need to be included, but it is a pretty fun time the whole way through.
